description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Council continues to ensure that assets are maintained and renewed to meet community needs. Asset renewal and upgrade expenditure will fluctuate from year to year depending on the nature of the Capital Works Program and completion of these projects in the year planned. The 2024–25 actual result is below target due to capital projects carried forward to 2025–26.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
Council has been able to improve its underlying result to a small underlying surplus for 2024–25, however without the early payment of 50% of 2025–26 Victoria Grants Commission funds, would have had an underlying deficit.

The results outline that Council is reliant on capital grants and contributions to be able to continue delivering services at the current level.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation:
Council’s 2024–25 result was impacted by a 50% advance payment of the 2025–26 Victorian Grants Commission Allocation, paid in June 2025. 

Council has maintained stability in its ability to generate revenue from a range of sources to fund services and activities.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Property valuations are used to apportion the rate burden across each property and do not affect the total amount of rates levied across Council. For 2024–25 rating income was increased by the rate cap as set by the minister at 2.75%.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Expenditure increase for 2024–25 was in-line with budget and allowed Council to continue to deliver services to meet community needs.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Infrastructure per head has increased for 2024–25 as a result of both the level of capital works undertaken during the year as well as a change in accounting standard interpretation that increased valuations of existing infrastructure assets.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The municipality has maintained a relatively consistent ratio with the majority of the Council’s road network inhabited and serviced.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Council aims to generate income outside of rates and grants from sources such as rental income, fees and charges, property sales and developer contributions. This income is used to support population growth throughout the community, fund capital works and initiatives and to meet service delivery expectations of the community.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation:
The amount of grants received from the Australian and Victorian Governments fluctuate each year according to the funding programs offered.

Council's results were heavily influenced by the timing of the Victorian Local Government Grants Commission (VLGGC) payments with 50% of the 2025–26 VLGGC allocation was paid in advance and recognised as income in the 2024–25 financial year.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Council has maintained stability over the years against this ratio to support community outcomes where services are most needed.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation:
The historical staff turnover results were calculated including temporary staff (short-term contracts). The 2024–25 results have excluded temporary staff in line with the guidance. If they had been included the 2024–25 result would be 20.41% which is consistent with prior years.

Council continues to implement strategies to reduce the workforce turnover through staff engagement strategies, recruitment, and retention initiatives.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Bass Coast Shire has one aquatic facility. 5 health inspections were completed in the 2024/25 financial year.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The total number of visits to the aquatic facilities has increased marginally from 2023/24.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Visitation to the aquatic centre increased from 141,369 in 2023/24 to 146,550 in 2024/25 which has decreased the direct cost per visit to the facility from $3.98 in 2023/24 to $3.61 in 2024/25.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Council's reporting framework did not allow reporting on number of days between receipt of animal management requests and first response action this financial year. This will be investigated further and resolved for future years.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: In 2024/25, there were 269 animals collected and 136 were reclaimed by their owner. These figures are consistent with 2023/24 of which 274 animals were collected and 140 rehomed.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Of the 133 unclaimed animals collected, 89 were rehomed. These figures are consistent with 2023/24 of which 134 animals were unclaimed and 99 rehomed.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The overall direct cost of the animal management service decrease is attributed to improved operational practices and lower staffing costs compared to the previous year. Ongoing year round beach patrols continue to uplift patrol and service provided for community.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: All eight animal management prosecutions in 2024/25 were successful.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: In 2024/25 Council received and investigated 32 food complaints.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: A food safety assessment was completed on all food premises which required an annual food safety assessment in accordance with the Food Act 1984. All other food business that require an annual mandatory inspection were inspected in accordance with the Food Act.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: There was no change to the direct cost of the food safety service in 2024/25, however there was a decrease in the number of registered food premises.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Council received 11 non-compliance notifications regarding food premises and all 11 were followed up with an inspection.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Council obtained 93 food samples from food businesses for analysis which was above the required number of 89 food samples to be analysed.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: During 2024/25, 9 of 85 decisions were considered at closed meetings of Council. In comparison, 10 of 105 decisions were considered at closed meetings in 2023/24. Closed decisions include personnel and contractual matters.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Bass Coast Council community satisfaction rating for consultation and engagement has increased by three points from the previous year and is above other Large Rural Councils and State-wide averages.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: In 2024/25, there was a total sum of 100 Councillors attendances at 12 Council meetings. In comparison, in 2023/24 there were 101 Councillor attendances at 12 Council meetings.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: Council expenses have reduced in 2024/25, in line with 2022/23 results. This is due to no CEO recruitment occurring in 2024/25.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Bass Coast Council community satisfaction rating for making decisions in the interest of the community increased two points from the previous year, and is above other Large Rural Councils and State-wide averages.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: There were 55,761 items in the library collection including e-resources. The number of items purchased in the last 5 years has increased from 33,882 in 2023/24 to 34,977 in 2024/25.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The decrease in direct cost of the library service is due to contribution funds being redirected from operational expenditure to make capital purchases for the establishment of the Grantville library in 2024/25.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The number of physical library collection loans increased from 173,898 in 2023/24 to 180,544 in 2024/25.

description: Library membership
variation: In 2024/25 there were 20,418 members registered to the libraries located within the Bass Coast Shire.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: In 2024/25, there was a total of 244,151 visits to the libraries located within the Bass Coast Shire.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Council does not have operational control of a MCH service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Council does not have operational control of a MCH service.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Council does not have operational control of a MCH service.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Council does not have operational control of a MCH service.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Council does not have operational control of a MCH service.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Council maintains 621.61 kilometres of sealed local roads. We received a total of 66 sealed local road requests in 2024/25 compared to 71 requests in 2023/24.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: In 2024/25 there were 621.61 kilometres of sealed local roads with 583.45 kilometres below Councils intervention level. The variance to target is due to updated condition data from the revaluation of Road assets completed in the 2023/24 financial year. For 2023/24 there were 608.09 kilometres of sealed local roads with 566.87 kilometres below Councils intervention level.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Council's pavement reconstruction costs have decreased per square metre from $125.73 to $93.96 due to an increased proportion of rural road reconstruction from the previous year.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: In 2024/25, Council resealed 46,474.27 square metres of sealed local roads, compared to 58,179.80 square metres in 2023/24. There has been an increase in the cost per square metre from $13.25 in 2023/24 to $16.83 in 2024/25. The increase is primarily due to a higher proportion of urban roads being resealed in 2024/25, where the associated repair and preparation costs tend to be higher than those for rural road resealing.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: The Community Satisfaction score of 52 is a two point increase on last years result, 13 points higher than the average for Large Rural Councils and 7 points higher than the State-wide average. It is important to note that the survey results are also a reflection of the state controlled roads within the municipality.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The median number of days between receipt of a planning application and a decision on the application has decreased compared to the previous years figure. This can be attributed to Council placing a focus on improving system processes and operational practices.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The total number of planning applications remained high for 2024/25 and there was an increase of planning application decisions compared to the previous year. Councils focus on improving operational practices and timeframes for decision making is reflected in the improved outcomes for this indicator.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: There was an increase of planning applications for 2024/25 alongside a decrease in direct cost of the statutory planning service compared to the previous year.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation:
There were 17 VCAT decisions in relation to planning permits in 2024/25. VCAT upheld 10 of those decisions. many of these cases were highly technical or legal in nature, and some involved matters of state significance such as applications related to the Land Subject to Inundation Overlay (LSIO) in Silverleaves.
A large number of VCAT decisions were the result of constructive mediation sessions that resulted in mutually agreed terms between the parties involved. While these outcomes are captured as decisions overturned or varied by VCAT, they actually reflect collaborative agreements that led to acceptable planning applications.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation:
For every 10,000 kerbside bins in Bass Coast, the collection of 1.14 are missed. This is an improvement on the 2023/24 result of 2.7 bins missed per 10,000. 
All reported missed bins are collected the same or next day.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation:
Despite a slight increase in kerbside garbage bins collected, the 39.3% decrease in collection costs is attributed to improved operational practices and cost-saving measures. 
The 2.8% increase in cost of kerbside garbage collection is primarily due to inflationary pressures affecting third party kerbside collection costs.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: A 2.7% increase in kerbside recyclable bins collected alongside the 4% decrease in collection costs is attributed to improved operational practices and the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme which has positively impacted recycling rates.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Our Waste Diversion rate has been impacted by a 6% decrease in the volume of recyclables collected through kerbside bins. This is consistent with the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme which has generated a positive shift in recycling. An improvement has been made in the reporting of contamination rates in 2024/25 which has also impacted the total tonnage diverted.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The 9.0 per cent increase is due to a 33 per cent increase in depreciation and an increase in other expenses for a one-off settlement payment for transfer of two Cemetery trusts to a separate entity.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The 2.97 per cent increase in average rates per property assessment reflects a combination of the 2.75 per cent rate cap and the development of vacant land.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: This indicator has been impacted by an increase in cash balances and a decrease in the value of borrowings classed as current liabilities following the refinancing of a short-term loan to a longer term.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The decrease in this indicator in 2023/24 is due to funds held in term deposits with original maturities greater than 90 days of $36.6 million being classed as other financial assets instead of cash. In 2024/25 Council held a higher balance of funds as cash rather than investments. These cash balances are included as cash in Council’s forecast.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: During 2024/25 Council drew down on borrowings for construction of intergenerational projects, this will continue during the forward outlook.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: A short-term loan rollover ($8 million) has been accounted for as a repayment and redraw in each financial year, increasing to $12.57 million in 2023/24. In 2024/25 this loan was increased to $18.5 million and redrawn as two separate loans with extended terms.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The increase in this indicator in 2024/25 is due to refinancing of a short-term loan to a longer term. Forecasts from 2025/26 and onward are consistent with the 2024/25 result and are due to the planned draw down of borrowings which will fund the delivery of intergenerational projects.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: In 2024/25 Council’s depreciation expense has increased 33 per cent from the prior financial year and 24 per cent to budget which has impacted the target. This is a result of asset revaluation increases and capitalisation of new assets.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Council's underlying result has been impacted by increases in depreciation expense and other expenses, partly offset by the advance payment of $4.6 million in funding from the Victorian Grants Commission.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Council continues to have a high reliance on rate revenue consistent with other large rural Councils. The 2025 reduction is due to the early payment of the financial assistance grants.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: The increase experienced in 2024/25 is due to an overall 3.90 percent decrease in capital improved property values within the municipality and a 2.75 percent rates increase.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The 2024/25 increase of 7.72 per cent is attributable to increases in depreciation due to asset class revaluations and other expenses relating to payment for the transfer of two Cemetery trusts to a separate entity and a 1.94 per cent increase in population.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The value of Council’s infrastructure has increased in 2024/25 because of; fair value infrastructure revaluation adjustments ($46 million), capital works expenditure ($28 million) and developer contributed assets ($6 million).

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The increase in population of 1.94 per cent has resulted in higher population density per kilometre of road.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The result for 2024/25 is due to additional revenue associated with the operation of Berninneit through bookings and ticket sales and improved financial returns due to higher interest rates on investments.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Increase in recurrent grants in 2021/22 and 2022/23 is due to Victorian Grants Commission funding received in advance. This did not occur in the 2023/24 financial year. Payment for 2024/25 and early payment for 2025/26 were received in 2024/25 resulting in a large increase in this result from 2024 to 2025.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The socio-economic disadvantage index is prepared by the ABS. The result is based on a scale of one for most disadvantaged and ten for least disadvantaged.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Council’s staff turnover is consistent with the prior year and is on par with industry average.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council Officers completed all required inspections of Council owned aquatic facilities.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Total attendance at Council’s aquatic facilities for the 2024/25 financial year was 448,610. This figure represents a 14 percent increase in total patronage compared to 2023/24 (391,401 visits) and is attributed to the expanded facility being in operation for a full 12 months.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The increase in visits, combined with a small reduction in the cost of service resulted in a total cost of $3.80 per visit.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation:
In 2024/25, Council responded to 2,363 animal management requests, with an average response time of 1.9 days from receipt to action.

While the average response time increased slightly this year, all requests were actioned within two days. Council remains committed to prioritising higher-risk matters, including dog attacks and dogs wandering at large.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation:
The number of collected animals increased by 27 percent, from 478 last year to 609 this year, whilst the number of animals reclaimed reduced by 17 percent from 314 to 255. 

While Council continues to reunite pets with their owners, the increased number of collected animals may be a symptom of cost of living pressures on pet owners.

description: Animals rehomed
variation:
Council continues to actively work on the rehoming of unclaimed animals. Despite Council’s efforts, animals rehomed or reclaimed throughout the year was 60 percent of surrendered animals, which is significantly lower than previous years.

It should be noted that there are many reasons out of Council’s control that impact people’s willingness or ability to reclaim or rehome animals.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The total cost of the service increased by approximately $800,000 in 2024/25. This increase is primarily attributed to the rise in animals collected, triggering: higher staffing levels and associated overtime costs (including training of new staff and equipment), and increased veterinary expenses, including animal food and medication.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation:
There were seven animal management prosecutions this year (decreasing from 16 last year). All were successfully pursued by Council.  

Prosecutions are conducted under the Domestic Animals Act 1994 when appropriate and necessary. Council’s primary strategy remains focused on community safety, communication, education, and promotion of responsible pet ownership.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation:
Council responded to 33 food safety complaints in 2024/25. Some complaints received related to businesses that only operated on the weekend, and this caused Council's response times to increase. 

Despite the increase, Council still responded to complaints in under 1.5 business days.

description: Food safety assessments
variation:
Council aims to complete all required annual food safety assessments for registered Class 1 and 2 food premises. 

Council completed 373 assessments this year, representing almost 98 percent of the required assessments. Assessments not completed were largely due to some premises only opening for short periods during the year due to seasonality of their trade.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The cost of service per premises increased to $298. Data cleansing to remove inactive food businesses resulted in a lower number (1,110) of registered businesses in 2024/25, compared to the 1,170 last year.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: During the year, there were a total of 21 critical and major non-compliance notifications, all of which were followed up by Council.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Council collected 91 samples throughout the year, five more than the required 86.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council decisions made during meetings closed to the public were from tender recommendations, acquisitions and other decisions defined as confidential under the Local Government Act 2020. 10 decisions were made in these circumstances, while 168 decisions were made during open Council Meetings.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Satisfaction with consultation and engagement decreased by five points to 42 compared to the previous year. The average result for similar sized Councils was 48.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost of elected representation includes Councillor allowances, the cost of travel to meetings to advocate to State and Federal Governments, the cost of mandatory Councillor induction, the cost of training, professional development, catering, and IT expenses. After the election period, the cost returned to be consistent with previous years.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Satisfaction with Council making decisions in the interest of the community decreased by three points to 41 compared to the previous year. The average result for similar sized Councils was 46.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation:
There are 55,175 items in the library collection (including e-resources) with 38,687 of those items being purchased in the last five years. 

The proportion of the library collection that is less than five years old is consistent with previous years.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The cost of delivering the library service ($28.34 per head of the population) is consistent with previous years.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: There were over 198,000 loans made during the year, which equates to 3.20 loans per head of population. The total number of loans is consistent with last year's result.

description: Library membership
variation: There were 19,466 registered library members at the end of the 2024/25 year. This number equates to 31 percent of the municipal population and is consistent with last year’s result.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: There were just over 146,000 visits to Council libraries during the 2024/25 year, equating to over two visits per head of population. This is consistent with last year’s result.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation:
All birth notifications were actioned, and all newborns were enrolled in Council’s Maternal and Child Health Service. In 2024/25, Council received 731 birth notifications and recorded 737 infant enrolments.

Enrolment exceeds 100 percent due to infants being born out of the municipality, moving into the shire and receiving the service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation:
The total cost of service did not increase significantly, however the number of hours delivered decreased. Resulting in a higher cost per hour of service.

More than 15,500 hours of Maternal and Child Health Services were provided during the year.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: The overall participation rate for the Maternal and Child Health Service was almost 68 percent, which is consistent with the previous financial year's result.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation in the Maternal and Child Health Service by Aboriginal children was almost 72 percent, which is consistent with the previous financial year's result.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: The service received 731 birth notices during the year and conducted 678 four-week Key Age and Stage assessments.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation:
Council received 584 requests for sealed local roads in the 2024/25 year. This equates to 50 requests per 100km of sealed local road. This is a significant decrease from 23/24 (97 requests) and is attributed to less inclement weather conditions throughout the year resulting in less potholes and damage. 

Council’s proactive inspection program is also detecting and fixing more defects before they get reported.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation:
Council continued to maintain a high proportion of its road network to the condition standards. 

Due to the large extent of the road network, condition standards are assessed every four years. Therefore, the length of roads above the intervention level is relatively consistent over a four-year period.

The next assessment is scheduled for 2025/26 for consideration and use by Council in 2026/27 onwards.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation:
In 2024/25, Council completed a reconstruction project in Longwarry in addition to other pavement renewal works. 

To achieve a better outcome for the community, Council increased the scope of reconstruction, which in turn led to a higher per metre cost for the year.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Council entered into a new contract in 2024/25 and was able to secure a lower contract rate, resulting in a 16 percent reduction in cost for the year.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads increased by one point to 36 compared to the previous year. The average result for similar sized Councils was 39.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The time taken to decide planning applications decreased to 111 days in 2024/25. This is a positive result, decreasing from 132 days two years ago. Council's sustained focus on reducing the backlog of historical applications with long processing times has led to this improvement.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The strong gains achieved in the previous year for the percentage of planning applications decided within required timeframes continued this year. These positive results are attributed to process improvements, a focus on reducing the backlog of historic applications, and workforce stability.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The total cost of service reduced by approximately 18 percent ($300k) for 2024/25. Maintaining a fully staffed team over the year, eliminating the need to engage consultants contributed to the cost reduction.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation:
16 of the 19 decisions were upheld at VCAT for 2024/25, resulting in a success rate of 84 percent.

Improved decision-making processes have contributed to this result and fewer matters being appealed at VCAT.

This is Council’s best result in VCAT outcomes since the 2017/18 year.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation:
This result translates to two bins being missed every 10,000 scheduled pickups.

During the year, Council picked up and emptied over two million bins, missing only 479. 

The slightly higher number of missed bins this year is attributed to the change in schedule associated with the introduction of the glass collection service. In addition, large storm events in August 2024 delayed emptying of some bins. Although these were emptied as soon as safely possible, they are still counted as missed bins.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost of kerbside garbage collection remains consistent with the last two years at $156 per bin. Service cost remained steady after increasing three years ago due to the landfill levy, labour, fuel, population growth, and transportation costs.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The cost of the recycling bin service increased to $74 per bin in 2024/25. Council entered into two new contracts in 2024/25 which included increased gate fees and collection rates.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation:
51 percent of waste was recycled and diverted from landfill in 2024/25. This is slightly lower than previous years. The introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme has impacted this result as community members are recycling eligible containers through this scheme, and as such, those containers are not being recycled through Council bins.

For the first time in ten years, the total weight of collected garbage, recyclables, and green organics decreased on the previous year.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
Current year actuals consist of $8.5 million natural disaster rectification cost (most of which was funded from the state government grants) which was not factored in the council targets. Excluding the material costs pertaining to emergency events rectification expenses for property assessment is $3,955. 

Additionally, $3.4m in asset write-offs were incurred as part of Council’s asset renewal program. 

Depreciation expenditure, a non-cash item, increased significantly due to revaluation increases in Buildings in 2023/24 and Infrastructure assets in 2024/25, which included the addition of disruption costs in accordance with AASB 13 amendments.  The increase in Depreciation expenditure has not been factored into the 2025/26 Adopted Budget forecasts.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Slight increase in line with the expected rate increase within the rate cap and the forecast growth within the Shire.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Term deposits were all classified as current in the target however $6M is classified as non-current in the 2024/25 actual result due to contractual terms. A ratio greater than 100% indicates Council is able to meet its current obligations as and when they become due.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The reclassification of interest-bearing loans to Non-Current Liabilities led to an $11.2 million reduction in Current Liabilities compared to target. This ratio excludes $35.6 million in short term investments that are sufficient to cover reserves and other restricted items.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: This indicator measures total interest-bearing liabilities compared to rate revenue and reflects borrowings undertaken in 2023/24 of $6.49 million.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: During 2024/25, existing loans were refinanced and not physically paid out.  In 2025/26, $3.3 million in the Local Government Funding Vehicle loan is expected to be repaid.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Increase in non-current liabilities in 2024/25 reflects the reclassification of existing loans from current to non-current. The ratio is forecast to remain within the 32 – 40 percent range in future years and well below the 60 percent prudential limit.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The 24.2% decline in the ratio is primarily due to increased depreciation due to Buildings revaluation in 2023/24 and Infrastructure revaluation increase in 2024/25.Capital expenditure for 2024/25 was lower than target due to the strategic rephasing and re-planning of key projects including the Baw Baw Civic Precinct, the Rollo Street Yarragon Masterplan, the Black Spot Program and Sealing Gravel Roads Program. The ratio is expected to increase and surpass 2023/24 levels in 2025/26 supported by the planned completion of these multi-year projects.  The forecast for 2026/27 and beyond is a decrease in line with the Long-Term Infrastructure Plan.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit) decreased by 43.3 percent% to -11.06 percent%. This result was impacted by the early receipt of the The 2026 Financial Assistance Grant was prepaid in the 2024/25 financial year, resulting incontributing to a favourable variance of $6.7m . Ccompared to the previous prior year. However, the negative outcome is largely driven by increased depreciation following asset revaluations, higher materials and services costs related to the August 2024 storm event, asset write-offs under the asset renewal program, and rising software licensing expenses as Council transitions its core systems to a cloud-based SaaS model.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The early receipt of the Financial Assistance Grant temporarily increased underlying revenue, resulting in a lower ratio. This is expected to normalise in future years, assuming the timing of recurrent grants remains consistent.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: This result is consistent with last year.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: This indicator measures Council’s total expenditure over the municipal population and is within the expected range despite Council having significant natural disaster expenditure and higher depreciation from asset revaluations.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Council's infrastructure asset base increased due to a combination of higher unit rates and changes to accounting standards (AASB 13), which impacted how assets are valued.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Council manages a local road network of 1,850 km resulting in a ratio of 33 people for each kilometre of local road. This is consistent with previous years.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: This measures Council’s own sourced revenue and is consistent with the previous year.  Slight increase is mainly related to higher interest revenue.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: This year, 50 percent of the Financial Assistance Grant (a main component of recurrent grants) for 2025/26 was received in advance. Additionally, the advance payment of 75 percent for 2024/25 was received in July 2025 (historically this has been paid in Q4 of the prior year). This led to an increase of funds per head of municipal population.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) prepares an index of relative socio-economic disadvantage. The result for the municipality is six, where one is the most, and ten is the least disadvantaged.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Council’s turnover has continued to decline from a high of 41.9 percent three years ago (due to an exit of service) to 13.5 percent this year. This is the third consecutive year that turnover has decreased.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council owns two aquatic facilities in Bayside: Sandringham Leisure Centre and Middle Brighton Sea Baths which are managed under private commercial lease and agreements. Council is not responsible for the operation or management of the Sandringham Aquatic Centre and the Middle Brighton Sea Bath is not technically defined as an aquatic facility.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Council owns two aquatic facilities in Bayside: Sandringham Leisure Centre and Middle Brighton Sea Baths which are managed under private commercial lease and agreements. Council is not responsible for the operation or management of the Sandringham Aquatic Centre and the Middle Brighton Sea Bath is not technically defined as an aquatic facility.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Council owns two aquatic facilities in Bayside: Sandringham Leisure Centre and Middle Brighton Sea Baths which are managed under private commercial lease and agreements. Council is not responsible for the operation or management of the Sandringham Aquatic Centre and the Middle Brighton Sea Bath is not technically defined as an aquatic facility.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: The increase in processing time is partly attributed to a higher volume of requests, as all animal administration requests are now included in the total count of animal requests actioned. Processing times remained strong throughout most of the year; however, performance declined in the final quarter due to an unexpected vacancy, which reduced the team’s capacity to maintain previous processing rates.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: In 2024/25 50% of animals collected are reclaimed which while reducing remains, higher than the historic average of similar Councils.  In Bayside there is a high portion of animals being registered, combined with Council’s efforts to reunite collected animals before impounding and our community’s care for their pets.  There is a sector wide issue with an increasing number of animals being surrendered.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: With the sector wide increase in animals being surrendered, there has been an increase in the number of animals Council has been able to rehome with new households and families.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Continued focus on efficiency of service means the cost remains stable and within budget. The cost is similar to previous years and close to average for similar metropolitan councils.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: There was an increase in prosecutions with a rise in dog attacks in the municipality. Council is committed ensuring responsible pet ownership is reinforced though  education programs, communications and officer patrols along with taking enforcement action consistent with the Domestic Animals Act 1994. Serious matters warranting prosecution are pursued accordingly for both specific and general deterrence. The 100% success rate reflects the high quality of officers’ investigations and Council’s willingness to act.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Time take to action food complaints remains relatively low. The slight increase in average time taken is attributable to an increase in the number of complaints.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: All mandatory food safety assessments were conducted in the calendar year 2024. (93 of 93 high-risk businesses). All other class 1 and 2 registered food businesses were also inspected in 2024 (462 additional inspections).

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: A number of temporary pop up and event businesses have closed resulting a changed ratio of cost per businesses. Costs remain well within budget, and consistent with the average in prior years for similar metropolitan councils.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Council maintains 100% resolution of non-compliance notifications. Major and critical non compliances continue to be a high priority. Environmental Health Officers ensure that all major and critical issues identified during the assessment period are followed up as a priority  within the required timeframes to mitigate the risks to community.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Council collected all food samples (135) as required under the Government Gazette. This includes 38 food samples collected from class 1 food premises and 102 food samples collected class 2 and other premises. The additional food samples related to specific food safety matters that required investigation.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: The number of decisions made at meetings closed to the public remains below our target of 5% (12 of 277). The percentage of 4.33% is a slight increase on the previous year, due to more than half the items relating to recruitment and appointment of the CEO.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The satisfaction rating is sourced from the annual satisfaction survey conducted by an independent research company. Council uses diverse methods to communicate and engage with the community, had had widespread engagement with the community and a deliberative community panel related to the development of strategic plans for the next 4-10 years . The community panel had 92% satisfaction with the process over four months. This is may have contributed to the  increase in satisfaction.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Bayside continues to demonstrate a good attendance of Councillors at Council meetings well within the expected range. The new Council elected in October 2024 has continued to demonstrate attendance consistent with previous years, despite an increase in Councillors with full-time workloads and young families.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost of elected representation has increased and relates to statutory increase in Councillor allowances as awarded by the Victorian Independent Remuneration Tribunal. Following Council elections in October 2024 there is an increase in mandatory training over the first 6 months, plus increase in associated internet and phone costs.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The satisfaction rating is sourced from the annual satisfaction survey conducted by an independent research company. The result shows a minor increase in satisfaction, but is affected by reduced satisfaction reported in suburbs in the east of the municipality impacted by SRL and Victorian Government planning decisions.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: A strong result, which reflects libraries' removal of obsolete material and showcasing new titles as well as the ongoing inclusion of e-resources in the calculation reflecting the full collection available.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The service cost has dropped slightly but still includes capital purchases of library materials, which better reflects the true cost of providing a contemporary collection to the community. Other operational costs are consistent with previous years and remain well within the standard and are similar to other metropolitan councils.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: A strong result, consistent with previous year reflects libraries removal of obsolete material and showcasing new titles and ongoing inclusion of e-resources in the calculation.

description: Library membership
variation: Registered members include those whose membership has been updated or used to access library collections, programs or technology services at any time in the past 36 months. Bayside registered 6,461 new members in 2024/25 and has a strong active membership across 4 branches and the book lockers facilitating out of hours borrowing and a solid online membership.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Despite a temporary forced closure of one branch due to flooding, the number of visits remains strong. This is reflected in both higher borrowing figures and higher program attendance.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: The high level of infants enrolled in the MCH service reflects a continuing high level of community satisfaction with the service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Council is committed to delivering cost-efficient MCH services. The cost of MCH services has increased 5.5% from 2023/24 due to reactive maintenance at two centres.  MCH services were still delivered within its budget.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation in the MCH service remains consistently high at 85.6% reflecting a high level of community satisfaction with the service.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation rates remain strong across all age cohorts with an increase of 9.52% to 95.92% from 86.4% in 2023/24.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Defined as the percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service who participated In the 4 week Key Age and Stage visit. In 2024/25 the enrolment percentage is strong showing greater than 100% as families move into the Bayside LGA after the birth of their child in another LGA or out of the state.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Complaints have continued to decline and are now consistent with the previous metropolitan council average. However, ongoing development means that at least 50% of complaints relate to state-managed roads and poor reconstruction by other service authorities. Work is under way to review and improve how this is managed in conjunction with relevant agencies.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Council's pavement management system and Geographic Information System (GIS) provide timely data capture on road condition, enabling an accurate response so that Council can achieve the 97% target - some planned work is on hold prior finalisation of works by other service authorities.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The works delayed to 2025 have commenced but are not complete and will be reported in 2025/26 year once finalised.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: A similar length of roads were resealed to prior years, but overall cost of works and supplies have increased. Council cost of resealing is still just below similar metropolitan council average in prior years.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Council’s day-to-day local road management is the same as previous years. Roads are maintained to agreed standards. However, the ongoing issues affecting state-managed roads contribute to resident dissatisfaction with sealed roads overall. Council continues to consult with state authorities to address any significant issues.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The median processing time is based on the total calendar days it takes to assess an application. The improved times reflects our continued focus on processing efficiency within our statutory planning service. This is a strong result substantially lower than the inner middle urban average of 71 days.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: In early 2025 the Victorian Government introduced some of the most significant changes to the planning scheme in more than 30 years. This required resources to plan for and manage the ongoing implications, and it affected Council’s capacity to provide its usual services to the same standard. However, we continue to maintain processing efficiency within our statutory planning service and remain above the target of 75%, and better than the metropolitan average.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: A slight increase in the cost of processing statutory planning relates to increased costs. However, this is consistent with the cost of the planning services for similar metropolitan councils.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: There is a slight improvement on the previous year with fewer decisions rejected at VCAT, which upheld 15 of  27 decisions taken to appeal. This reflects the contentious and complex nature of some of the decisions.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: The new contractor is well established and working with Council to focus on reducing the number of missed bins. This has resulted in return to historic low levels and well below the metropolitan council average.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Cost of delivering service is stabilised, and the slight increase is as anticipated based on industry prices which continue to rise. This is within budget and below the average for similar metropolitan councils in prior years.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: Cost of the recycling service is very similar to the previous year and within budget. This is also below the average for similar metropolitan councils in prior years.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Council continues to sustain a high level (69.33%) of waste diverted from landfill, which is close to our target (71.1%) and  well above the metropolitan average of 53.5%.  The container deposit scheme has contributed to a reduction in co-mingled recycling in kerbside collection affecting the overall percentage ratio.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Council is committed to providing high quality services to the Bayside community in the most efficient way possible. Ongoing savings have continued to be found to keep expenses per head of municipal population low and measure is within the expected range.  The increase in expenses per head in 2024/25 is driven by a number of factors including the expansion of Aged Care including Glen Eira and Council's fully funded Home Care Package Aged Care service, an increase in recurrent service costs funded from additional revenue, and increased depreciation due to Council's aggressive capital replacement program.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The general rates and the municipal charge for 2024/25 increased by 2.75%, in accordance with the Fair Go Rates System.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Council's result is an indicator of a strong financial position. Higher cash levels at the end of 2024/25 due to lower capital spend and higher capital works carried forward is contributing to a higher ratio compared to last year.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: A higher overall cash level at the end of 2024/25 due to carry forward of capital works projects is contributing to a higher unrestricted cash level.  Unrestricted cash is expected to decrease slowly over future years as Council funds it's aggressive capital program.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council continues to have no requirement for borrowing at this time.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council continues to have no requirement for borrowing at this time.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Own source revenue has remained steady in 2024/25.  The slight decrease in non-current liabilities (employee provisions) has reduced the ratio of non current liabilities compared to own source revenue slightly.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Council maintains a level of renewal and upgrade expenditure which is sufficient to ensure that its assets are renewed in accordance with condition assessment and service needs. The results have reduced in 2024/25 due to a lower capital spend and carry forwards into 2025/26.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The underlying result has remained constant in 2024/25.  The result will decrease slightly in future years as Bayside delivers an ambitious capital program funded from cash reserves, coupled with an increase in depreciation.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: There has been an increase in other sources of revenue, particularly user fees and interest income, and thus Council was slightly less reliant on rate revenue.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: General rate revenue increased in accordance with the rate capping framework (2.75% for 2024/25) and this combined with a slight increase in property values has kept this ratio steady for 2024/25.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Council is committed to providing high quality services to the Bayside community in the most efficient way possible. The increase in expenses per head in 2024/25 is driven by a number of factors including the expansion of Council's fully funded Aged Care service, an increase in recurrent service costs funded from additional revenue, and increased depreciation due to Council's aggressive capital replacement program.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Bayside has outsourced all of its maintenance works and therefore holds very low levels of plant and equipment.  The value of Council's building assets is relatively low  given it is old and in need of an upgrade. Council has doubled the level of capital spend over the last five years to address this issue. This is reflected in the gradual increase in the ratio over time.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The population density of Bayside is comparable to other metropolitan councils and reflects the relatively low density nature of Bayside suburbs.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Both own source revenue and population has remained steady in 2024/25.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Recurrent grants continue at a higher rate than previous years, including the expansion of Home Care packages resulting in $11.4 million in recurrent grants from the federal government (2023/24: $8.1million).

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The rating indicates that overall, Bayside has a lower number of disadvantage when compared to most other local government areas in Victoria, and to metropolitan Melbourne. However, we acknowledge that Bayside does have scattered pockets of neighbourhoods experiencing significant hardship and disadvantage, which is masked by the affluence across the municipality in general.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Staff turnover has reduced slightly - still consistent with previous years. The number of permanent employees has increased due to the Aged Care expansion and transfer of Glen Eira Council’s aged care service to Bayside. Significant work has been done to understand and improve our organisational culture which is helping to position Bayside as an Employer of Choice, to attract and retain talent and may contribute to keeping staff turnover at a rate lower than the metropolitan average for similar councils.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: This measure has increased due to the significant expansion of Swimming Lesson program to 7 days a week, resulting in increased utilisation.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: This measure has decreased due to the increased utilisation and visitation resulting in more membership income.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
Animals reclaimed
variation: This measure has increased due to less animals being reclaimed by owners because of cost of living and dog attacks where animal owner's surrender the animals.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Council does not operate a rehoming service. This is undertaken by our contract pound service provider.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: This measure has increased due to the cost of Council’s pound contract.

description: Animal management prosecutions
Time taken to action food complaints
Food safety assessments
variation: This measure has increased due to intensified performance expectations to accelerate deliverables.

Food safety samples

variation: This measure has increased due to the appointment of the Chief Executive Officer. A higher number of reports were presented on the recruitment process and appointment of the CEO and were listed as confidential business items pursuant to s3(1) and s66(2)(a) of the Local Government Act 2020.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: This measure has increased due to positive publicity regarding key Council projects.

Cost of elected representation

Recently purchased library collection
variation: This measure has increased due to the updated data report regarding the libraries digital collection. This change aligns with Public Library Victoria discussions and for consistency across the High Country Library Network.

variation: No material variation.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: This measure has increased due to the updated data report regarding the libraries digital collection. This change aligns with Public Library Victoria discussions and for consistency across the High Country Library Network.

Library visits per head of population
Infant enrolments in the MCH service
Cost of the MCH service

Sealed local road requests
variation: The results for this measure have decreased due to a higher emphasis on sealed road maintenance and the recent drier climate is also a contributor.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The results for this measure have decreased due to a more competitive market associated with a decrease in civil projects across the state.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The results for this measure have decreased due to a competitive market associated with a decrease in civil projects across the state.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: The results for this measure have increased due to positive publicity regarding sealed road infrastructure projects from both a renewal and maintenance perspective.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: This measure has decreased due to additional resources received to undertake planning applications through funding from the Regional Planning Hub.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
Cost of statutory planning service
variation: This measure has decreased due to a reduction in staff costs during the period.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: This measure has increased due to new kerbside collection contractor and obtaining familiarity with the network.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: This measure has increased due to change of recyclable processing being undertaken locally, to now being undertaken in central Victoria which attracts a higher transport and handling cost.
