Expenses per property assessment
Average rate per property assessment
Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Material variation to budget due to current assets being $15.366 million more than budget, primarily due to unexpended capital project grant funding and early receipt of operating grant funding.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
Council considers current definition of “unrestricted cash” misleading as it excludes investments with a maturity date of 90 days. At 30 June 2025, $25.9 million of uncommitted cash as held to fund operations, projects and capital works.

Careful and timely treasury management used to maximise investment returns and to ensure cash availability.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Debt reduced by $585,000 in 2024/25. New borrowings included in 2025/26 Budget to part fund capital works program.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Material variation to budget due to asset renewal expenditure being $3.527 million less than budget.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Result significantly improved from 2023/24 due to timing of Financial Assistance Grants funding - 2023/24 allocation prepaid in 2022/23 and 2025/26 allocation part prepaid in 2024/25.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Adjusted underlying revenue impacted by timing of Financial Assistance Grants funding - 2023/24 allocation prepaid in 2022/23 and 2025/26 allocation part prepaid in 2024/25.

description: Rates compared to property values
Expenses per head of municipal population
Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Increased result primarily due to a $58.3 million increase in value of infrastructure assets.

description: Population density per length of road
Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Result significantly increased due to timing of Financial Assistance Grants funding - 2023/24 allocation prepaid in 2022/23 and 2025/26 allocation part prepaid in 2024/25.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
Percentage of staff turnover
Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council's 3 annual mandatory inspections for Council-run aquatic facilities have been completed within the reporting period. Inspections have reduced from the previous reporting period due to fluctuations in requirements for officer attendance outside of annual inspections.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Attendances are tracking well, supported by strong membership numbers. Boroondara Leisure and Aquatic Facilities (BLAF) attendances have increased from 2023-24.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The result indicates strong financial performance as membership numbers and visitations continue to increase year-on-year following the COVID-19 pandemic.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Animal Management team response time is running efficiently with officers now having more time to investigate during core business hours as after-hours animal collections are now managed by Lost Dogs Home as a contracted partner.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation:
Impounds of cats have increased due to the animal team conducting operations on known cat colonies, which adds to overall impounds and reduces reclaim rates. Work at known cat colonies is ongoing to resolve the issues permanently. 
The total number of animals reclaimed during 2024-25 was 126.

description: Animals rehomed
variation:
The adoption of cats has increased this financial year by 22.47% which is due to increased impoundment from actions taken at known cat colonies. This is proactive work undertaken by the Animal Management team to resolve long-term issues.
The total number of animals rehomed during 2024-25 was 173.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: A new, approved calculation has been applied to this measure and reconciled during 2024-25. Operational changes have resulted in a standalone animal service, with after-hours collections outsourced to the Lost Dogs Home, reducing reliance on other teams and lowering service costs.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: The Prosecutions team achieved a 100% success rate, prosecuting 10 cases during 2024-25. This is consistent with previous year’s results.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The 2024 year result demonstrates a clear commitment by the Health Services team to action food complaints in a timely manner and is consistent with the previous year's performance.

description: Food safety assessments
variation:
All Class 1 and 2 premises received a food safety assessment during the reporting period. This is in line with previous year's results and regulatory obligations defined within the Food Act 1984.
The total number of Class 1 and 2 premises that received a food safety assessment during  2024 was 1,061.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Accounting adjustments have been applied to this reporting periods calculations that provide a more accurate year-on-year direct cost. The result is consistent with similar benchmarked Councils.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation:
Council officers have attended to all critical and major non-compliance outcomes in a timely manner. This demonstrates Health Services commitment to maintaining food safety standards and supporting business owners within the municipality. 
The total number of critical and non-compliance notifications during 2024 was 247.

description: Food safety samples
variation: The annual target has been met. This is a statutory target provided by the Department of Health (VIC) every calendar year. The result of 256 exceeded the target of 247 samples, providing additional health detection services to the community.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: The number of confidential Council meetings has decreased compared to previous years. This reduction is primarily due to fewer matters requiring discussion of legal advice, which had previously necessitated closed sessions.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation:
The community consultation and engagement index score is down 1 point from 2024. While this change is not significant, it highlights the ongoing difficulty in shifting perceptions despite increased investment and effort in this space. Protective factors contributing towards the score in 2025, included: 
 	high levels of awareness and participation in the Boroondara Community Plan consultation where more than 5,400 community members were consulted - the highest in the state
	increasing levels of awareness of consultations more generally, based on an increased number of consultations as well as wider-reaching awareness campaigns to support each consultation
	a focus on plain language in consultation materials
	a focus on closing the loop after each consultation with the broader community, not just consultation participants
	awareness of Council’s efforts to inform the community about Planning Reforms and Council’s lobbying efforts.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance as measured remains strong and consistent with previous year’s results.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: Additional costs were incurred in this financial year following a general election where there were 7 new councillors elected (out of 11). Further, the Victorian Government introduced a regime of compulsory training in statute. As a result, training costs for councillors increased by over 20 per cent in this financial year.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Decisions made in the interest of the community remains one of Council’s lowest-rated measures. This is a slight but not statistically significant 3 point decline since 2024 and the lowest score recorded in the past decade. The decline was most pronounced among men (54, down 6 points), 18 to 34 year-olds (57, down 9 points), and residents in the North ward (56, down 6 points).

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation:
The proportion of items less than 5 years of age remained stable over the last 4 years, while the 4 year Council average of 64.68% aligns with modern library standards.
The total number of library collection items purchased in the last 5 years was 366,014.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The cost of library services per population increased this year by 1.61%. This can be attributed to higher online subscription costs for electronic books, magazines and newspapers.

description: Loans per head of population
variation:
The total number of library item loans in 2024–25 exceeded 2 million, marking the highest level since 2018–19 (pre-COVID-19), reflecting strong community engagement with library collection.
The total number of library collection item loans during the 2024-25 was 2,081,044.

description: Library membership
variation:
Over 43% of library members have borrowed or reserved an item (physical or digital) in the last 36 months. This is an increase of more than 9% on the 2023-24 result.
The total number of library registered members during 2024-25 was 78,175.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Library visitation continues to grow in 2024–25, reaching its highest level since 2018–19 (pre COVID-19) with 972,360 visits, an increase of over 50,000 from the previous year.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation:
Infant enrolment is greater than birth notifications received due to enrolments of infants born outside of Victoria.
The total number of infants enrolled in the Maternal and Child Health service during 2024-25 was 1,128.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The actual cost per hour of MCH service is within the expected range and remains consistent with the previous year’s result.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation:
Participation in the MCH service has met the annual target, with the result in line with previous years. 
The total number of children who attended the MCH service at least once during 2024-25 was 4,025.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation:
Participation by Aboriginal children in the MCH Service is just below the annual participation target as not all children are eligible for a key age and stage visit in this financial year.
 The total number of Aboriginal children who attended the MCH service at least once dring 2024-25 was 26.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation:
Infant attendance for the 4-week Key Age and Stage visit exceeds 100% due to the inclusion of infants born outside of Victoria, for whom no birth notification has been received.
The total number of infants who participated in the 4-week Key Age and Stage visits during 2024-25 was 1,133.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The number of sealed local road requests from the community is variable and this value has declined. Possible factors include rainfall, maintenance and renewal delivery. The variation may be due to confusion between state-managed and Council-managed roads, which can influence how and where community members direct their requests.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The successful achievement of the target is due to the ongoing delivery of the road reseal and rehabilitation program.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The cost of sealing local road reconstruction is lower than the previous year’s result. This decrease is primarily due to the completion of several larger scale projects in the fourth quarter, which tend to be more cost efficient per square metre.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Higher resurfacing costs in 2024-25 compared to previous years, was due to contract CPI increases and carrying out more smaller scale projects, which attracts a small quantity surcharge.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Although satisfaction with the condition of sealed local roads declined this financial year. the result is still significantly better than the Metropolitan average of 59.  Reasons influencing this outcome include higher rainfall and more intense heatwaves, resulting in more potholes, pavement deterioration and more roadworks.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The median days to make a decision was 83 days, which is an improvement of 13 days (13.54%) compared to the previous financial year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The result indicates that 67.68% of applications were determined within the 60 and 10 day statutory timeframes, which is an improvement of 1.18% over the previous financial year.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost of service per application is $3,869.26. This represents a marginal increase of 1.18% compared to the previous financial year’s result of $3,824.17.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The result of 44.44% is within the target range yet 3.39 percentage points less than the previous financial year. Over the year, a number of matters were resolved by mediation and did not proceed to appeal. The introduction of ‘Deemed to comply' provisions has reduced the ability for officers to raise grounds with problematic applications, however, the impact of this has tapered down over the year.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Improvements within the internal Waste team have resulted from additional training, a customer-centric focus, and a boost in contractor performance, leading to a decrease in missed bins for the year.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The kerbside garbage collection cost is higher than the previous year, due to increases in landfill levy costs for general waste sent to landfill.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The kerbside recycling collection cost is lower than the previous year, due to a reduction in fuel prices.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme in November 2023, which diverts higher-density glass material away from the kerbside recycling stream, has resulted in a reduction in the annual result compared to 2023-24. Although lower than the previous year, it remains above target.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Council continues to deliver services in accordance with its Long Term Financial Plan.  There has been an increase in the overall expenditure primarily due to an increase in the net loss on the disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment compared to 2023-24. There has also been an increase employee costs, in line with the rate cap increase, and increases in materials and services and depreciation. Future forecasts are expected to remain steady.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Council's budgeted rate increase in 2024-25 was capped at 2.75%.  The rate cap is set by the Victorian Government's Fair Go Rates System (FGRS) and Council's forecast rate increases have been aligned to the forecast FGRS. The forecast rate increase for 2025-26 has been set at 3.00%.  Council is delivering services in accordance with its Long Term Financial Plan.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The indicator was lower in 2020-21 due to reduced cash reserves, increased unearned income from additional capital grants and higher trust funds and deposits. During 2022-23, current assets declined due to unspent conditional grants unspent and lower cash reserves, while current liabilities decreased with reductions in unearned income and payables. In 2023-24, current assets increased primarily due to Council holding $167.26 million in other financial assets at 30 June 2024. This increase was driven by higher interest rate returns, the sale of Serpells Land ($16 million) and the divestment of the Boroondara Tennis Centre ($30 million).  In 2024-25, current assets increased slightly due to increases in rates and parking debtors partially offset by impairment losses for infringements and reduction in other debtors. Current liabilities reduced by $9.60 million which was largely driven by the reduction in trade and other payables. Working capital remains well above the minimum sustainable level.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The unrestricted cash is lower primarily due to a reduction of cash reserves.  In 2021-22, Council's own source revenue streams decreased as a result of the impacts of COVID-19 with recovery back to pre-pandemic levels in 2022-23. At 30 June 2024, Council held $167.26 million in investments with a maturity date greater than 3 months. During 2024-25, these term deposits were redeemed and transferred to cash and cash equivalents, which is now unrestricted cash. Current liabilities reduced by $9.60 million which was largely driven by the reduction in trade and other payables. In the long term, unrestricted cash remains within a sustainable level.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: In 2012-13, Council continued with substantial investment in major building redevelopment works and borrowed $29 million.  The borrowings were at a fixed interest rate for 10 years and were due to be repaid in full in 2022-23. Council reviewed its existing loan portfolio in 2022-23 and refinanced the remainder of this loan for a further 10 years. Council also took out borrowings of $70 million in 2021-22 to fund significant infrastructure projects. No new borrowings were taken out in 2023-24 and the decrease is due to lower principal repayments as Council continues to reduce existing debt. In 2024-25 loans and borrowings continue to be repaid and reducing.  In 2025-26, Council is proposing to take out borrowings of $9.50 million to fund the purchase of 17 side loader waster trucks. All existing borrowings are projected to be repaid by 2033. Rates revenue continue to increase in line with the legislated rate cap.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: In 2012-13, Council continued with substantial investment in major building redevelopment works and borrowed $29 million. The borrowings were at a fixed interest rate for 10 years and were due to be repaid in full in 2022-23. Council reviewed its existing loan portfolio in 2022-23 and refinanced the remainder of this loan for a further 10 years. Council took out borrowings of $70 million in 2021-22 to fund significant infrastructure projects.  The decreases in 2023-24 and 2024-25 are due to the principal repayments as Council continues to reduce existing debt.  In 2025-26, Council is proposing to take out borrowings of $9.50 million to fund the purchase of 17 side loader waste trucks. All existing borrowings are projected to be repaid by 2033.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Own-source revenue declined in 2021-22 due to COVID-19 impacts on user fees and charges. That year also saw a $70 million loan to fund major infrastructure projects, increasing non-current liabilities. In 2022-23, revenue returned to pre-pandemic levels for user fees and charges and Council also refinanced its 2012-13 loan for a further 10 years. In 2023-24, growth was driven by a net gain on asset disposals and higher other income, while non-current liabilities decreased due to loan repayments. For 2024-25, increased returns from the Regional Landfill Clayton South joint venture and increased income for rates and charges supported revenue growth, alongside further reductions in borrowings.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The result reflects Asset Renewal budgets and the timing of major projects. Increases in 2020-21 and 2021-22 were driven by planned major project expenditure, demonstrating Council’s ongoing commitment to renewing existing community infrastructure. During 2022-23 and 2023-24, the timing of planned renewal projects were reviewed, resulting in the deferral of some works to future years. In 2024-25, renewal expenditure and depreciation remained consistent with prior years, while upgrade expenditure was lower than the previous year. Forecast years remain steady and are based on an agreed program of works.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The adjusted underlying result for 2021-22 was due to the ongoing impacts of COVID-19 and the resulting impacts on revenue and expenditure streams. For 2022-23 Council saw a reduction in the impacts of COVID-19 on its operations.  The improved adjusted underlying result for 2023-24 was positively impacted by a once off net gain on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment and an increase in other income and user fees and charges. The 2024-25 result was primarily influenced by increased returns from the joint venture arrangement with Regional Landfill Clayton South. Additional increases in rates and charges, statutory fees and fines, and user fees were largely offset from no gain on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment in 2024-25.  It is anticipated that the adjusted underlying result will improve over the long term future year projections.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Boroondara receives a very low level of financial assistance from the Victorian Government therefore, rate revenue will continue to be a key source of funding for the delivery of high quality services and infrastructure to the community.  The adjusted underlying revenue for 2021-22 was at reduced levels due to the ongoing impacts of COVID-19 on Council's revenue streams primarily in user fees and charges.  The adjusted underlying revenue for 2022-23 improved compared to previous years due to a reduction in COVID-19 impacts across Council services.  Additionally, the adjusted underlying revenue for 2023-24 improved due to a net gain on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment which was partially offset by a reduction in grants from the Federal Assistance Grants from Victoria Local Government Grants Commission (VLGGC).  The result for 2024-25 is consistent with 2023-24 levels, noting that also Council received 50% of the 2025-26 Federal Assistance Grant allocation.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Boroondara receives a very low level of financial assistance and rate revenue is a key source of funding for the delivery of high quality services and infrastructure to the community. This shift is due to the adjustment to the Capital Improved Value (CIV) of all rateable properties and the impact of the 2.75 per cent overall rate increase. It is anticipated that there is no significant variation in the future forecasts.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Council continues to deliver services in accordance with its Long Term Financial Plan.  There has been an increase in the overall expenditure primarily due to an increase in the net loss on the disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment compared to 2023-24. There has also been an increase employee costs, in line with the rate cap increase, and increases in materials and services and depreciation. There has also been a slight increase in the population by 2% for 2024-25.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The result reflects Council's continued commitment to invest in high quality infrastructure. The increase in 2024-25 is primarily due to the completion of building and infrastructure projects, along with a revaluation of infrastructure was undertaken during the year. There has also been a slight increase in the population by 2%.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: As part of our Road Management Plan review in 2021 as per the Road Management Act, Council agreed to include additional laneways under the revised plan, which has increased the road length reported against Local Government Reporting. The length of roads reported in 2021-22 was 655 kilometres compared to 569 kilometres in 2020-21. There has been no change in the length of roads reported since 2022-23. The slight increase in the population by 2% has driven the increase noted in 2024-25 which remains within the expected range.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Own-source revenue was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic in 2021-22, with reductions across user fees and charges. In 2022-23, Council's own-source revenue increased primarily in user fees and fines returning to pre-pandemic levels. In 2023-24, growth was driven by a net gain on the disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment, alongside increases in other income and user fees. The 2024-25 result was primarily influenced by increased returns from the joint venture arrangement with Regional Landfill Clayton South. Additional increases in rates and charges, statutory fees and fines, and user fees were largely offset from no gain on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment in 2024-25. The result was also impacted by an increase in the population by 2%.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Council received 100% of the 2023-24 Federal Assistance Grants allocation ($5.35 million) in the 2022-23 financial year. No early payment was received for the 2024-25 allocation during the 2023-24 financial year. In 2024-25, Federal Assistance Grants were higher primarily due to timing, with 50% of the 2025-26 allocation brought forward into the 2024-25 financial year. Adjusting for this early payment, recurrent grants per head of population would reduce down to $64.09, highlighting that grant funding is not keeping pace with population growth. This was further impacted by a 2% increase in population during 2024-25.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The Socio-Economic Disadvantage indicator remains consistent and the result reflects a low level of socio-economic disadvantage.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: The 2024-25 year turnover rate of 11.48% is a positive reduction from the prior year. This indicates strong employee retention and stability within the organisation and falls within our target range. Additionally, a level of turnover is good for innovation, growth and improved organisation performance.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Brimbank City Council continues to conduct inspections of Council's aquatic facilities during peak and off-peak periods. Leisure Services also arrange biomedical tests on both facilities in addition to health inspections. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Visitation continues to be excellent across the facilities with over 1,500,000 visits for the Brimbank Aquatic and Wellness Centre (BAWC) alone. This is due to the retention of high gym and aquatic member numbers, in addition to growth of the learn to swim program to 4,500 people. Sunshine Leisure Centre has also achieved growth due to learn to swim numbers growing to 1,700 people and group fitness visits also exceeding last year's figures. The result of 9.92 is higher than the 9.73 from last year, a good result considering the reduction in casual swimmers at BAWC, in its third year of operation.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The positive financial performance of the Brimbank Aquatic and Wellness Centre (BAWC) is the primary factor influencing the continued 'negative' cost of aquatic facilities. The results for the third year has seen impressive growth in swim school numbers and solid retention of gym members. While the cost of aquatic facilities continues to sit outside the expected range, due to strong performance, this does not include all the costs of running the centres and some additional maintenance and renewal costs. This means the overall cost to Council is higher. The two year defect and liability period is now over so repairs and maintenance costs have started to grow and will continue to cost more when renewal projects are undertaken. Sunshine Leisure Centre has reduced the operating deficit with better than expected Learn to Swim performance and member growth.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Council places a strong emphasis on responsible pet ownership and community safety, with all animal requests followed up within the required result range from receipt of request. These figures represent all requests requiring the attendance and action of one of Council's Animal Management Officers. The result is a  slight decrease  from last year and well within the range expected by Council.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Many residents continue to experience financial difficulties, with some unable to pay the costs of longer-term animal care and animal collection which can result in an infringement for a breach of the Domestic Animals Act 1994 exposing the resident to fines.  This figure has increased slightly but the pressures previously experienced and commented on, continue for many in the community. Council and its partners are focused on the opportunity for animals that are not reclaimed to find new homes via adoption and rehoming pathways.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: While this result is a decrease on the 2023/2024 result, it remains above the range expected by Council. The decrease is likely due to ongoing financial pressures and the tightening of household budgets. Animals that remain in care are still available to be rehomed in the future and Council remains committed, in partnership with our stakeholders, to rehoming opportunities for all animals where possible.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Council continues to prioritise the delivery of Animal Management services in a cost-efficient manner. This result is slightly higher than that of 2023/2024 though remains well within the range expected by Council.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council continues to investigate and prosecute, where possible, all animal related matters. This figure represents concluded prosecutions. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Council places a strong emphasis on food safety and public health. Reports of food complaints and related requests are responded to promptly. This ensures a continued high level of food safety monitoring. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Assessments are conducted on all premises that are required to have one. The current (5-year from August 2022) exemption in the requirement for some businesses to have a Food Safety Program in place has led to a significant reduction in the number of Class 2 businesses requiring an assessment. All remaining businesses have been either assessed or inspected to ensure compliance with the Food Act 1984.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Council prioritises food safety and the health of the Brimbank community. Despite being higher than 2024, the cost of the food safety service is at the lower end of the expected range, demonstrating an efficient service.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Council continues to give precedence to the delivery of an effective and efficient risk-based food safety service. All premises with major or critical non-compliance notifications are routinely followed up. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Food safety samples
variation: This service indicator was added in 2023/2024. It reports  on how many food samples are tested, whether they meet safety standards, and any follow-up actions for non-compliance. The 2024/2025 result sits just outside the range expected by Council in a positive way.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: This is a positive, consistent result and well within the range expected by Council.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The community satisfaction rating on Council's performance in community consultation and engagement is reported from the independent annual Community Satisfaction Survey for 2024/2025. This excellent result can be attributed to Council conducting extensive deliberative engagement and community consultations, both in person and online, as we developed our new Community Vision and Council Plan. Other consultations carried out over the year related to community services and infrastructure planning, park upgrades, water management, community transport, parking, ageing and disability care and governance. This result sits above the range expected by Council.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: This result is an improvement on 2023/2024 results and sits within the range expected by Council.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost of elected representation for 2024/2025 is slightly higher than that of the previous financial year. This can be attributed to a new Council being elected and the costs that are associated with their induction. The result continues to sit within the range expected by Council.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The community satisfaction rating on Council's performance in making decisions in the interest of the community is reported from the independent annual Community Satisfaction Survey for 2024/2025. This result remains consistent with the 2023/2024 result and sits above the range expected by Council.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Council ensures that its library collection meets the evolving needs and preferences of the community, while continuously improving resource management, purchasing, and maintenance practices. The percentage of recently purchased library items has increased year-on-year, placing Brimbank Libraries in the upper region of the expected range and reaching the stated 80% target in our Collections guidelines.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Council continues to deliver cost efficient library services that foster social connection and community wellbeing. Libraries support the community by providing a wide range of resources including learning materials, leisure resources, a Languages Other Than English (LOTE) collection and literacy development programs, English proficiency support, job seeking resources, and other assistance. This result sits within the range expected by Council.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: By again reaching the higher end of the expected range, results indicate that the collection has a strong alignment with community need and interests. The result also reflects continuous improvement in resource management, purchasing and maintenance practices.

description: Library membership
variation: The membership rate of 32.91% of the population reflects strong community engagement and utilisation of library services, with many residents benefitting from the resources and opportunities offered. This level of membership demonstrates that the libraries are effectively meeting the needs and interests of a large portion of the community. It also highlights their role as essential community hubs, promoting literacy, learning, social engagement, and access to information.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The result of 4.16 library visits per capita indicate that Brimbank Libraries retains a high level of community engagement and utilisation across library facilities. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Council has consistently achieved more than 100% infant enrolments in the Maternal and Child Health Service, maintaining a high level of community engagement with the service for the past eight years.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Council places a strong emphasis on delivering services in a cost-efficient manner. The cost of service is lower than 2023/2024 due to no longer using agency staff and successful recruitment of permanent and fixed-term staff. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Council is committed to providing high quality Maternal and Child Health services. The 2024/2025 participation result is lower than the expected range due to the impacts of parental leave and other staff leave and the lack of qualified MCH staff state-wide to provide back-fill. Available appointments were prioritised for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander clients identified as vulnerable. This result is outside of the range expected by Council.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Council prioritises providing high quality Maternal and Child Health services to Brimbank's Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander community. This result has increased when compared to the previous reporting period due to new initiatives within the outreach stream of Maternal and Child Health services.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Council recorded an attendance rate of 92% for infants at the 4-week Key Age and Stage Visit in the 2024/2025 financial year. This is within the range expected by Council.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Council responds to resident requests for sealed local roads within the approved requirements of the Road Management Plan. There was a 13% decrease in road requests in 2024-2025 compared to 2023-2024, likely due to an increased focus on proactive road maintenance. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The proportion of sealed local roads maintained to the required condition standard is comparable to the 2023/2024 level and remains within the range expected by Council. Council remains dedicated to maintaining the local road network and continues to carry out necessary maintenance and rehabilitation works.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: CPI and rising material and labour costs have seen an increase in the pavement construction rate of 6%. The additional variance from 2023/2024 is as a result of a change to the baseline figure, which now includes the costs of kerb and channel, which was previously excluded in previous years figures. Council's focus on cost-efficiency, and the use of a rolling annual works contract, enabled it to maintain baseline costs near to previous levels. The resulting costs remain within the range expected by Council.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The cost of sealed local road resealing continued to rise in 2024/2025. This increase was partly driven by the inclusion of approximately 35% of high-volume, heavy traffic roads in this year's program. Additionally, ongoing material shortages, price fluctuations, market demand, and competitive pressures contributed to higher costs, reflecting broader trends in the rising costs of goods and services. As a result, the final cost was slightly above Council.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: The community satisfaction rating on Council's performance with sealed local roads is reported from the independent annual Community Satisfaction Survey for 2024/2025. This result increased on the 2023/2024 result. There has been a corresponding reduction in the number of sealed local road requests. These results potentially indicate an increased overall satisfaction in the condition of roads in the municipality for this year.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: Council places a strong emphasis on working with applicants and objectors to achieve good outcomes in planning determinations and continues to balance time taken to negotiate land use and development issues with the need to ensure efficient processing of applications. Whilst there has been an increase in the median number of days for processing applications in 2024/2025 compared to the previous year, this is attributed to dealing with some very complex applications which take longer and allowing applicants some additional time to provide all required information to promote better outcomes. The result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Council works with applicants and objectors to achieve good outcomes in planning determinations and has put a lot of work into improving processes to be as efficient as possible. Whilst this result is slightly less than the result achieved last year, it is comparatively better than most other Councils and sits within the range set by Council. The data for this measure was obtained from the Planning Permit Activity Reporting (PPAR) website on 11 July 2025.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: This result has seen costs of planning applications increase when compared to the 2023/2024 reporting period, primarily due to the reduction in the number of applications received during this period, meaning the overall cost is spread out over fewer applications. This result continues to sit within the range set by Council.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Council places a strong emphasis on ensuring planning decisions are consistent with the Brimbank Planning Scheme and achieve positive outcomes for the community. No decisions on planning applications were set aside during the 2024/2025 financial year. This, combined with the fact that only 7 applications were considered and determined by VCAT during this time, suggests that there is a strong alignment between the decisions being made and the expectations of the Brimbank Planning Scheme.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Council seeks to deliver an effective and efficient kerbside collection service for the community. While there has been a slight increase on last year's result, it is within the range expected by Council.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: While the result sits outside of the range expected by Council, there has been a decrease in the cost of this service for the 2024/2025 financial year. Kerbside collection operating expenses have shown an increase over the last two years. Unique factors contributing to Brimbank’s higher than average costs include a fully in-house collection service, logistics costs due to the large area and low density, relatively high volumes of waste per household, opt-in food organics and garden organics (FOGO) service, and a weekly garbage collection service.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: Council aims to deliver an effective and efficient kerbside recyclables collection service. This result shows a 25% decrease from 2023/2024, resulting from a reduction in the kerbside recycling tonnes collected and approximately $601k of income from Councils recyclables processing contractor from the sale of recyclables collected from the kerbside bin collection service.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The waste diversion rate decreased slightly from last year's result. There was a decrease in tonnages in organic material due to changes in climatic conditions and a reduction in recyclables tonnages due to the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme (CDS).

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Expenses per property assessment increased slightly from the prior year but remain below the budgeted target and within Council’s expected range. This reflects efficient cost management in delivering services.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The average rate per property assessment increased during 2024/2025, reflecting the rate rise adopted in the budget. This outcome is within the Victorian Government’s rate cap and aligned with Council’s long-term financial planning.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Council places high value on the effective management of Council’s assets and liabilities. The current year result is lower than last year, primarily due to an increase in current interest-bearing liabilities. An interest-only loan of $21 million is due for repayment in 2025/2026, which has also contributed to the reduced forecast result for that year. The 2024/2025 result is also higher than the targeted budget mainly due to the increase in cash  and cash equivalents due to early payment of 50% of the 2025/2026 Victorian Local Government Grant Commission (VLGGC) allocation and higher amount of open space contributions.  The result is in the range expected by Council.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Brimbank City Council places high value on effective management of Council assets and liabilities. The 2024/2025 unrestricted cash balance has decreased since the last financial year. This is mainly due to increase in statutory reserve balances. The result is in the range expected by Council.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council places strong emphasis on efficient and effective financial management, including management of loans and borrowings. The 2024/2025 result is slightly higher than last year as the interest bearing liabilities are higher than last years due to the draw down of additional borrowings of $14 million. The significant decrease in the 2026 forecast is due to the payout of an interest only loan of $21 million and the remaining forecast years have no further lump sum payouts. The result is in the range expected by Council.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council places strong emphasis on efficient and effective financial management, including management loans and borrowings. The repayments figure slightly increased in 2024/2025, with an overall loan balance increase due to the draw down of additional borrowings of $14 million. The 2026 forecast result is higher due to the payout of an interest only loan of $21 million. This result remains in the range set by Council.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Council places strong emphasis on efficient and effective financial management. The improved result in 2024/2025 is due to the movement of  interest only loan from non-recurrent to current liabilities, for loan to be paid out in 2025/2026. This result is within the range expected by Council.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Council places strong emphasis on the ongoing renewal and upgrade of assets and has obtained resourcing for a number of asset renewal projects over the past few years.  Renewal expenditure on capital projects increased by $11 million in 2024/2025 year.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: This indicator has improved due to the early payment of 50% of the 2025/2026 Victorian Local Government Grant Commission (VLGGC) allocation.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Council endeavours to manage revenue and rates efficiently. The 2024/2025 financial year saw a decrease in rates compared to revenue compared to last year.  This is due to early payment of 50% of the 2025/2026 Victorian Local Government Grant Commission (VLGGC) allocation.  The result is in the range expected by Council.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Council continues to ensure rating levels are based on the community’s capacity to pay and within the State Government rate cap limits. Rates, as a whole, includes general rates and municipal and environmental charges. This result remains in the range set by Council.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Council is committed to minimising expenses where possible while ensuring essential services are available. The main contributor to the increase is employee cost and slight increase in depreciation cost.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Council endeavours to deliver efficient and effective financial management. Infrastructure value has increased reflecting valuations and increased construction costs. The result is in the range expected by Council.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Council places strong emphasis on maintaining a road network that services the requirements of our community. This result is consistent with previous years and remains in the range expected by Council.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Council is committed to high quality revenue management. The increased result in 2024/2025 reflects increased income from statutory fees, user fees and charges and increased interest on investments. This result remains in the range expected by Council.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The result reflects the early receipt of 50% of the 2025/2026 Victorian Local Government Grants Commission (VLGGC) allocation in June 2025.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Council remains the second most disadvantaged municipality in Greater Melbourne and the third most disadvantaged in Victoria. These results have remained consistent over the past five years.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Council experienced slight increase in staff turnover during the 2024/2025 financial year influenced by the wider economic uncertainty. The result is in the range expected by Council.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Pool inspections in previous years have been undertaken externally. However, they missed out on being reported.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The pools were open for more days in 24/25 compared to the previous year.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Increase in the duration of aquatic facilities being open, caused an increase in attendance thus decreasing the unit cost of the service.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
Animals reclaimed
variation: Reduction in impounding activity due to staff shortages, resulting in the absence of full-time active patrols across the shire to detect dogs at large

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Lower reclaim rates have led to an increase in animals rehomed upon impoundment

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Reduction in salaries and wage expenses due to staff shortages.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council undertook one animal management prosecution during the period, representing a 100% increase compared to the prior period

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: As Council does not have an on-site Environmental Health Officer, investigations are undertaken by external contractors- resulting in longer timeframes to action matters

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Council engaged a contracted Environmental Health Officer, resulting in the delivery of the food premises inspection program effectively during the period

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Using external contracted EHO’s for all EH responsibilities in lieu of having a full time onsite EHO.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: There were no critical or major non-compliance notifications.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Change considered nominal

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Change considered nominal

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Based on community satisfaction survey

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Change considered nominal

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: Increase in expenditure relates to trainings and workshops held for new council elected

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Based on community satisfaction survey

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Change considered nominal

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: No material change

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Increase due to addition of new library collection materials.

description: Library membership
variation: Buloke as a rural community has decreasing and dispersed population

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Officers aim to inspect all pools monthly during peak periods. Inspections conducted this year were higher than previous years due to programming of inspections and the availability of officers.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The increase in use of aquatic facilities can be attributed to an increase in attendance at the Echuca War Memorial Aquatic Centre. There has been a 9% decrease in the use of outdoor facilities.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Council’s decision to provide free access to the outdoor pools resulted in reduced income. Increasing operational costs for all facilities has been slightly mitigated by increased attendance at the indoor facility.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: During 2024/25 Council undertook a review of its systems and processes related to this indicator . Officers continue to prioritise a timely response to animal management requests.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Officers have been working hard to ensure that impounded animals are reunited with registered owners through many channels including direct communication and social media posts.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Council has been transitioning unclaimed animals from the shelter to animal rescue groups using section 84Y of the Domestic Animals Act 1994. This has resulted in a further 228 (27%) animals being transitioned out of Council's facility.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The reduction in cost is due in part to Council  transitioning unclaimed animals from the shelter to animal rescue groups using section 84Y of the Domestic Animals Act 1994.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: No animal prosecutions were issued in 2024/25.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Action on the majority of complaints received by Council was commenced on the same day they were received. While the investigation of two complaints was delayed due to their low risk or resourcing levels, all complaints were investigated.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Delays in sourcing and engaging suitably qualified staff affected performance for the first half of 2024. The need for Secondary Impact Assessments for the first quarter following minor flooding in areas of the municipality also contributed. Despite these challenges 83% of the required assessments were completed.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The number of registered premises increased in 2024/25 resulting in a decrease in per premise service cost, returning it to comparable figures for the 2022 and 2023 results. A decrease in the number of registered food premises in  2023/24 contributed to an increase to the per premises service cost in that period.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Non-compliant businesses were managed as a priority, which ensured that any identified issues were addressed by businesses as directed. In the previous year one non-compliance issue was not completed within the 2023 year resulting in the 90% result for that year.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Food sampling has been completed in line with statutory requirements.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Decisions made at meetings closed to the public increased from 4.7% to 11.2% due to an increase in commercial and confidential issues tabled including property sale decisions, service reviews, tender awards and personnel related matters.  Where possible Council continues to provide reports in a format which allows more decisions to be made in meetings open to the public without the inappropriate release of confidential information, and endeavours to release information as soon as appropriate to the public.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Council's consultation and engagement rating remained at 56% for the second year in a row,  the highest level of satisfaction for Council since 2015. Consultation and engagement have been undertaken across many council service areas and included major engagement around flood recovery, key infrastructure projects, Place Based Plans, the preparation of Council's 2025/26 Annual Budget, and the establishment of a Citizen’s Panel to inform the development of the new Council Plan. Community engagement remains an area of focus for Council. Council's score exceeds the average overall satisfaction level (52%) across regional Victoria.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance remained high. This has been aided by the continued provision of online participation in Council meetings.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation:
The Mayoral, Deputy Mayoral  and Councillor allowances increased in line with the determinations of the Victorian Independent Remuneration Tribunal.
The increase in cost compared to the previous years also reflects the inclusion of costs related to the legislative mandatory training requirements following the General Election in 2024.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Although there was a small drop in the result, shared by Councils across Victoria, the 2025 Community Satisfaction Survey continues the overall trend of increase in satisfaction with Council's decision making in the interests of our community. Council's score exceeds the average overall satisfaction level across regional Victoria (47).

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The increase in the library collection is attributed to the inclusion of the digital  collection items.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Increased funds have been allocated to address the age of the physical collection and to increase digital resources to meet user demand.  Increased staffing costs have arisen across the service due to casualisation, staff leave and changes in operating hours.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Library loans per head of population have increased compared to previous year. This indicator now includes digital resources.

description: Library membership
variation: Participation is stable and well within the expected benchmark figure for Councils for this indicator.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: This is the second year this indicator has been reported and shows an increase in Library visitations.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: The trend in infant enrolments in the MCH service has generally remained steady across the last four years of reporting.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: An increase in cost is noted when compared to previous year. This is mainly attributable to the Victorian MCH service funding model.  A reduction in grant funding occurred based on prior year data.  Staffing was planned and budgeted to meet the staff to client ratio requirement under the industrial instrument.  This increased Council's overall subsidy to the service.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: This result is slightly higher than the previous reporting period showing a positive trend over the past 4 years. The service and the sector continues to review engagement practices with the MCH service to increase participation rates, particularly from 8 months - 3.5 years.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: The trend in participation in MCH services by Aboriginal children gradually increasing, reflecting dedicated effort to grow attendance rates, particularly from 18 months to 3.5 years.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation in the four-week key age and stage visit has remained on trend with prior reporting periods.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Requests in 2024/25 have returned to similar levels to the years prior to the 2022 flood which led to a higher number of sealed local road requests in the year following.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: A review of the sealed local road network condition data during the 2024/25 period identified an increase in the number of roads that had reached their intervention level. The roads that have reached intervention level will be scoped as part of the 2025-26 Capital Works renewal program.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Results are higher than the previous reporting period due to only one road rehabilitation being completed this year. Other projects were put on hold due to delays with external parties. The completed project was a smaller, urban project with some extra detailed works (i.e. DDA parking hardstands).

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The cost for road resealing remains consistent with the previous year’s results.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: The Community Satisfaction Survey result for sealed local roads 2025 is consistent with the previous year and reflects an increase in satisfaction. Council continues its focus on road maintenance in line with community expectations. The result is 6 points higher than the regional Victorian average, and 11 points higher than the large shire average.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: While reviewing this year’s data sources it was identified that last year’s input average used statutory days which is different from the collection of Planning Permit Activity Reporting System (PPARS) data. Last year should have been 64 which would result in only a slight change for this year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Data used in last years reporting on this indicator was not taken fromthe Planning Permit Activity Reporting System (PPARS)  as there were discrepancies in reporting. This has now been resolved and the discrepancies understood. Had PPARS been used last year the percentage indicator would have been 78.74% and the real increasea was a 2% change. Applications decided in timeframes has seen a change due to the assessment of a number of larger applications which have required additional stakeholder engagement to achieve good planning outcomes. Despite this the overall time frames for processing remain appropriate.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: Council has seen an increase in costs of the service due to staff changes which has required the use of external consultants at a higher rate.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: During the 2024/25 period only one Council decision was resolved at VCAT, upholding Council's decision.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Missed bin rates remain well below the State average, while turnover in drivers and contractor supervisors has contributed as the primary cause of missed bins.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Costs decreased slightly compared to 2023/24 due in part to a reduction in waste tonnage.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: Significantly reduced recycling tonnage has reduced costs, including a positive change in glass commodity pricing.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: While the ratio of kerbside collection diverted from landfill decreased in 2024/25 compared to the previous year, overall there has been a total reduction in tonnage of material collected through the kerbside streams. An increase in Food Organics Garden Organics was offset by significant reduction in recycling tonnage (12%), the reduction in recycling is attributed to increased access, and awareness of, the container deposit scheme.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
Council's expenses increased due to the cost of delivering services, increased depreciation following asset revaluations and the write off of disposed assets. 

Forecast amounts for 2026 through to 2029 years improve on the published 2025/26 Budget Document due to a revision in the number of rate assessments applied to this indicator, resulting in a decrease in expense per assessment.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation:
A 2.60% increase on 2023/24 actual, when comparing 2024/25 actual, is consistent with the 2.75% rate cap increase, supplementary rates during the year (council saw an increase in assessment numbers by 133 for the 2024/25 year). 

Forecast amounts for 2026 through to 2029 years amend slightly on the published 2025/26 Budget Document due to a revision in the number of rate assessments applied to this indicator.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation:
Net increase is predominately due to the advance payment of financial assistance grant (of $7.93 million) in June for the 2025/26 financial year. Other movements in current assets and liabilities offset, however current assets has increased by net $3.18 million and liabilities has decreased by $4.55 million, resulting in the overall increase in this indicator compared to target. 

Forecast amounts for 2026 through to 2029 years amend when compared to the published 2025/26 Budget Document due to a revision on external restrictions, whereby some restrictions were not treated as external restrictions when calculating the future forecast figure.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
A combination of higher cash, cash equivalents and investments, with a lower current liability balance as of 30 June 2025, when compared to the 30 June 2024 balances, has resulted in an improved indicator. This is tied back to receiving the financial assistance grant in advance (which did not occur for the 2023/24 year), as well as lower liability balances for loans and trade payables owed at 30 June 2025.

Forecast indicators through to 2028/29 financial year are different to the published indicators in the 2025/26 Budget document due to treatment of Council's internal restrictions. For the purpose of this indicator, these internal restrictions should be treated as non-restricted cash. This resulted in this indicator being understated in the 2025/26 Budget document.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation:
For the 2024/25 financial year, Council continued to repay current loan borrowings, whilst having no new loans for the year. 

In Council's 4-year budget forecast to 2029 year, this ratio is increasing as Council's 2025/26 budget document outlines a proposal to borrow funds for capital works over the financial years 2027, 2028, and 2029.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Decreases in line with loan holdings being repaid by Council during the year and rates revenue increases in total for the 2025/26 year. With the forecast borrowings to 2029 year, this ratio does increase.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation:
For the 2024/25 financial year, whilst employee provision increased, this was more than offset by the decrease in current loan borrowings, which Council continues to pay down. Overall, non-current liabilities decreased by $0.82 million and own source revenue increased by $2.61 million. This results in a lower percentage compared to previous year. 

In Council's 4-year budget forecast to the 2029 year, this ratio is increasing as Council's 2025/26 budget document outlines a proposal to borrow funds for capital works over the financial years 2027, 2028, and 2029. If the loans occur, then this will increase the non-current liability balance at 30 June for each forecast year.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation:
Down when compared to 2023/24 financial year as Council only completed 82% of the original $32.596 million capital works budget for 2024/25 year. Some projects experienced delays and have been carried over into the 2025/26 financial year and there were also some savings on projects completed (under budget).

In Council's 4-year budget forecast to the 2029 year, the figures reported are different to the published indicators in the 2025/26 Budget document due to understatement of asset renewal and upgrade works totals for the years 2028 and 2029.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Improvement on prior year result was a $22.27 million change. The main consideration for this turnaround is to do with Council receiving two financial assistance grants allocation in 2024/25 that total $20.85 million. This can be referenced on the Grants - Operating line on the Income Statement and Note 3.4 in the Financial Statements. This is a timing variance due to when Council receives the funding allocation.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation:
This ratio is below target of 56% predominately due to Council receiving two financial assistance grants allocation in 2024/25 that total $20.85 million, whereas there was no advance payment in the 2023/24 financial year. This can be referenced on the Grants - Operating line on the Income Statement and Note 3.4 in the Financial Statements. This is a timing variance due to when Council receives the funding allocation. 

In Council's 4-year budget forecast to the 2029 year, the figures reported are different to the published indicators in the 2025/26 Budget document due to correction to the adjusted underlying revenue amount to include the Roads to Recovery grant funding as recurrent funding. This resulted in a decrease in original reported figures over the 4-year forecast.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation:
2024/25 actual has remained consistent when compared to previous year.

Forecast indicators through to 2029 year are different to the published indicators in the 2025/26 Budget document due to correction to capital improved value of ratable properties. This resulted in an improvment in original reported figures over the 4-year forecast.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The time taken to decide planning application has improved due to a stabilisation of application numbers and sustained improvements to the resourcing and capacity of Council's Planning Department.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Council has seen improved timeliness in planning decisions, supported by stabilised application volumes and ongoing enhancements in processing. A review of the methodology used to calculate performance was undertaken which has also impacted this performance measure.

Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: This result is always highly variable given the small number of cases that proceed to VCAT and the specific nature of each case.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Council has re-tendered the recycling processing contract and established a cost saving in this space. This saving is reflected in the overall cost of running kerbside services.
