Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
Expenses per property assessment
variation: The increase in expense per property was mainly due to higher material and service costs, asset write-offs, and storm recovery efforts. These were mainly driven by works on non-Council owned facilities, unplanned infrastructure adjustments, and external project impacts.

description: Average rate per property assessment
Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The increase in liquidity is primarily driven by a reduction in current liabilities and an increase in current assets. Lower trade and other payables at year-end contributed to the decline in liabilities.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Council continues to regularly review and actively manage its unrestricted cash. Movements are reflective of uncertainty of movement in reserves and grant funding recognition

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Repayment of loans and timing of new loan drawdowns to support capital delivery, resulted in a net increase of 49.79% compared to FY23/24. Increase of rates revenue has been 5% in FY24/25 from FY23/24.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Due to timing of loans drawdowns and pay out of maturing loans during the FY24/25 financial year.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: This ratio shows how much of the entity’s own source revenue would be needed to cover its long-term liabilities. The 25% increase is attributed to the timing of new loan drawdowns in order support capital delivery.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Council reviewed the capital renewal program with an emphasis on optimising financial sustainability and is aiming to maintain an asset renewal ratio between 90%-110%. The reduction in the FY24/25 program was the first step towards this target.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Mainly due to the advance payment of 50% of Financial Assistance Grants for FY25/26 ($8.4M) received in June 2025.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: This ratio indicates how much of the Council’s regular income comes from rates, compared to other sources. Even though rates income increased by 5% compared to FY23/24, Council received significantly more contributions in the form of assets (like donated infrastructure) which increased the total income.

description: Rates compared to property values
Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The variance in materials and services is primarily due to the reclassification of capital projects on Crown land as operational expenditure, including Cora Lynn Reserve ($1.2M) and Upper Beaconsfield Reserve (741K). Additionally, the construction and demolition costs associated with the Cardinia Youth Facility Expansion ($3.6M) were expensed. Relocation of bank charges amounting to $687K from the "Other Expenses" category into materials and services.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
Population density per length of road
Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Significant increase is due to timing of the receipt of Financial Assistance Grant from Victorian Local Government Grants Commission (VLGGC). 50% of Financial Assistance Grants from VLGGC for FY25/26 received in advance in June 2025 and 95% of FY23/24 allocation was received in June 2023.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
Percentage of staff turnover
variation: When max-term and casual staff is included, Council’s overall turnover is improving (at 14.8% in FY24/25). A higher-than-usual number of retirements in the past 12 months has also impacted this figure.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: There has been a 20% increase in the number of health inspections of aquatic facilities since last year. This can be attributed to officer inspections conducted in response to outbreak investigations. It should be noted that these aquatic facilities are operated by third party organisations, but owned by Council. The result remains in the expected range.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: There has been a 76% decrease in the cost of aquatic facilities since last year. This can be attributed to implementation of new Leisure Contract that guarantees return to Council.  In addition, the new Leisure Management company have successfully reduced expenditure across all sites.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: There has been a 10% decline in the number of animals reclaimed compared to last year. This decrease is primarily due to a rise in nuisance complaints involving cats, resulting in more impounded cats and kittens that are not microchipped or registered. Without proper identification, these animals cannot be effectively reunited with their owners. Additionally, the number of dogs being reclaimed has dropped, with many owners surrendering their pets due to ongoing cost-of-living pressures.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Animal management service costs have risen by 19% compared to last year. This increase is largely driven by higher contract expenses and a surge in the number of uncontained and unregistered animals being impounded. It’s important to note that this figure excludes revenue generated from animal registration, Domestic Animal Business registration, infringement notices, and pound reclaim fees.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
Animal management prosecutions
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: There has been a 16% increase in time taken to action food complaints since last year. This can be attributed to an increase in the number of complaints compared to prior periods. With the requirement to include weekends and public holidays in the total response time, Council has been restricted in its ability to resolve complaints within one day. However, the vast majority of complaints have been addressed on the same working day or the day after receipt. The result remains in the expected range.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: There has been a 14% increase in the cost of the food safety service since last year. This can be attributed to increasing contract cost associated with the delivery of this service. The result remains in the expected range.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council makes very few decisions in closed Council meetings, with a strong preference for transparent decision making in open Council meetings. While Council did make slightly more confidential resolutions in 2024/25 than it did the previous year, the overall result is still within the expected range.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: There has been a 18% decrease in satisfaction with community consultation and engagement since last year. The score decline can largely be attributed to negative media surrounding several high-profile issues that gained significant attention during January and February this year. These include community concerns regarding historical engagement methods used for the Community Local Law 2023, as well as the Hampton Park Waste Transfer Station. In addition, although outside our direct remit, unrest related to the renaming of Berwick Springs Lake is also likely to have contributed to the drop.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Attendance results remained within the expected range throughout the year. It’s worth noting the transition from Administrators to Councillors during this period: the first four months were overseen by three Administrators, followed by eight months with 12 Councillors in attendance. Councillors demonstrated consistent engagement, with attendance levels aligning with expectations and reflecting their active involvement.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: There has been a 76% decrease in the cost of elected representation since last year. The decrease in cost of elected representation reflects the return of Councillors during the year. The cost for the current year is within the expected range.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Council continues to work hard to make decisions in the best interest of the community. While the score is lower than previous years, the overall result is within the expected range. Council will continue to focus on high quality community engagement and clear reporting to improve decision making on behalf of the community.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The proportion of the library collection purchased over the past 5 years has remained stable (a 1.99% increase) since the previous year, when there was a 31% increase due to the renewal of stock following the departure of Cardinia Shire from the Regional Library, continued investment in e-books and e-audio books and the opening of the Cranbourne West Library Lounge.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: There has been a 35% increase in the cost of the library service per population since last year, however the result is still in the expected range. The cost this year includes capital expenditure on library collections.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Library loans per population have remained stable (a 1.41% increase) and are significant, given the recent population growth (revised to 405,415), noting that there is a lag between population growth and provision of additional services.

description: Library membership
variation: Library membership per population has increased by 6.02% since last year. This can be partly attributed to promotion of memberships to patrons of the new Cranbourne West Library Lounge and click-and-collect Library Lockers at 3 Council facilities (opened in Feb 2024).

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Library visits per population have remained stable, with a 1.33% increase. This does not include use of click-and-collect Library Lockers at 3 locations or outreach programming (including the library van), which are predominant in the south and growth areas of Casey where there are no permanent library branches.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: There has been a 20% increase in participation in the MCH service since last year. Over the last 12 months Council has invested in reducing staffing vacancies and increasing access to MCH services through additional weekend sessions. Improved staff retention and additional Saturday sessions have resulted in an increase in participation in the MCH service.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: There has been a 24% increase in participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children since last year. Through a dedicated Outreach role the MCH service has increased the engagement of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (ATSI) families into the MCH service. MCH nurses have also undergone cultural awareness training to support a more positive experience for families.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The rise in service requests is primarily due to the expansion of the road network transferred to Council by developers, along with a surge in residential and subdivision construction across the municipality. Various infrastructure projects across multiple locations have also contributed to the increase in complaints. This has led to higher traffic volumes and accelerated road deterioration, particularly where heavy vehicles bypass major routes to access construction sites. In additional, many residents are unaware of the distinction between roads managed by the Department of Transport and Planning (DTP) and those managed by Council, resulting in misdirected concerns. Council are currently reviewing raw data to support improvements in the Request for Service (RFS) and Work Order (WO) recording system. These enhancements, as part of the recent RMP (Road Management Plan) implementation process and corporate system upgrade (One Council), will enable more accurate categorisation of requests based on road ownership and defect types.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: There has been a 28% decrease in the cost of sealed local road reconstruction since last year. This reduction is primarily due to an increase in footpath and concreting works within the program, which required less expenditure on asphalting. The savings achieved were redirected to support other projects that experienced budget overruns.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The cost of sealed local road resealing has decreased by 18% since last year. This is attributed to a strategic shift in the program towards repairing and strengthening roads with lower traffic volumes, rather than focusing on high-traffic areas. The cost reduction is evident in the reduced thickness of asphalt applied to these lower-volume roads.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: There has been a 14% decrease in time taken to decide planning applications since last year. As compared to last year, the results show that we are making decisions faster. This positive result could be attributed to continued focus on process, staff development and reform by State Government resulting in streamlined assessment pathways.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: While this result is below the set target, there was an overall decrease in the time taken to decide applications.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: There has been a 58% increase in council planning decisions upheld at VCAT since last year. This may be attributed to fewer appeals overall being made to the Tribunal than previous years, including proportionately fewer appeals against Council’s failure to grant a permit and appeals against permit conditions.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Missed kerbside collection bins have increased by 202% compared to last year. This rise is largely attributed to changes in project activities and reporting mechanisms. Although Quarters 1 and 4 recorded relatively low volumes of missed collections, they were still higher than previous years. In Quarter 2, Casey relied on raw data from a contractor's bin container audit, which was not fully validated due to limited resources to verify whether bins were presented on time. Quarter 3 also used raw data, following a change in how Casey’s waste management team handled reporting. The overall results remain in the expected range

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Result is in the expected range.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The cost of the kerbside recyclables collection service has decreased by 23% compared to last year. This reduction is primarily due to the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme (CDS) in November 2024, which led to a lower volume of materials being collected—resulting in cost savings. In addition, revenue from CDS eligible containers found in kerbside bins has increased, with the council receiving 50 per cent of that income.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Non-current borrowings have decreased significantly during the year, falling by 36.8%, which has contributed to a lower ratio. Unless Council enters into a new long-term borrowing arrangement, this ratio is expected to continue declining due to the anticipated absence of non-current borrowings in the 2026/27 and 2027/28 financial years. However, a rebound in the ratio is projected for 2028/29, driven by the renewal of lease arrangements and the ongoing growth in employee provisions..

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: At City of Casey, the asset renewal ratio should be viewed in light of its relatively young, long-life asset base, much of which was constructed in the past 30 years. While current renewal needs are low, Casey actively models future requirements and will progressively shift funding from new infrastructure to asset renewal as growth stabilises. The present ratio aligns with community expectations for asset upkeep.

Northcote Aquatic and Recreation Centre (NARC) represents 1,311,615 (76%) of total visitation while RLC reflects 423,359 (24%) of total visitation. This is a significant increase on 2023-24 as NARC's 2024-25 data represents a full year of operation.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: This result improved significantly in 2024/25 due to Northcote Aquatic and Recreation Centre (NARC) operating for the full financial year. NARC contributed significant revenue to Council (including additional profit share for the 2023/24 financial year) and higher than expected visitation. Reservoir Leisure Centre’s visitation and expenditure remained steady. These factors resulted in a combined profit of $1,733,585 for the two centres and almost double the number of visits.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Variance increase is due to staff movement with an increase in complex matters and customer request expectations.

description: Animals reclaimed

description: Animals rehomed
variation: wat djerring, the Animal Welfare Facility, have an adoption program and have partnered with other rescue organisations to successfully rehome collected animals.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: There has been a 36% increase in the cost of animal management services since last year this is due to increase of operating costs at wat djerring Animal Facility

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: One case was heard in court relating to a charge of causing serious injury. This is consistent with previous years.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Council's consistent and high service levels continued with over 96% of premises assessed.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: There has been a 11% decrease in the cost of the food safety service since last year, which is an improvement to overall cost in service delivery of $59 per premises.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Council has demonstrated its commitment to addressing non-compliance in food safety by continuously achieving 100% for this indicator.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Council has exceeded the amount of food samples required and delivered a result of over 100%.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Satisfaction with Council’s community consultation and engagement performance has remained stable.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: The attendance rate of Councillors at meetings has remained stable.

variation: There has been a 22% increase in cost of elected representation since last year. This is due to the new mandatory training and induction programs required for new councillors.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The community satisfaction rating improved slightly in comparison with last year.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Capital investment in library collections has remained static which limited the capacity to renew and improve the age of the collection. Continued collection maintenance work will improve the collection age by withdrawing and disposing of older material which have not been borrowed.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Library service expenditure has remained stable, with the increase largely due to CPI and Enterprise Agreement increases.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Physical and digital collections continue to be popular with an overall increase of 38,385 loans compared to the previous year. Physical collection loans are stable and represent 76% of loans. Consolidating digital collections on one platform has increased the use of digital collections, with eAudiobooks the most popular.

description: Library membership
variation: The increase in active membership compared to the previous year may be due to promotional campaigns and increased programs and events which attract new customers.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Visitation increased by 56,099 visits compared to 2023/24, an increase of 11%. This is due to a strategic approach to increase program and event delivery for our community.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: All babies born in Darebin are enrolled in the Maternal and Child Health service and provided with a home visit.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Additional grant funding has led to the service employing additional Maternal and Child health nurses

variation: The service received an increased number of referrals to assess refugee children and babies.

variation: The Maternal and Child health service has developed relationships with the Mercy Mother Baby hospital and Aboriginal services to better support families, children and babies.

variation: The quantity of community requests fluctuates from year to year. description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Council continues to maintain 568km of sealed roads to condition standards, this is 94.13% of sealed roads are within the intervention level and meeting the target range.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The low figure represents spend to date and is due to timing and delivery of two construction projects that will be completed in 2025/26 FY.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: There has been a small decrease in the cost of sealed local road resealing since last year. This is explained by improved contract and project management practices.

variation: Slight decrease in satisfaction from last year, due to several variables including increased traffic and road surface deterioration.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The time taken to decide applications has continued to reduce due to a range of continuous improvement initiatives.

description: Cost of statutory planning service

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT

variation: There has been a slight reduction in cost due to less green waste being processed.

variation: The recycling rate was lower partly because dry autumn weather reduced food and green waste collection, as less plant growth led to a smaller proportion of recyclable material compared to garbage.

variation: Unrestricted cash was impacted by higher holdings of term deposits with a maturity of > than 90 days ($41.9 million) which are classified as other financial assets. Actual result would have been 104% if these amounts were included.

variation: Result is in-line with expected results. Loan servicing in-line with repayment schedules.

variation: Result impacted by delays in capital projects and capital expenditure of $3.91 million not being included as renewal as it was classified as maintenance or not capitalised in accordance with Council's asset capitalisation policy. variation: Current year result was impacted by increased depreciation, receipt of Commonwealth financial assistance grant advance payment ($2.7 million). variation: No material variation from the previous year

variation: Current year results were impacted by receipt of Commonwealth financial assistance grant advance payment ($2.7 million). The Commonwealth financial assistance grant for 2025-26 was paid in advance in June, 2025.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The index (expressed as a decile) for Socio Economic Index for Areas (SEIFA) published by the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) measures the relative advantages and/or disadvantages of the area based on a range of Census characteristics. The index for Darebin at year end is consistent to prior years.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Total turnover for the 2024-25 year is within the expected range and is slightly lower than the previous year.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: All aquatic facilities were inspected by an authorised officer during the reporting period. Aquatics staff also assessed water quality five times daily, with records of results maintained at all locations.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Recreation centre utilisation continued to grow over the reporting period, with record patronage levels recorded. Utilisation data includes the Bairnsdale Aquatic Recreation centre, Bairnsdale outdoor pool, Lakes Entrance Aquadome and Orbost outdoor pool.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: This year the cost associated with aquatic facilities decreased slightly over the reporting period.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Response times improved over the previous year, driven by two key factors: the implementation of streamlined data management processes, and operational adjustments during the Animal Registration Renewal period. While quarter four typically experiences delays due to increased customer requests, these improvements contributed to more efficient handling of enquiries across the year.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: The year-to-date trend shows a decline in the proportion of registrable animals being reclaimed, falling below 25%. Contributing factors include broader economic pressures, with some owners choosing not to reclaim or adopt animals due to financial constraints. This challenge is consistent with trends reported by animal welfare agencies across the sector. Council continues to actively promote responsible pet ownership and animal adoption.

description: Animals rehomed
variation:
We have observed a growing number of surrendered animals that are difficult to rehome—particularly older animals, those with health issues, and animals displaying aggressive behaviours. Post-COVID socialisation gaps have contributed to behavioural challenges that further limit rehoming options. 
Economic pressures also appear to be contributing to the surrender or abandonment of animals with health issues, compounding the difficulty of finding suitable homes.
Despite these challenges, rehoming figures for the year remained within the target range, reflecting ongoing efforts to promote responsible pet ownership and support animal welfare outcomes.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The overall cost of animal management services has increased, primarily due to higher expenses associated with operating the animal shelter (Pounds) service.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation:
There were no Court prosecutions for the year. Note that in many cases infringements for animal related offences have been issued. 
Court prosecutions are restricted to the most serious offences, where the public interest is served by a Court appearance and there are witnesses willing to attend Court to give evidence.
This year there were several incidents that warrant a court prosecution but the delays in the court system mean that they will appear in the next financial year's results.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: A total of 31 food complaints were received during 2024. All complaints were investigated within 1 to 3 days.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: 386 of 387 registered premises received annual inspections during the 2024 calendar year. The total number of inspections conducted was 400, but due to some businesses since closing, they have not been included in the total calculation.

description: Cost of food safety service
Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: A total of 10 critical or major non-compliances, 9 were followed up in 2024 calendar year. The 1 non-compliance that was not followed up related to a low-risk mobile food premises and was identified by another council. The non-compliance was rectified at the time of the inspection. A food safety inspection has since been conducted, and the premises was found to be compliant.

description: Food safety samples
variation: In accordance with Section 32A of the Food Act 1994, 104 food safety samples were required to be obtained and analysed during the 2024 calendar year. Council collected 106 samples during the year, exceeding the requirement.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation:
Council made 104 resolutions over the reporting period. Of these, 12 were confidential resolutions made at meetings closed to the public.
Council is committed to making decisions in the public arena wherever possible and is working diligently to reduce the number of decisions made in closed meetings.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement has increased over the reporting period. Council is continues to review its consultation and engagement practices in an effort to improve the community satisfaction rating.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance at Council meetings remains high as Councillors are able to attend meetings online or face to face.

description: Cost of elected representation
Satisfaction with council decisions
variation:
Satisfaction with council decisions has increased over the reporting period.
Council is committed to making decisions in the community's best interest, and the slight increase in satisfaction reflects this.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: 51.69% of the library collection was purchased within the last five years, placing the result comfortably within the target range of 40% to 90%. This figure remains consistent with the result from the previous financial year, indicating ongoing stability in the age profile of the collection. We continue to maintain a balanced and strategic approach to collection development through regular purchasing and weeding to ensure relevance, freshness, and alignment with community needs.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Cost of library service increased slightly during the financial year.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Library loans per population have increased slightly this year. Consistent engagement across all quarters reflects the effectiveness of ongoing initiatives such as targeted marketing campaigns and the introduction of automatic renewals for overdue items. The results demonstrate strong community use of library services and confirm that the service is operating at a healthy level of utilisation relative to population.

description: Library membership
variation: Active Library membership calculation was previously calculated incorrectly, resulting in a lower than expected range for 2023-24. However, we have calculated correctly in 2024-25, which shows that we are now within the expected range of active library members.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The result is consistent with previous years and reflects stable, ongoing community engagement with library services. Although marketing and outreach activities have been increased over the past 12 months with the aim of further boosting visitation, the trend remains steady. Continued efforts to promote programs, services, and spaces, including both physical branches and mobile libraries, will support future opportunities to grow library usage across the municipality.

variation: The cost of sealed road reconstruction can vary from year to year depending on materials used and the treatment required. Costs were also impacted by the trial of more environmentally friendly products.

variation: The cost of sealed road resealing can vary from year to year depending on materials used and the treatment required. Costs were also impacted by the trial of more environmentally friendly products.

variation: During the reporting period, 13 Council planning decisions were appealed to VCAT, with five being set aside.

Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Council continues to focus on reducing response time for all animal management requests. High risk animal management requests such as dog attacks and dog wandering at large continue to be prioritised, in terms of response and action, above other animal management request categories.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Animals reclaimed remains consistent with the previous year. Council continues to promote to the community the benefits of animal registration and the ability to reunite animals back with their owners.

variation: Animals rehomed remains consistent with the previous year.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Council continues to explore opportunities in reducing service cost through prioritising return home when possible. Resourcing vacancies have also reduced the cost of services this financial year.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council is active in the prosecution space, and will utilise its legislative options, when appropriate, under the Domestic Animals Act 1994.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation:
To effectively manage food safety risks, the Environmental Health team continues to prioritise all food safety complaints.
This approach reflects Council's ongoing commitment to public health and safety.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: All registered food premises received an annual food safety assessment, further demonstrating Council's dedication to maintaining high food safety standards.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation:
The cost of food service delivery saw a slight reduction compared to the previous year. 

This is attributed to service delivery efficiencies, a vacant position, and a growing number of food businesses operating in the municipality.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation:
Critical and major non-compliance outcomes are a high priority.

Environmental Health Officers ensured all critical and major non-compliance outcomes raised at food premises were followed up as a priority demonstrated by the 100 percent result. 

A total of 96 follow ups were completed, demonstrating a significant drop from the previous year, and highlighting the Environmental Health team's ongoing commitment to food safety.

description: Food safety samples
variation:
In accordance with section 32 of the Food Act 1984, Frankston City Council was required to collect and submit 166 food samples for analysis in 2024. This included 55 samples from Class 1 food premises, and 111 samples from Class 2, Class 3A, or Class 3 premises.

Frankston City Council exceeded its sampling requirement by submitting a total of 174 food samples. Of these, 59 were obtained from Class 1 premises and 115 from Class 2, Class 3A, or Class 3 premises.

This reflects Frankston City Council’s proactive approach and strong commitment to food safety compliance.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council decisions made in meetings closed to the public have steadily reduced since 2020, demonstrating transparency in Council’s decision making process.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: There has been a marginal increase in community satisfaction with Consultation and Engagement in the 2025 Annual Community Satisfaction Survey, with satisfaction rising to 7.0 out of 10, retaining a “good” rating and marking the equal highest result for Frankston City. This included 39% of respondents being “very satisfied” and only 8% “dissatisfied” (down from 10%).

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillors have attended Council Meetings consistently which demonstrates a high commitment to their decision making role.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: Councillor allowance values increased in 2024-2025 as a result of the Victorian Remuneration Tribunal determination.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Satisfaction with council decisions is stable and has risen significantly since 2021.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Work has continued all year to purchase new items to refresh the library collection, which has resulted in a total increase in the number of items purchased in the last five years. An increase in Capital works funding has supported the renewal of the library collection for the whole community to offer entertainment, knowledge, education and cultural enrichment.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The direct cost of the library service per population continued to track well, to provide library services to the community.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The library collection saw an increase in loans this year for library loans per population.

description: Library membership
variation:
21% of the community are an active library member. The decrease in active members results from last year to this year is a result of the realignment of the indicator calculation. This does not include people who make use of the library without using their library card, families who borrow using one family member card, spend time reading the newspaper, accessing the Wi-fi or public computers, or attending a library event which people do not need to register for. 
Active members have increased year on year to 19583, up from 17200 the previous year.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The library visits to Frankston, Carrum Downs and Seaford libraries reached almost 260,000 visits this year. Frankston City Libraries also saw 201,000 visits to the library website.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Frankston City Council MCH Service has achieved 100 percent infant enrollments. This highlights highly effective engagement of families into the MCH Service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Frankston City Council is committed in delivering a high quality, cost-efficient MCH Service. Although the cost of delivering the MCH service has increased, it remains at a manageable level and does not currently pose a concern for service sustainability.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: The percentage of enrolled children participating in the MCH service has declined slightly compared to the previous financial year. This may be attributed to limited workforce capacity and an increase in parents returning to work.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children has slightly declined, although it remains high. This expected decrease is linked to the end of fixed-term funding for an Aboriginal MCH Liaison Nurse and the opening of First Peoples' Health and Wellbeing MCH Service. Frankston City Council maintains strong partnerships with Nairm Marr Djambana Aboriginal Gathering Place and First People's Health and Wellbeing (Frankston) to empower community self-determination in accessing maternal and child health services.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Frankston City Council MCH Service has had a slight decrease in the percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service who participated in 4-week Key Ages and Stages (KAS) visits. This result may reflect an increase in families receiving care from independent midwives through the first six weeks postnatally, leading to delayed engagement with MCH services.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The sealed local road network of Frankston City Council has had a marginal decrease this year, attributed to the declaration of Hall Road as an arterial road and other minor residential and industrial gifted assets.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The standard of the road network has decreased from last year however it is still within expectations. Through its Long Term Infrastructure Plan (LTIP) and in accordance with its Asset Management Policy, Council continues to invest in the sealed local road network to ensure roads stay above renewal intervention levels.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The cost of reconstruction has slightly increased due to the use of more traditional reconstruction methodologies in some projects due to suitability. These methodologies are generally more costly to undertake.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The rates of local road resealing have risen due to Council's commitment to utilise more sustainable options in resurfacing. This included recycled content and specific treatments that are designed to ensure a longer asset life from the road, resulting in a lower lifecycle cost.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Community satisfaction with the maintenance and repair of sealed local roads remained stable this year at 70 per cent in 2024-2025, which remains a “good” level of satisfaction. This is an excellent result and is 2 per cent higher than the metropolitan Council average. Additionally, this result was measurably (4%) higher than satisfaction with major arterial roads and highways managed by VicRoads.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The time taken to decide applications has decreased marginally in comparison to the previous financial year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The percentage of applications determined within statutory timeframes has improved from previous years demonstrating strong performance in meeting regulatory requirements.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost of the statutory planning service has decreased in comparison to the previous financial year.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: During the financial year there was a greater number of outcomes than usual where the VCAT technically ‘set aside’ Council’s decision.  However, the majority of these outcomes were the result of changes to the proposal and/or negotiated outcomes with third parties during VCAT processes, which Council both participated in and endorsed.  The percentage of outcomes where Council’s decision was directly overturned by the VCAT was 14% (meaning that the percentage of VCAT outcomes which aligned with Council’s position on modified proposals was 86%).

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: A continued high level of service provided throughout the Municipality with in-vehicle technology improving accuracy and efficiency of the service.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost of the garbage service per collection has increased, mainly due to the increase in EPA landfill levy charges.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The increase in associated costs of collection and processing of recyclables with the introduction of the separate glass collection service has resulted in the overall reduction in cost per bin service. This is mainly due to the significant increase in total number of kerbside recyclables bins collected along with benefits from the introduction of the container deposit scheme.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Councils landfill diversion rate has decreased overall. The garbage tonnes remained consistent compared to the previous year, however, there has been a significant reduction in diverted tonnes collected due to the introduction of the container deposit scheme and lower garden organic tonnes generated.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Expenses have risen across Council operations and services largely impacted by inflationary pressures across the business.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Council’s general rate income has been increased in compliance with the rate cap of 2.75 per cent set by the Victorian Government. The increase is expected to remain stable over the forecast period due to rate capping.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: This underlying deficit indicates the true cost of service delivery and reflects the long-term impact of rate capping, the challenging economic environment and inflationary pressure on materials and services. The result has improved in 2024-25 as the organisation has been focused on cost control and more efficient service delivery to ensure long term sustainability. Council also received an early payment of 50% of the 2025-26 Financial Assistance Grants which has contributed to the improved underlying result.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: 730 animal management requests were recorded in 2024/25. The average response time from when a request was received to the first response action was one (1) day.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: A positive increase in animals reclaimed was noted with 32 animals reclaimed in 2023/24, increasing to 52 in 2024/25. Noting the reclaiming program is not under Council's direct control. Council has an agreement with two animal shelters and once an animal is transferred to a shelter, the responsibility for reclaiming shifts to that facility.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: A positive increase in unclaimed collected animals rehomed was noted with 26 animals rehomed in 2023/24, increasing to 47 in 2024/25. Noting the rehoming program is not under Council's direct control. Council has an agreement with two animal shelters and once an animal is transferred to a shelter, the responsibility for rehoming shifts to that facility. Both facilities undertake programs across the year to support the rehoming of unclaimed collected animals.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The cost of the animal management service per population has decreased when compared to 2023/24 actuals.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Two (2) prosecutions occurred across the reporting period, with one (1) successful prosecution recorded.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Four (4) food complaints were recorded in the 2024 calendar year.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: The 2024 calendar year notes that 100 annual food safety assessments were completed on registered class 1 and class 2 food premises across 116 premises.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The direct cost of food safety services is variable and contingent upon the number of registered or notified food premises. The number of Class 2, 3 and 4 registered or notified food premises fluctuates annually. 330 premises were registered as of the 30 June 2025. For the 2023/24 financial year a detailed audit of this indicator, including increased officer costs, has been completed to accurately represent the delivery of food safety services. The actuals should represent $399.99 (2023/24).

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Seven (7) critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications about food premises were recorded within the reporting period, with all requests followed up in the 2024 calendar year.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Food samples are collected in accordance with the Victoria Government Gazette Declaration under Section 32A for Food Sampling Requirements. 32 of the required 31 food samples were obtained across the 2024 calendar year.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Decrease is reflected in a 40% reduction in resolutions made during Council meetings closed to the public, dropping from 10 in 2023/24 to 6 in 2024/25. Additionally, there was a 6.30% decrease in the number of Council resolutions made during meetings of the Council or delegated committees comprising only Councillors dropping from 127 in 2023/24 to 119 in 2024/25.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Satisfaction with community consultations and engagement show a decline when compared to 2023/24 actuals. These results are obtained via the Local Government Community Satisfaction Survey, conducted by the Department of Government Services on behalf of Council. While the identified target has not been met, Council will take action to improve future outcomes, with an increased focus on targeted and varied communication methodologies and more opportunities to listen to the community.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: An increase in the number of Councillors who attended each Council meeting was recorded. A total attendance of 112 was recorded in 2024/25, compared to 83 in 2023/24. The number of Council meetings additionally increased, with 17 meetings held in 2024/25, increasing from 13 meetings in 2023/24. In accordance with guidance materials, calculations for this indicator account for Councillors who were on an approved leave of absence or noted as an approved apology.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: A 4.73% decrease for the direct cost of delivering Council’s governance service per Councillor has been noted when compared to 2023/24 actuals.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Satisfaction with decisions made in the interest of the community performance remain consistent from the 2023/24 actuals, with a moderate decline noted.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Actual is represented by a slight decrease in library collection items purchased in the last 5 years when compared to previous years results.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Cost per population remained consistent across the 2024/25 reporting period, noting a 2.84% increase in the direct cost of library service.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Library loans per capita remains steady, with a slight decrease of 1.72% noted when compared to previous years results.

description: Library membership
variation: A total of 4,294 registered library members were recorded at the end of the reporting period, a 7.40% increase from 2023/24 actuals.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: A total of 43,609 library visits were recorded, a 4.72% increase when compared to previous years results.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Indicator captures the number of new enrolments in the 12-month period of which most are new births.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Improvements in CDIS data entry accessibility, training and review has impacted the result favourably. Additional funding was provided to extend Key Age and Stage service capacity for vulnerable cohorts which led to adjustments to program delivery.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Not all children within the municipality have scheduled Maternal and Child Health appointments in a 12-month period, appointments are dependent on age. 2024/25 actuals continue to reflect consistently with 2023/24 results.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children may fluctuate due to engagement of other support services. 2024/25 actuals reflect the children attended Maternal and Child Health service at least once.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: A decrease in sealed local road maintained to condition standards when compared to 2023/24 data has been detailed. Actuals have decreased from 100% to 97.76% as reflected in the most recent road condition assessment report.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: This indicator pertains to road reconstruction projects. It encompasses tendered projects, road widening works and ancillary works such as drainage, kerb and channel and guardrails. The nature of these works varies each year in both area and scope, resulting in cost fluctuations. An increase in actual costs is indicative of rising expenses associated with the projects undertaken during the reporting period.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: An additional 118,176 square metres of sealed local road was resealed in 2024/25. Considering the increase, the cost of sealed local road resealing remained consistent when compared to 2023/24 results.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads indicates an increase from 2023/24 actuals.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: A positive decrease in this indicator has been recorded, attributed by improvements in internal processes, a reduction in the number of applications, and a State Government grant to support report writing have all enhanced the department's capacity to make quicker decisions.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: A positive increase in this indicator has been recorded. Attributed by improvements in internal processes, a reduction in the number of applications and a State Government grant to support report writing have all enhanced the department's capacity to make quicker decisions and resulting in this indicator exceeding the identified target. Following a detailed audit of this indicator in 2023/24 actuals should represent 62.25% (2021/22) and 46.50% (2022/23).

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: A recalculation of officer costs for 2024/25 has been completed utilising a revised costing methodology to more accurately represent the expenses associated with the statutory planning service. This update has resulted in an overall increase in costs.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Nine (9) out of ten (10) decisions were upheld by VCAT across the reporting period.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed

variation: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service remains consistent from the 2023/24 actuals, with a moderate increase in the direct cost of the service per kerbside garbage collection bin noted.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: With the introduction of a glass only collection service and the reduction of recycling collection frequency to fortnightly in February 2025, the cost of collecting and disposing of recyclables has reduced.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The decrease in this indicator is explained by the reduction of collection frequency of the recycling bins from weekly to fortnightly since February 2025 and the on-going impact of the Container Deposit Scheme. The weight of all kerbside collections has also decreased.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
The average cost per property has increased by $156 (4%) compared to the previous year, consistent with historical annual increases.
This uplift reflects a 3.5% Enterprise Agreement (EA) adjustment for staff, a 2.9% rise in the Consumer Price Index (CPI), and additional costs linked to initiatives such as the Bendigo Regional Employment Precinct (a fully funded project).

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation:
Council adhered to the Minister's rate cap of 2.75%. Additional rate revenue was due to supplementary valuations being issued during the year.
The 2024/25 rates issued included 34% of properties who's annual rates decreased on prior year and a further 27% who were less than or equal to the minister's rate cap.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: In the final week of June, the City received $11.6 million prepayment from the federal government under the Financial Assistance Grants program.  The irregular timing of these payments over recent years has affected the City's working capital, making year on year comparisons challenging.  Without this prepayments, the city's working capital would have been 167%

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The City's unrestricted cash increased substantially on prior year, notably due to the prepayment of the Financial Assistance Grants (received in the final week of June).

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation:
During the 2024/25 financial year, the City reduced its loan balance by $5 million and fully repaid one outstanding loan.
The City's total loan portfolio now stands at $9.96 million, with further borrowings planned in future years to support multigenerational
infrastructure projects.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The City reduced its loan balance during the 2024/25 financial year, resulting in a lower debt-to rate revenue ratio.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation:
The City's indebtedness is materially impacted by the right of use asset for Galkangu (long term lease), increasing the City's non current liabilities balance.
The City's capacity to borrow is not taken into account in this indicator, however remains as an important factor when assessing the City's financial position.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Of the $56 million invested in capital works, $35 million (63%) was allocated to renewal projects, consistent with previous years. However, a rise in depreciation has led to a decline in the renewal ratio indicator.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
The timing of the financial assistance grant payment has had a notable influence on the City's adjusted underlying result. In June 2025, the City received a 50% prepayment of the 2025/6 grant, which materially affected the reported outcome for the financial year.
Additionally, when comparing actuals to budget, several projects - such as the Bendigo Regional Employment Precinct and the Tramways Workshop - were completed during 2024/25. These projects were fully funded and although originally budgeted as capital works, these projects
were undertaken on behalf of external entities and, in accordance with accounting standards, have been expensed through the income statement.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation:
The proportion of the City's rates relative to adjusted underlying revenue has returned to levels consistent with those seen prior to 2024. In 2023/24, this indicator rose due to the absence of the financial assistance grant prepayment, resulting in rates comprising of a larger share of total revenue. By contrast, the 2024/25 result reflects a
prepayment of the grant which aligns with the pattern observed in previous years.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Result is in line with prior years with no substantial increases in capital improved value across the municipality.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Expenses per head of population has increased by 4% which reflects both an increase in staffing costs following EBA negotiations, as well as CPI increases on materials and services. Additionally, there are costs associated with Bendigo Regional Employment Precinct, Flood Recovery and other funded programs.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation:
During 2024/25, the City undertook a revaluation of its two largest asset
classes (infrastructure and land and buildings). This together with
clarifications to accounting standards has resulted in a material increase to
the value of assets across the municipality.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: This indicator has shown a slight increase compared to previous years, while remaining broadly consistent overall. It is expected to trend upward in the coming years driven by the City's ongoing housing initiatives.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation:
The City's own-source revenue has grown by 5%, partially offset by a 1.3%
increase in population. This growth is primarily driven by rate adjustments
within the legislated cap and higher user fee income.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation:
The timing of the Financial Assistance Grant has fluctuated significantly in
recent years, resulting in notable impacts on this indicator. In 2024/25, the
City received a 50% prepayment of the 2025/26 grant during the final week
of June. This approach mirrors the pattern observed in 2022/23 and prior
and as a result, the indicator has returned to the same level recorded during that period.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation:

variation: The minor increase in cost to deliver the service is reflective of cost increases across labour, materials and services.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: We continue to successfully prosecute all matters brought before the Court, with eight animal management prosecutions in 2024−25.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The increase in time taken to action food complaints was primarily due to a rise in food premises complaints received (up from 163 in 2023 to 203). This higher than usual variation impacted the speed of our response. Despite this, complaints are being addressed within an average of two days, which remains well within the expected timeframe.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Less employee vacancies resulted in more premises being attended and a higher number of food safety assessments completed. A total of 1,107 of 1,497 assessments required for Class 1 and 2 businesses were completed.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Service costs have risen compared to previous years, mainly due to higher levels of staffing and increased fees linked to the cost of living.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Less staff vacancies enabled more re-inspections with all 218 critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications followed up (213 in 2024 and 5 from 2023).

description: Food safety samples
variation: Less employee vacancies resulted in more samples being collected. A total of 422 Class 1, 2 & 3 samples were collected, exceeding the required number (406 samples).

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: The slight increase in Council resolutions made in closed meetings is largely due to tender reports now being considered confidentially.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: While community satisfaction with consultation and engagement has seen a decline over time, it is consistent with results seen for both Regional Centres and the state average. Community feedback highlights the ongoing need for timely and meaningful opportunities to provide input, particularly face-to-face engagement and greater transparency in how feedback is used and decisions are made.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance increased in 2024−25, with just two apologies and four approved absences.  As per the Better Practice Guide, approved absences are counted as having attended a council meeting.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The increase in cost is in line with the incremental increase of Councillor Allowances in accordance with Section 39 of the Local Government Act 2020 (Vic).

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Community satisfaction with council decisions is a complex issue influenced by various factors. While there was a marginal increase in the satisfaction with decision making, community feedback indicates the continued importance to provide timely communication, consultation opportunities and transparency with residents around Council's decision making processes.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: A new collection was purchased for the Biyal-a Armstrong Creek Library.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The opening of the new Biyal-a Armstrong Creek Library increased the direct cost of the library service. Included in the cost (for this year only) is a contribution of $903,058 towards the initial purchase of books at Biyal-a Armstrong Creek. The indicator would be $43.50 without this being taken into consideration.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: City of Greater Geelong services reported a 15 per cent increase in loans when compared to the previous financial year. The majority of the growth comes from the opening of the new Biyal-a Armstrong Creek Library in August 2024.

description: Library membership
variation: City of Greater Geelong services reported a 12 per cent increase in the number of active library members compared to the same period last year, following the opening of the new Biyal-a Armstrong Creek Library.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: City of Greater Geelong services reported a 14 per cent increase in visits when compared to the same period last year, following the opening of the new Biyal-a Armstrong Creek Library.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: The percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service was comparable with previous years.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The City's MCH service delivery costs returned to previous levels after increasing in 2023−24.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation in the City's MCH service remained relatively unchanged. There were 9,930 children enrolled in the City’s MCH service of which 9,796 attended at least once in the year.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: There were 236 children who identify as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander (ATSI) enrolled in the City’s MCH service, of which 174 attended at least once in the year.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation in the 4-week Key Age and Stage visit was comparable with previous years.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Requests were consistent with previous years following the sharp increase in 2022−23 due to adverse weather events.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Council maintains the majority of its road network in accordance with established condition standards. The current target represents the proportion of roads that are classified as within excellent to fair condition. The City will be pursuing a future target where by 97 per cent would fall within this excellent to fair condition range.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The higher cost of the sealed road reconstruction results from significant costs in sub base (road pavement) and kerb and channel projects in two large residential reconstruction projects as part of the City’s Roads to Recovery program.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The higher cost associated with sealed road projects was primarily due to a significant amount of resealing being carried out using asphalt, which incurs substantially greater expense compared to spray seal methods.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Despite asset data indicating that the majority of City-managed sealed local roads meet established condition standards, community satisfaction remains low. We believe confusion regarding the distinction between roads managed by the City and those overseen by the state government does impact results however we acknowledge this is a consistent year on year issue and therefore does not impact trends. Additionally, recent state and local government infrastructure projects have necessitated road closures, imposing detours throughout our municipality, potentially affecting user satisfaction as necessary upgrades progress to support the City's ongoing growth.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The average decision time for planning applications was 92 days, similar to previous years.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Council achieved its target for the percentage of planning application decisions made within legislated time frames, despite a minor drop due to temporary employee shortages.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: This year's figure more accurately reflects the direct service cost, as previous calculations included unrelated expenses identified through benchmarking other councils.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: In 2024−25, Geelong had the second-highest number of planning applications in Victoria with 1,343 submissions. Fewer than 1 per cent (nine cases) were referred to a full hearing at VCAT, with five of those cases overturned. Since so few cases reach VCAT, any changes can significantly impact percentage results.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: The proportion of missed bins remained relatively constant, even as population and population density increased.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: A slight increase in the amount of waste collected led to higher disposal costs. Fuel prices, a significant cost for waste collection vehicles, have been rising, adding further pressure to service pricing.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: Reduced disposal fees charged by the waste facilities contributed to a slight decrease in cost of the City’s kerbside recyclables collection. Tonnages of recyclables have stabilised following the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme and improvements in consumer packaging.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The slight decrease in the diversion rate can be attributed to decreases in green organics and recyclable materials while general waste increased. The result was within range of the projected target.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The rise in expense per property assessment reflects the ongoing population growth in the region and the number of new rateable properties, while expenditure has also increased due to a significant year on year increase in depreciation that reflects improved asset data through the City's comprehensive audit and revaluation process and the significant volume and value of developer contributed infrastructure assets transferred annually for the City to maintain.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The average rate per property typically grows in line with the annual rate cap sent by the State Government.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The increase in the City's working capital ratio reflects an increase in cash held in term deposits as a result of delays in delivering the City's planned capital program for the year.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Council have allocated cash to term deposits in order to maximise the return to Council funds. The term deposits are reported as Other Financial Assets and the total of $115.0 million at 30 June 2025. If the term deposits were considered Cash and Cash Equivalents, then the Unrestricted cash ratio would have been 80.11%

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Loans and borrowings reduced with the repayment of a $10 million interest only loan in 2024−25. The City is committed to reducing borrowings to more sustainable levels over the next four years by repayment of loans using proceeds from sale of surplus properties.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The City repaid $21 million in loans and borrowings in 2024−25 contributing to the increase in the result.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Non-current liabilities reduced by $8.4 million, the improvement reflecting the City's commitment to repay debt over the next four years.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The City invested $67.7 million in asset renewal and upgrades in 2024−25, with delays in the delivery of the remaining upgrade program of $31.43 million to be spent in the following year. The City is committed to an 85 per cent renewal target in the Long-Term Financial Plan.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Financial operational performance in 2024−25 improved overall, with material variances including $15m early receipt of 2025−26 Financial Assistance Grants offset by loss on the disposal of assets $4.1 million unfavourable to budget.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The result reflects significant increase in adjusted underlying revenue due to timing of the receipt of the Federal Government's Financial Assistance Grants relating to 2023−24 and 2025−26 being received in the 2024−25 financial year.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Rates revenue and Capital Improved Value increases have remained stable.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The expense relative to population has remained stable.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The value of infrastructure assets has increased primarily due to growth in subdivision assets.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: We add to our local road network every year to meet the needs of growing population. In 2024−25 the increase in length of local roads was slightly less than the increase in population.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The own source revenue relative to population has remained stable.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: This result reflects the timing of receipt of the Federal Government's Financial Assistance Grant funding relating to 100% of 2024−25 and 50% of 2025−26 being received in the 2024−25 financial year.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Greater Geelong's Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage Index from the 2021 Census was in the sixth decile. The first decile indicates the most disadvantaged and the tenth decile indicates the least disadvantaged. We acknowledge that there are areas experiencing significant disadvantage and others with low disadvantage within the municipality.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Despite a slight increase from the previous year, the City’s turnover rate remains well below the Victorian Public Service turnover rate of 16.7 per cent in 2024.

Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The increased utilisation of aquatic facilities continued this year with 30,000 more visits than 2023-24.
