description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: There has been a significant improvement in planning applications being decided within timeframes due to State Government funding for flood affected Councils. This funding was until June 2025. This funding has helped reduce the backlog of applications through the allocation of additional resources and enabled more timely processing of new applications.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: There has been a significant increase in statutory planning costs in 2024-25 mainly due to an increase in consultants and legal expenses (this is due to more applications going to VCAT and some to the Supreme Court). The additional consulting costs have been offset by a grant and this income is not reflected in the calculation. Overall, there have been fewer planning applications in 2024-25 compared with 2023-24.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: In 2024-25 there were 3 out of 5 Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT compared to 2 out of 3 in 2023-24.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: In 2024-25, there was an increase in the number of missed bins due to the transition to the new kerbside collection contract. The number of reported missed bins declined over the first 3 months of the contract as routes were established.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: In 2024-25, there was a reduction of waste to landfill which contributed to a lower landfill disposal cost. The new kerbside collection contract has a more favourable financial impact for cost of service resulting in a markedly decreased cost of service for direct cost of kerbside garbage compared to 2023-24.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The new waste contract has a more favourable financial impact for cost of service resulting in a markedly decreased cost of service for direct cost of kerbside recyclables compared to 2023-24.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Marginal decrease in kerbside landfill diversion rate due to a reduction in total volume of collected recyclables likely due to greater utilisation of the container deposit scheme. Landfill waste from garbage has decreased as a result of the fortnightly frequency change.

description: Expenses per property assessment
Average rate per property assessment
Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Council's working capital target was 121%, based on 2024/25 Adopted Budget projections. The 2025 result exceeded this target mostly due to rebudgeted capital works projects, including the GV Link Stage 1 project. A full list of rebudgeted projects is available in the 2025/26 Adopted Budget. The 2025 result was favourably impacted by partial early receipt of the 2025/26 Federal Financial Assistance Grants received in advance (June 2025).

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The 2025 result is negative as this indicator excludes other financial assets (term deposits) that while not considered cash, are current assets with maturities that align with Council's current obligations. If financial assets are included, the result is 51%.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The reduction in loans and borrowings compared to rates reflects repayments during 2024/2025 and no new borrowings. Council has $9.68m borrowings budgeted in 2025/2026, for the GV Link Enterprise Park Stage 1 project.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Variability in forecast years is due to short-term interest only borrowings budgeted in 2025/2026. Repayment of these loans aligns with expected cash flows from the GV Link Enterprise Park Stage 1 project.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The target result reflected Council's 2024/2025 budgeted capital works and depreciation. Through the year, savings and project deferrals within Council's renewal and upgrade capital programs were identified, unfavourably impacting the indicator. Refer to note 2.1.2 of the 2024/2025 Financial Statements for performance against budget commentary and explanation of variances.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The 2025 result has been favourably impacted by Council receiving the Federal Financial Assistance Grants for the following year in advance in June. During the 2024 result year, Council received no Federal Financial Assistance Grants, having received the full allocation in advance in 2023. The 2026 forecast is favourably impacted by non-recurrent land sales, associated with Council's GV Link Enterprise Park Stage 1 Project.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The 2025 result is impacted by partial early receipt of the 2025/26 Federal Financial Assistance Grants received in advance (June 2025), increasing operating grant revenue and reducing rates revenue as a proportion of adjusted underlying revenue.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Mechanism to collect data was not always available throughout the season, has resulted in lower than anticipated visitation figures for the 2024/2025 Aquatics Season.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Cost efficiency measures to pool operations have resulted in a decrease in services costs.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Time taken to action animal management requests has remained consistent with the previous year, with slight reduction and improvement in time taken.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Increased number of animals reclaimed due to increased follow-up with pet owners to register their pets and update microchip details.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: The number of unclaimed collected animals has reduced from 2023/204 and from these there has been a higher number of unclaimed animals being rehomed in 2024/2025.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Overall reduction in cost of animal management service per population due to savings as a result of reduction of permanent and contractor staff.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council prosecutes as needed and on the legal advice from Council's lawyers. There was 1 successful animal management prosecution in 2024/2025.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: All food complaints were responded to and actioned within 1 business day this year.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Higher risk premises were prioritised. A slightly lower percentage compared to the previous year, but performance remains high. Changes to programs of work due to (1) staff transitioning (2) increased wastewater priorities due to property development and (3) increased enforcement such as food act prosecutions

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Marginal increase in direct cost of the food safety service due to increased resourcing with responsibilities shared across multiple officers.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: All critical compliance notifications were followed up. Change in non-major compliance notifications were due to notices being issued in December and then followed up in January of the next year.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Achieved all required food sampling and testing obligations

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: An increase on our previous year's results, Council continues to prioritise decisions being made in an open forum where possible. Resolutions made at confidential meetings are included in the Public Minutes where possible.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: There has been a reduction in satisfaction with community consultation from 2024. Throughout 24/25 there has been a vast program of Councillor and executive officer engagement with community, particularly during the Council plan development project. It is anticipated that this will impact positively on the next survey results.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Council continues to maintain a high attendance rate for Council meetings.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: New councillor term commenced November 2024 with average costs slightly reduced due to reduction in travel and other expenses to support the councillor group.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Community satisfaction with Council decisions has decreased in 2024/2025. Engagement campaigns and improved communications with the community are being undertaken to improve the satisfaction levels.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Result indicates regular updates of the collection and includes ebooks and audiobooks for the first time in 2024/2025.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Cost of the service has further decreased this financial year with a focus on cost reduction through greater efficiencies across the four libraries. Programs have continued and been well attended throughout the year.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Borrowings show an increase from last financial year. Loans of ebooks and audiobooks are included for the first time in 2024/2025.

description: Library membership
variation: Library membership remains high in Hepburn Shire. We are leading a campaign in 2025/26 to further boost membership along with the introduction of Open Libraries in 2025/2026.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Our libraries are popular destinations, with Daylesford Library being the busiest of our sites, with 36,327 visits during the financial year.  This good result will further improve as the Open Access Libraries Project is implemented.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: The percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service is per the number of birth notifications received.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Cost of service delivery result is similar to other rural Councils.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: The percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service who attend mCH has remained fairly constant. There is a drop off in 3-year-old key age and stage attendance as families will often attend GPs instead of MCH services.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Remains consistent with previous year and showing slight decrease in Aboriginal children attending over the four years as families choose to attend BADAC services in Ballarat for its dedicated cultural support service. Council has provided free cultural competency training to service providers and has seen a high uptake from Central Highland Rural health staff who are dedicated to maintaining a culturally safe service.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: The percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service who participated in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit has remained constant over the four-year period with 100% attendance in 2024/2025.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: A slight reduction of overall requests received due to targeted capital programs focused on sealed road defects.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Council continues to maintain road assets to a targeted condition.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Council had a limited amount of reconstruction projects in the year 2024/2025, but has equally experienced rising costs, particularly in relation to construction.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Council's program in 2024/2025 was reflective of spray seal works covering larger areas at lower unit rates than the previous year.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Slight increase in the performance rating this year for sealed local roads and is reflective of the poor state of roads in the region, not necessarily local roads.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: On average a reduction in permit decision times resulting from a review of process and targeted resources applied to the service.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: A turnover of staff in key roles over the last 18 months, coupled with a number of ongoing, complex planning enforcement and VCAT matters, has resulted in a challenging backlog of planning applications.   However, the percentage of applications decided within 60 statutory days is steadily improving, in response to vacancies being filled and the continuing implementation of targeted process improvements

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost per application reduced substantially and is within the target range. This reflects efficiencies gained through productivity improvements but also a reduction in the provision of specialist expert advice being required.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Council have had a reduction in numbers of VCAT matters going to VCAT over the last 12 months and are pleased with the all decisions being upheld at the tribunal.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Significant changes to bin collection schedules in 2023/2024 saw an increase in requests for missed bin collections. After the adjustment to changes, requests have returned to a lower level for the 2024/2025 period.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Decrease in cost due to collection frequency change from weekly to fortnightly and the introduction of FOGO kerbside collections from April 2024.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: Slight decrease to the cost of recycling processing.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Diversion increased with introduction of organics service in 2023/2024, as well as a change in general rubbish collection frequency from weekly to fortnightly.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The average expenditure per property has reduced due to operational efficiencies implemented for 2024/2025 and has been higher in previous years due to storm related expenditure. These operational efficiencies will continue into 2025/2026 as Council addresses its long term financial sustainability.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: This measure shows the continuing upward trend in average rates and reflects Council's commitment to maintain rate increases within the State Government imposed restrictions on rate revenue. Future years reflect the approved higher rate cap (7%) implemented for 2025/2026 that is in addition to the 3% standard rate cap award. Hepburn Council remains a lower rating Council compared to most other Councils.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: This measure is one reflection of Council's current financial position. The higher assets reflects the advanced receipt of the 2025/2026 Financial Assistance Grants in addition to the annual allocation of the 2024/2025 grants. No advanced payment was received in 2023/2024.  Further aided by new borrowings in 2024/2025 to support the capital works program.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: This measure is one reflection of Council's financial position. The increase from the prior year is due to the advance receipt of the 2025/2026 financial assistance grants in the current financial year with no advanced payment received in 2023/2024. The improvement is aided by Council's strategies that have been put in place to improve Council's financial sustainability which included operational efficiencies, and new borrowings in 2024/2025 to support the capital works program.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Interest bearing loans as a percentage of rate revenue increased this year as a new loan of $5.528m was drawn down.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: New loan commitments drawn down in late 2024/2025 with commencement of repayments in 2025/2026. This indicator is low compared to industry targets.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Non current liabilities as a percentage of Council's revenue from rates and finance has increased due to a previous commitment to draw down a new loan in the current year. Council is well within its capacity to meet its loan repayment obligations.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The result reflects the large capital program and the investment in renewal and upgrade projects along with an increase in depreciation charge.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Council is showing an improving and positive trend through the implementation of operational efficiencies in 2024/2025 to address the long term financial sustainability of Council that will continue into 2025/2026. Further complimented by the advance partial receipt of the financial assistance grants for 2025/2026.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Advanced payment of the financial assistance grants for 2025/2026 have been received in 2024/2025. This is in comparison to the previous year where no advanced payment was received.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Remains constant and reflects future commitments to maintain the financial sustainability of Council within the scope of State Government imposed restrictions on rate revenue.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Total expenses have reduced this year due to operational efficiencies implemented for 2024/2025 and were higher in previous years due to storm related expenditure. The operational efficiencies will continue into 2025/2026 as Council addresses it's long term financial sustainability.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The construction and renewal of assets combined with the revaluation of assets has increased the value of assets controlled by Council.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Remained constant

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Remained consistent and trending in a positive direction.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Advanced payment of the financial assistance grants for 2025/2026 have been received in 2024/2025. This is in comparison to the previous year where no advanced payment was received.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The socio-economic index summarises a range of information about the economic and social conditions of people and households within the shire. The decile has remained constant throughout the past four years.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Unplanned Employee turnover rate (reflected as resignations where the employees transitioned to a new workplace) was 15.73%. When the overall turnover is adjusted to include Planned Turnover (redundancies, terminations and retirements) the total annual turnover was 22.4%, a reduction of 1.0% on 2023-2024. The overall employee turnover rate is higher than would be preferred. Council’s turnover rate remains consistent with the industry average for a small rural shire and reflects the challenges of small rural councils in recruiting and retaining suitably qualified individuals.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council undertook an inspection at each of the four outdoor pools prior to the season commencing in November 2024.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: For the 2024/2025 outdoor pool season Council resolved to remove fees and have free pool entry. This has resulted in a significant increase in utilisation.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Outdoor swimming pools were previously operated by a contractor, for the 2024/2025 season Council directly operated the swimming pools.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Animal management requests are actioned within one business day.

description: Animals reclaimed

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation:
There has been a noticeable increase in participation in the Maternal and Child Health (MCH) service compared to previous years. This reflects the success of ongoing efforts to boost service engagement, including targeted outreach to childcare centres, kindergartens, and playgroups to connect with families and children who may have previously faced barriers to access.
Work is ongoing to re-engage families who have not yet participated. Strategies include follow-up on missed appointments, initiatives to improve customer service and service accessibility, and the promotion of new parenting and support groups—particularly aimed at supporting vulnerable families within the community.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Overall participation rates in the MCH service have increase, with a notable rise in engagement among Aboriginal children compared with the same time last year. This positive trend reflects the success of targeted efforts to support and engage Aboriginal families through culturally safe and appropriate practices. The increase in participation among Aboriginal children aligns closely with the overall growth in service engagement, highlighting the impact of inclusive and responsive service delivery.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Results have declined compared to the same time period last year. Several factors contribute to the decrease in participation in the 4week Key Ages and Stages (KAS) visit. These include infants who remain hospitalised after birth, families who opt for alternate services during the first 6-8weeks (such as private midwives), or those who temporarily reside outside of Hobsons Bay during the early postnatal period.

description: Sealed local road requests

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads

description: Time taken to decide planning applications

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill

The Container Deposit Scheme (CDS) has captured some of the recycling that would have previously been in the Mixed Recycling or Glass bins resulting in a downward trend for both these bins. In addition, residents continue to prefer to use the garbage bin for their food waste.

variation: The community have indicated a desire for increased communication and transparency when it comes to Council decision making. Result is in line with interface councils who perform lowest in this metric however is at the higher end for this group.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Council aims to maintain a collection target of 80% items purchased in the last five years. The age of collection has remained stable over the reporting period, this is indicative of a well-managed, contemporary public library collection.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation:
Modest reduction in cost of operating library service compared to last year, this is due to large capital investment 
in the 2023-24 reporting period.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The increase on figure from previous reporting period reflects an increase in outreach services over the last year, along with an increase in library membership and visitation.

description: Library membership
variation: The increase in this figure is in part due to increased library access points and outreach services in council growth areas.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Hume libraries include five physical libraries, two mobile vans and three Community Centre Library Kiosks, this indicator measures visits to physical library locations and vans, but not library kiosks.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: This result is greater than 100% due to the number of babies where Hume City Council do not receive a birth notice, yet the baby is enrolled in the service following notification by domiciliary services and families contacting MCH to advise they have relocated to Hume on discharge from maternity services

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The cost of the MCH service has seen a reduction in the 2024/25 year due to a number of impacting factors including a more accurate calculation (an increase) of staffing hours to reflect actual hours worked which includes additional hours work by the part time workforce.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: MCH participation rates have remained steady with only a slight decrease despite significant workforce pressures statewide. Service innovations including increased outreach to community settings, operating a waitlist and staff redirection to meet demand have ensured our community have been able to remain engaged.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Hume has a designated Aboriginal Engagement Team to provide a multidisciplinary approach to support Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, This includes home visits, centre visits and playgroup and have been effective in establishing relationships and trust within the community. Impacts of staff leave and workforce pressures have resulted in a slight decline in participation this year.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation rates and engagement in Maternal and Child Health Key Age and Stages visits remains a key focus as MCH services have adapted innovatively to the workforce shortages. Hume continues to see many families transfer into our catchment across all age groups resulting in greater than 100% for some KAS participation rates.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Council has continued with an elevated level of effort to improve the condition of roads.  This has alleviated the unanticipated effects caused by arterial road upgrades done as part of Victoria’s Big Build.  The result is a decrease in this service measure.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Resurfacing work that has occurred through the year has ensured that the proportion of roads outside intervention levels has remained within budgeted targets.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Results are higher than previous due to an increased amount of ancillary works including service relocations & full depth asphalt pavement required in some locations.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Results are higher than previous due to increase in preparation work required, larger proportion of works being asphalt rather than spray seal and increase in average asphalt thickness required, supplier and bitumen costs also continue to increase.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation:
Feedback from the community highlights the impact of roadworks, and repairs needed.
Council has continued with an elevated level of effort to improve the condition of roads.  This has alleviated the unanticipated effects caused by arterial road upgrades done as part of Victoria’s Big Build.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The reduction in processing days is a result of ongoing workload management and implementing a process to supply consistent data reporting to keep applications on track. Moving forward, continued focus on older applications will see this metric continue to improve in 2025/26.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Process improvements and consistent monthly data analysis meetings with department coordinators and management has ensured a focus on at risk applications can be maintained.  This has resulted in better application management and overall timeframes being reduced. Continued departmental reporting should see this metric continue to improve in 2025/26.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: Similarly to last financial year, this financial year has seen a lower number of applications lodged compared to 2020-2022 figures.  This has caused the cost per application to remain higher than in previous years as a result of the retention of existing staff to manage a lower application load.  It is noted that the cost of the service for 2024/25 is comparable with the cost of delivery of last years service.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Two decisions proceeded to a full VCAT hearing in 2024/25 from 766 applications, with both decisions overturning Council’s decision.  It is noted however that the majority of decisions in 24/25 were not appealed, with less than 0.3% of Councils decisions proceeding to a VCAT hearing.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: This result has remained stable from 2023-24 to 2024-25. We are exploring opportunities, including through the increased use of technology, to further improve our performance in this metric.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The higher result in 2024-25 is a result of an additional seven trucks that were delivered, however have been unable to be used as yet – resulting in us carrying additional vehicles throughout the year. Once the new vehicles are in service, the old vehicles can be sold, reducing the overall cost of this service.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The decrease in cost of the recyclables collection service can be attributed to a competitive tender process, resulting in lower processing costs.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The substantial increase in diversion of waste from landfill can be attributed to the introduction of the universal FOGO service to all Hume households in July 2024. This change of service provision increased the number of households with a FOGO bin from 44,639 in June 2024 to 90,622 in June 2025.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
This ratio is higher than the target due to:
1. Higher-than-budgeted depreciation expenses resulting from increased contributed assets and asset revaluations in 2023/24.
2. An unbudgeted asset write-off recorded under other expenses for the remaining written-down value of infrastructure assets renewed during the year.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The increase of this ratio aligns with rates capping increase plus additional supplementary rates income from growth.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The increase in this ratio is mainly due to higher cash and term deposit balances as at 30 June 2025, resulting from pre-paid VGC grants for 2025/26 and incomplete capital works projects carried forward from 2024/25 to 2025/26.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The increase in the unrestricted cash ratio is primarily due to lower balance of term deposits as at 30 June 2025 compared to the previous year, which are classified as other financial assets rather than cash. Increasing capital works program in the next 4 years will continue to erode the balance of unrestricted cash.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The decrease in this ratio is mainly due to higher own-source revenue from rates and user fees, along with a reduction in non-current landfill provision liabilities following costs incurred in 2024/25.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation:
The unfavourable variance against target is mainly due to lower capital works expenditure and higher-than-budgeted annual depreciation (refer to the explanation in the Expenditure Level Indicator).

Forecasted spending on asset upgrades will continue to rise until FY2027 and decline as projects get completed through FY2029.

Forecasted assets renewals will remain relatively stable until FY2028 and then decrease in FY2029 as projects get completed.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
The increase in the adjusted underlying surplus ratio in FY2025 is primarily driven by higher underlying income from rates, along with the prepayment of FY2026 VGC grants and developer contributions.

In 2025-26, the adjusted underlying surplus declines mainly due to substantial payments to developers for reimbursement of the land equalisation and  provision for work-in-kind and land-in-kind items. These payments are expected to gradually decrease in subsequent years, resulting in an improvement in the adjusted underlying surplus moving forward.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: This ratio improved compared to last year, mainly due to a higher adjusted underlying surplus driven by increased user fees, developer contributions, and prepaid VGC grants.

description: Rates compared to property values
Expenses per head of municipal population
Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in this ratio is mainly due to the higher value of infrastructure assets resulting from the revaluation conducted in 2024/25 in accordance with Australian Accounting Standards Board 13 amendments.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: This measure remains consistent with previous years in line with population growth.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in this ratio is mainly due to the prepayment of the 2025/26 VGC grant in 2024/25.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: This index score is a decile score and is calculated once every 5 years with the most recent being conducted during the COVID pandemic in 2021. This indicator combines census data from income, education, employment, occupation, housing and family structure. The COVID pandemic had a significant impact on the Hume community that resulted in an increase in disadvantage compared with the 2016 result.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Staff turn over has remained steady compared to 23/24 whilst overall headcount grew through 24/25. Turnover still remains  lower than 22/23 and 21/22.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation:
Pool visitation decreased from 29,930 to 23,934 from the 2023/24 season to the 2024/25 season representing a difference of 5,996 visits.
Decline due to shortened season and climate.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Councils swimming pools contract increased from $639,000 to $715,276 from the 2023/24 season to the 2024/25 season representing a difference of $76,276.

Animals reclaimed

variation: Continuation of incresing community trend towards rehomed animals.

variation: No animal management prosecutions this year.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
Percentage of staff turnover
Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation:
The City of Kingston ensures that Waves Leisure Centre meets all relevant health and safety requirements. During opening hours, pool water is tested every 3.5 to 4 hours in accordance with Department of Health regulations. Change rooms are inspected hourly, and the sauna and steam rooms are cleaned regularly.

Annual health inspections of Council aquatic facilities have not been a statutory requirement under the Public Health and Wellbeing Act 2008 since 2019. As such, health and safety compliance is managed internally by staff at Waves. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The City of Kingston’s Waves Leisure Centre recorded 671,600 community visits during the 2024/25 financial year, which equates to approximately four visits per head of municipal population. Usage of the aquatic facilities continues to grow, with strong attendance across all programs, including Learn to Swim, group fitness classes and school holiday activities. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The City of Kingston continues to deliver aquatic services efficiently. Despite increased patronage contributing to higher operating costs, Council achieved a slight cost reduction in operating Waves Leisure Centre compared to 2023/24. This result is within the range set by Council and reflects strong returns on investment in the facility.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: The City of Kingston promotes responsible pet ownership and prioritises community safety. In 2024/25, animal related requests were addressed within an average of 6.37 days, which is an increase from the 2023/24 results. This rise reflects the more complex nature of animal related requests received this financial year, such as dog attacks and persistent barking reports. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: The City of Kingston continues to place a strong emphasis on animal health and wellbeing through its Domestic Animal Management Plan 2021-2025. In the 2024/25 financial year, the animal reclaim rate was impacted particularly by cats with three surrendered in 2023/24 when compared to 38 surrendered in 2024/25. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: The City of Kingston recognises that responsible pet ownership enhances the wellbeing of both residents and their pets. Council's pound service provider is committed to securing a rehoming pathway for all unidentified and ownerless animals, in accordance with the code of practice for shelters and pounds. The rehoming rate has increased compared to the previous year, influenced by Council's pound service provider increasing assessment and rehoming programs for cats this financial year. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The City of Kingston provides a responsive animal management service for the Kingston community. In 2024/25, the cost of the animal management service per head of population increased from the previous year. This rise reflects higher service costs and an increased need for regulatory signage to be installed across Kingston's reserves and foreshore. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation:
The City of Kingston investigates offences under the Domestic Animals Act 1994, with a focus on serious breaches such as dog attacks. These matters are generally addressed through legal proceedings initiated in the Magistrates' Court of Victoria. Pursuing legal action serves to protect the community, promote compliance, and deliver justice to those affected.

Council also takes a proactive approach to managing and resolving animal-related complaints and incidents. In the 2024/25 period, all prosecutions initiated by Council were successful. However, matters resolved through compliance measures or Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) are formally withdrawn if withdrawal is warranted, and are not recorded as successful prosecutions, despite achieving satisfactory outcomes. This result is above the range set by Council and reflects its ongoing commitment to public safety and responsible pet ownership.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The City of Kingston continues to prioritise food safety and public health, with a high level of responsiveness in 2024. Food-related complaints were investigated within 1.98 days, on average, and all food-related risks were managed appropriately. This result was a slight improvement compared to the previous year and is within the range set by Council.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: The City of Kingston works with local food outlets to provide assessments, education, and guidance to ensure food is prepared and sold safely. During the year the percentage of registered food premises which received an annual food safety assessment increased significantly compared to the previous year. 1,216 food safety assessments were undertaken, with one business not inspected as they were closed at times of visit. The result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The City of Kingston seeks to deliver an effective and cost-efficient food safety service for the community. The cost of the food safety service is lower compared to the previous year due to a slight decrease in expenditure, as well as an increase in the number of food businesses operating over the course of the year. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: The City of Kingston prioritises food safety and ensures that any high-risk issues identified during an assessment were attended to immediately, and action taken to remove or reduce the risk. A total number of 411 critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications were all followed up. This result has remained the same as the previous year and is within the range set by Council.

description: Food safety samples
variation: The City of Kingston has a statutory obligation to obtain 307 food samples per calendar year. This target was slightly exceeded, with 324 samples submitted for analysis to ensure a suitable number of samples were taken at each food business visited as part of the Council's annual food sampling program.  This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: The City of Kingston made 257 of the 264 resolutions at meetings of council or delegated committee open to the public in 2024/25. A small number of resolutions (7) were discussed in closed meetings, consistent with the confidential provisions of the Local Government Act 2020. These resolutions involved matters relating to legal privilege, property information, Council business information, or personal information. Council remains committed to transparency and endeavours to make confidential resolutions - or parts there of - public where possible. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The City of Kingston continues to work hard to strengthen community consultation and engagement through initiatives such as Your Kingston Your Say and the Community Representative Panel. Council has improved 16 points from last years score of 58 index points. This rating is sourced from the annual Community Satisfaction Survey conducted by an independent research company. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: The City of Kingston continues to demonstrate strong Councillor attendance at Council Meetings. While attendances over 2024/25 were impacted by Councillor resignations, overall participation showed a slight improvement compared to the previous year. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The City of Kingston aims to deliver a cost-effective governance service. Although inflationary pressures during the financial year led to increased governance costs, these costs remain below both the statewide and similar council averages, and falls within the range set by Council.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The City of Kingston continues to work hard to make decisions in the best interests of our community. Council has improved by 13 points from last year’s score of 59 index points. This rating is sourced from the annual Community Satisfaction Survey, conducted by an independent research company. The result is above the range set by Council.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The City of Kingston remains committed to the community’s needs and demands by utilising the full library collection budget. 61 per cent of the library collection was purchased within the past five years. The decrease in library collection items reflects the increase in purchasing and processing costs, which has reduced the volume of new items able to be added to the collection. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The City of Kingston places a strong emphasis on delivering services in a cost-effective manner. Considerable efforts have been made to balance operational requirements with customer needs, particularly in relation to the increasing cost of materials and resources. Costs include operational expenses and capital purchases, such as library collection items. The result is within the range set by Council.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The City of Kingston continues to maintain a high-quality library collection, providing the community with access to both print and digital resources across all branches. Library loans increased by 4.6 per cent compared to the previous year, with total physical and digital loans exceeding one million. Borrowing trends continue to grow year by year. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Library membership
variation: The City of Kingston library service continues to have strong reach and attract new members across the community. 26.91 per cent of the population were active library members, meaning they borrowed at least one item during the past three years. Registered membership increasing by 7.7 per cent compared to the previous year. This growth reflects the library's diverse range of resources and programming. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The City of Kingston operates eight library branches, two of which offer extended access hours. While the overall library visits increased slightly, the Level Crossing Removal works impacted attendance at Parkdale Library. With completion of works and improved access, visitation is gradually recovering, supported by increased programming at Parkdale. This result in within the range set by Council.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: The City of Kingston has continued to achieve more than 100 per cent infant enrolments in the MCH Service, highlighting highly effective engagement. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The City of Kingston places a strong emphasis on delivering services in a cost-efficient manner. The cost of service result highlights Council's continued commitment to delivering the MCH Service within the expected budget and requirements. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: The City of Kingston is committed to providing high quality MCH Services. This result sees an increase when compared to the 2023/24 financial year, as we continue to work hard to re-engage with our young families post the COVID years. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: The City of Kingston continues to maintain its high level of engagement with our First Nation families with young children. This result sees a slight decrease when compared to the 2023/24 financial year, with a small number of families moving to the newly opened First Peoples Health and Wellbeing Clinic in Frankston, which is closer to their homes. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: The City of Kingston recorded an attendance of 94.73 per cent for infants at the 4-week Key Age and Stage Visits in the 2024/25 financial year, which is a decrease when compared to the 2023/24 financial year. This is due to an increased number of very premature babies still in hospital at 4 weeks and the increased number of home births where the birth notice is not received until after the completion of care at 6 weeks. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The City of Kingston gives priority to road safety and addresses issues in accordance with our Road Management Plan, with a regular road maintenance program and pro-active inspections. Sealed local road requests include issues such as potholes, damage and trip points. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The City of Kingston continues to invest in the local road network to ensure it does not deteriorate. Maintenance works undertaken in 2024/25 ensured that our road network is in good condition and above renewal intervention levels. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The City of Kingston focuses on delivering services cost-effectively. The components in a road reconstruction project are variable and may include traffic treatments, kerb and channel, footpath and nature strips. As such the average cost for sealed local road construction can vary widely. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The City of Kingston undertakes the local road resealing program to increase the durability and safety of road surfaces for users. The resealing contract is awarded through a competitive tender process to obtain the best market value. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads in the City of Kingston has increased on the previous year's result and aligns with the metropolitan average.  This data is collected annually in a Community Satisfaction Survey by an independent research company on behalf of the Victorian Government. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The City of Kingston is committed to working with applicants and objectors to achieve quality outcomes in planning application decisions. The median time to decide an application was 84 days. While there has been a decrease in total applications received, there has also been a reduction in planning staff, and periods within this financial year where there have been a number of planning vacancies. Council continues to balance the time taken to negotiate land use and development issues with the efficient processing of applications. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The City of Kingston is committed to working with applicants and objectors to achieve quality outcomes in planning application decisions. The percentage of planning application decided within required statutory timeframes is comparable to the previous year. This is within the target range set by Council.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The City of Kingston prioritises cost-effective service delivery. The cost per planning application for the statutory planning service increased compared to the previous year. The recent downturn in the development industry has led to fewer planning applications being lodged. The result less revenue and less applications with the same budgeted salary amount. Therefore, there was an increase in cost per application given fewer applications and the same budgeted amount of staff. This result is above the range set by Council.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The City of Kingston is committed to providing an efficient and effective statutory planning service. There was a decrease in the number of planning decisions upheld at VCAT compared to the previous year but there was also a decrease in the number of matters being taken to VCAT. Furthermore, a number of items were resolved at VCAT through consent or mediation or struck out or remitted. Although these are still reflected as decisions 'not upheld', Council considers this a good outcome as all parties reached an agreement through mediation. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: The City of Kingston is committed to delivering a reliable and efficient kerbside bin collection service for the community. In 2024/25, the number of missed kerbside garbage and recycling bins slightly decreased when compared to the previous year, reflecting improvements in the use of technology. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The City of Kingston aims to deliver an effective and cost-efficient garbage bin service for the community. In 2024/25, collection and disposal costs remained stable, with only a small increase in the cost per bin compared to the previous year. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The City of Kingston is committed to delivering an efficient and cost-effective recyclables bin collection service for the community. In 2024/25, kerbside recycling collection costs dropped significantly, driven by contractual changes and a reduction in recycling tonnages following the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme. This result is below the range set by Council.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The City of Kingston continues to encourage landfill diversion through efficient kerbside recycling and green organics collection services. In 2024/25, the amount of material diverted from landfill declined, primarily due to reduced recycling tonnages following the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme in late 2023. Landfill and green organic tonnages have remained consistent. This result is within the range set by Council.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: There is a year-on-year growth in expenditure due to inflationary and demand pressures, hence Council expects this indicator to continue to increase in future years.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: This indicator has been steadily increasing over the three-year period due to rate cap increases. The City of Kingston is compliant with the State Government's Rate Cap each year. The forecast trend is for this indicator to remain around the same level with a slight increase each year. The forecast results differ from the Adopted Budget due to the Adopted Budget formula inclusion of waste charges income, while the Performance Statement formula excludes waste charges income.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: This indicator has increased from last year due to an increase in current assets (specifically in other financial assets being term deposits). The increase is impacted by the $17 million loan taken out for the Mordialloc Aquatic Centre, as well as early receipt of Financial Assistance Grant. Council anticipates this indicator will reduce in future years as it utilises its cash holdings to fund its capital works program.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The indicator has declined in 2024/25 compared to 2023/24 due to the decrease in term deposits classified as unrestricted cash and an increase in current liabilities driven by higher unearned income and a $17 million interest-bearing loan for the Mordialloc Aquatic Centre. Looking ahead, unrestricted cash is projected to increase in 2025/26 due to higher term deposits held below 90 days, and subsequently decrease through to 2027/28 as Council draws on cash reserves to support its extensive capital works program, and continuing interest-bearing loan impact.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The indicators align with budget expectations, reflecting Council’s $17 million loan borrowing in 2024/25 to support development of the new aquatic facility. An additional loan is scheduled for 2025/26.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: This indicator remains aligned with budget targets, reflecting the $17 million loan undertaken in 2024/25 for the Mordialloc Aquatic Centre. An additional loan is scheduled for 2025/26. The result includes projected rate cap increases on rate revenue.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: This indicator has risen in 2024/25, driven by an increase in Non-Current Liabilities following the $17 million loan secured to fund the Mordialloc Aquatic Facility. Borrowing is expected to rise further in 2025/26, contributing to higher forecasts in subsequent years.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation:
The indicator declined in 2024/25, primarily due to a rise in depreciation expense following Council’s revaluation of fixed assets in 2023/24. 
In the forecast years, the proportion of renewal and upgrade expenditure as part of the broader capital works program is expected to grow. This reflects a greater focus on asset renewal works in the forecast years with major projects proposed. As forecast depreciation continues to grow, the indicator is forecast to decline. Future asset renewal works will be considered through upcoming budget processes.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
During 2024/25, there has been increased revenue from rates, user fees, statutory fees and fines, operating grants, and other income from found assets contributing to the underlying result. The result has been better than target/budget.
The forecast trend is due to expected growth in expenses above the level of growth in revenue and this outcome will be reviewed and sought to be improved in future budget processes. The expenditure increase is due to the challenging economic environment and inflationary pressures.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The indicator reduced in 2024/25 with rates revenue aligned with the rate cap being less than the increase in underlying revenue sources, specifically user fees, operating grant income, and other income. The forecast trend remains relatively flat with minor increases in the rate cap, and in user fees due to the new Mordialloc Aquatic Centre being commissioned.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: This indicator is consistent with the forecast. The forecast trend moving forward is for this indicator to gradually increase, as both rate revenue and the Capital Improved Value gradually increase over time.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: This result is consistent with budget expectations. It is forecast to increase in future years, in line with growth in expenditure due to inflationary pressures.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The result has increased from the previous year due to the revaluation of infrastructure assets in 2024/25

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The result demonstrates a consistent population density as City of Kingston's population increases when compared to an already established road network.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Council's own source revenue is continuing to increase due to rates and user fees increases.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The result is in line with expectations and reflects a continued strong level of operating grants received by Council, particularly in aged care and childcare, and the receipt of 50% of the 2025/26 Financial Assistance grant allocation.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The result reflects a relatively low level of socio-economic disadvantage within the City of Kingston's diverse communities.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: The result highlights a slight increase in staff turnover from the previous year. This is in line with the broader labour market.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Two health inspections were conducted at Knox Leisureworks in 2024-25.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Knox Leisureworks recorded 362,973 visits in 2024-25.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The cost of providing aquatic services has increased.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: All animal management requests are assessed based on risk and addressed in a timely manner. Council prioritises all dogs at large, animal collections, attacks, rushes, and animal cruelty requests, acting on them immediately. The figure for 2024-2025 shows a significant improvement on 2023-2024 due to improved systems that ensure requests are actioned more efficiently.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Council is committed to reuniting registered dogs and cats with their owners and has seen an increase in the number of impounded animals being returned to their owners. The number of successful reunifications varies from year to year, depending on how many animals are collected and whether officers are able to identify and contact their owners. The total number of animals collected for this measure includes 42 cases where the final outcome is currently unknown, as these animals are still being processed at the Council pound.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Local Government Victoria amended this measure in 2023-2024, resulting in an increase in the number of reported results when compared with previous years. The 2024-2025 figure has dropped slightly from 2023-2024.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Costs increased in 2024-2025 because it was the first financial year operating under a new structure, which includes additional staff to assist with the enforcement of community laws.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council prosecutes serious offences under the Domestic Animals Act, as well as matters involving repeat offenders who consistently show irresponsible pet ownership. The Council has maintained a 100% success rate in its prosecutions.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Response times have improved due to process improvements and gains in staff experience.

description: Food safety assessments
variation:
All high-risk and Class 1 premises trading were inspected during 2024-2025. Premises not inspected in 2024-2025 were:
• Businesses closed before an annual inspection could be conducted,
• Mobile and temporary premises that were not operating,
• Home-based businesses that could not be contacted for an inspection to be arranged, and
• Premises not trading with the proprietor unable to be contacted.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: A review of the financial model for the food safety service has identified a significant reduction in the costs allocated to this service as a proportion of the broader Health Services function at Council.  This in conjunction with a moderate increase in registered premises has seen a significant reduction in the cost per premises to provide this service.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: All critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications were followed up by Council in 2024-2025.

description: Food safety samples
variation: The percentage is greater than 100 because Council also obtains food samples when complaints are made or when checking the effectiveness of food safety procedures at the premises.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council continues to strive to limit the number of decisions made in meetings closed to the public as much as possible. Four resolutions were made in closed meetings of Council during 2024‐2025.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Satisfaction with consultation and engagement remains stable and measurably and significantly above the long-term average since 2014 of 59.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Attendance increased slightly from the previous year.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The direct cost of Council’s governance service remained stable in 2024-2025.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Satisfaction with decision making declined by three per cent from 2024 but remains measurably higher than the long-term average since 2014 of 60.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The number of items purchased has decreased due to the increasing regular retail price of library materials and rising processing costs of suppliers.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The cost of the library service remained stable in 2024-2025.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Library utilisation rose in 2024-2025.

description: Library membership
variation: The number of registered library members rose significantly in 2024-2025, attesting to the popularity of the new Knox Library opened in March 2024.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The number of library visits rose significantly in 2024-2025, attesting to the popularity of the new Knox Library opened in March 2024.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Infant enrolments for the MCH service remained stable throughout 2024-2025, with a slight proportional increase in the second half of the year. (Note that a result of over 100% occurs when we have additional infants transfer into our municipality before a first home visit, and/or the birth notice was initially sent to the incorrect LGA.)

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The increased cost of the MCH service in 2024–2025 is primarily due to Enterprise Agreement entitlements.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation in the MCH service over the first half of 2024-2025 was affected by workforce challenges. Successful recruitment in the second half of the year facilitated an uplift in participation.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Proactive engagement has supported First Nations participation in the MCH service.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Council maintains a very high level of engagement with families with young infants. (Note that a result of over 100% occurs when we have additional infants transfer into our municipality before a first home visit, and/or the birth notice was initially sent to the incorrect LGA.)

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Council includes all requests received from external customers (via customer service requests or after-hours requests) that relate to the quality of the sealed road surface and kerb and channel.  Requests are counted regardless of whether a maintenance activity is required to be undertaken in accordance with Council's service levels.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The condition of Council’s road network has remained steady, as confirmed by the 2023-2024 full road condition audit. These audits are conducted every four years, with the next scheduled for 2026-2027. Council maintains high road service standards and is progressively addressing renewal backlogs. The latest audit has informed a targeted works program, and the 'Target as per budget' will be reviewed through the upcoming Transport Asset Management Plan.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Total costs are based on the relatively small number of road reconstructions undertaken and vary according to the extent of reconstruction specified for each project.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The 3% increase from 2023-2024 is in line with the Consumer Price Index (CPI).

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads remains stable, up one per cent on the 2024 figure.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The median number of days to process a planning application was 47 days. This is consistent with previous periods.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The percentage of planning applications decided within the legislated timeframe was 76.73%. This is consistent with previous periods.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The direct cost of the statutory planning service per planning application received was $2,698.32 per application received, which was a slight reduction from the cost of 2023-2024, primarily due to a slight increase in the number of applications received.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: There were 6 appeals with decisions made by VCAT during 2024-2025, not including 10 appeals that were settled by consent or withdrawn. Of the 6 appeals, 2 were not set aside. 1 of the 6 applications with appeals was reported to Council.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Missed bin numbers remain very low due to excellent contractor performance, effective contract management and strong community engagement with the service. (Note: the figure is only for confirmed cases where the contractor was at fault.)

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The volume of garbage collected has decreased due to the successful implementation of FOGO and fortnightly rubbish collection. However, costs have increased compared to the previous reporting period, primarily due to rising disposal fees.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The direct cost of the kerbside recyclables bin service has remained consistent with previous years.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The slight decrease in Knox’s waste diversion rate is largely attributed to the successful uptake of the Container Deposit Scheme (CDS), which has shifted recyclable materials away from Council’s collection system, and to lower green waste tonnages due to reduced rainfall. As a result, these materials are no longer included in our reported recycling figures, impacting the calculated diversion percentage. Despite this, Knox achieved a 71% diversion rate—well above the state average—placing the Council as a leader in reducing waste to landfill and positioning us strongly to meet the state targets of 72% by 2025 and 80% by 2030.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The 2024-25 expenditure includes a net loss on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment totalling $8.8 million, leading to an increase in the 2024-25 expenses per property assessment.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: General rate income was increased by the approved rate cap percentage.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Cash and cash equivalents, together with other financial assets, are $10.2 million greater than last year, while trade and other receivables are up $3.5 million (including an increase in rates debtors of $2.9 million). This has been offset by decreases in assets classified as held for sale ($4.1 million), and prepayments and contract assets ($1.4 million), together with increases in trade and other payables ($7.0 million), contract liabilities ($3.0 million), and current interest-bearing liabilities $1.2 million).

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Cash and cash equivalents have increased by $17.7 million. Current liabilities have increased by $10.2 million, with increases in trade and other payables ($7.0 million), contract liabilities ($3.0 million), and current interest-bearing liabilities $1.2 million), partially offset by a $1.2 million decrease in current provisions. Year-end cash balances are forecast to be lower in the upcoming years, leading to a decrease in the unrestricted cash percentage.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Borrowings totalling $10.0 million were taken out in the 2025 financial year, with further borrowings forecast in future years to fund projects within the capital works program. Rates and charges revenue increased by $4.5 million.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Borrowings totalling $10.0 million were taken out in the 2025 financial year, with further borrowings forecast in future years to fund projects within the capital works program. Rates and charges revenue increased by $4.5 million, while loans and borrowings repayments increased by $2.9 million.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Although borrowings totalling $10.0 million were taken out in the 2025 financial year, repayments of interest-bearing liabilities and lease liabilities led to a decrease in non-current liabilities of $0.5 million. Own-source income increased by $8.9 million, including a $4.5 million increase in rates and charges revenue.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Asset renewal and upgrade works increased by $6.1 million on the 2024 financial year, while depreciation increased by $0.9 million. The 2026 capital works budget shows an increase in renewal and upgrade works, largely due to the carry forward of projects from 2025. The renewal and upgrade works return to 2025 levels in the following two years.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Recurrent grant income (operating and capital) increased by $13.6 million, largely due to the entire 2024-25 Victoria Loval Government Grants Commission General Purpose Grant and Local Road Funding being received during the 2025 financial year, while 50% of the 2025-26 funding was brought forward and paid prior to 30 June 2025. The improved 2026 adjusted underlying result is largely due to the forecast gain on disposal of land carried forward from 2025, as opposed to the loss on disposal of property and infrastructure included in the 2025 figure.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Recurrent grant income (operating and capital) increased by $13.6 million, largely due to the entire 2024-25 Victoria Loval Government Grants Commission General Purpose Grant and Local Road Funding being received during the 2025 financial year, while 50% of the 2025-26 funding was brought forward and paid prior to 30 June 2025. This was parttially offset by the $4.5 million increase in rates and charges revenue.

variation: Rates and charges revenue increased by $4.5 million. However property values have increased $4,800 million compared to the 2024 financial year.

variation: The 2024-25 expenditure includes a net loss on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment totalling $8.8 million, leading to an increase in the 2024-25 expenses per head of municipal population.

variation: The infrastructure value has increased by $266.4 million on the prior year, with additions of $49.5 million and a revaluation increase of $253.0 million being offset by a decrease related to depreciation and disposals. The municipal population has increased by 1,536.

variation: The municipal population has increased by 1,536.

variation: Own-source revenue has increased by $8.9 million, including a $4.5 million increase in rates and charges revenue. The municipal population has increased by 1,536.

variation: Recurrent grant income (operating and capital) increased by $13.6 million, largely due to the entire 2024-25 Victoria Loval Government Grants Commission General Purpose Grant and Local Road Funding being received during the 2025 financial year, while 50% of the 2025-26 funding was brought forward and paid prior to 30 June 2025.

variation: The number of health inspections conducted at aquatic facilities has stayed consistent with the results of previous years.

variation: Lower visitation numbers to Council's aquatic facilities has seen the cost of providing the service increase in comparison to the previous two financial years.

variation: The number of animals reclaimed has decreased which can be attributed to the economic circumstances experienced by the community over the past 12 months. Council is observing, many people are unable to afford to retrieve their animals.

Food safety samples

Measures have been implemented to prevent a recurrence in future reporting periods.

variation: There was an expectation that the increased consultation associated with the development of the Community Vision and Council Plan would result in a higher community satisfaction rating. variation: Councillor attendance had slightly decreased as a Councillor was granted a leave of absence for the periods of 8 May 2025 to 7 June 2025 and 30 June 2025 to 01 September 2025.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: From 26 October 2024 all Mayors, Deputy Mayors, and Councillors were required to complete mandatory training including Councillor induction training which is required to be completed by all Councillors within 4 months of taking the oath of office under section 32 of the Local Government Act; and Professional development training, also required to be completed by all Councillors beginning in the first full calendar year after being elected under section 33A of the Local Government Act. These new requirements can be attributed to a portion of increased councillor expenses.

Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: This represents the median number of calendar days it takes from the moment a planning permit application is received until a final decision is made. Council has seen a decrease in the median number of days to decide on an application, in comparison to last financial year. The median result for this financial year is now consistant with the 2021-22 and 2022-23 results.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The 23/24 financial year saw a lower than expected result due to staff vacancies and increased complexity of applications, the current year has seen a return to a more normalised year with ratio returning to prior year levels.
