description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Over the course of this financial year, Council had 4 decisions made by VCAT to which 2 were upheld. Council also had an additional 3 appeals that were lodged with VCAT however were withdrawn following negotiation.

Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The reduction in diversion from 23/24 is linked to the first full year of the container deposit scheme as there has been a reduced level of recyclable and green organics from the previous year, whilst general waste levels remained steady.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The result was above target largely due to items budgeted as capital that have been expensed due to them not meeting capitalisation criteria, together with unbudgeted items such as emergency clean-up works following the August 2024 storm event and higher depreciation expenses following infrastructure valuations undertaken in the previous financial year.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The average rate per property assessment is expected to increase in line with the adopted budget increases in 2026, and is currently estimated to increase in line with the expected rate cap from 2027 to 2029.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The reduction in the ratio in 2025 is due to increases in current liabilities for unearned capital grants, together with the movement of an interest only loan into current liabilities that will mature in the 2026 financial year. The ratio is predicted to rise again in future years after these liabilities are settled.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The ratio has decreased in 2025 due to high levels of  Contract and Other liabilities associated with unearned capital grants, and Interest Bearing Liabilities as a result of an interest only loan that matures in 2026. Unrestricted cash is expected to improve from negative 56.1% to negative 13.4% over the projected period as these liabilities are settled. The ratio is negative as Council invests most of its surplus cash in financial assets and thereby maintains a relatively small cash balance compared to the size of restricted amounts such as unspent grants, trust funds and statutory reserves. These investments are timed to mature when the cash will be required.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The result for this measure has decreased to 15.5% in 2025 due to the continued repayment of current borrowings and no new loans being drawn down for the year. The ratio is projected to continue to decrease over the forecast period with repayments currently expected to exceed new borrowings.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The result for this measure decreased to 2.7% in 2025, this was largely due to the repayment of a lump sum in 2024 before finalising the final borrowing amount required for the Moe Rail Precinct Revitalisation Stage 2 project. The increased ratio forecasted in 2026 is due to the principal repayment of an interest only loan falling due in that year.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The ratio is forecasted to remain in steady decline as council repays existing interest-bearing liabilities for the four-year forecast period

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The ratio decreased to 93.8% in the current year largely due to the deferral of road rehabilitation works, however the ratio has stayed relatively high as it has been supported by external grant funding. The reduction in the ratio in future years is symbolic of reducing estimates of government grants. Council’s largest asset class, roads, is expected to be maintained around 100%, however other classes such as drainage, which is renewed only on a reactive basis, and buildings which are often reliant on government funding for major renewal and upgrade works are forecasted to remain below 100% with the exception of 2028 when a new landfill cell is scheduled to be built.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Council’s large underlying deficit in 2024 was largely due to a reduction in operating grants which primarily related to the timing of receipt of Financial Assistance Grants funding. The ratio is projected to continue to see underlying deficits in future years due to restrictions on Council’s ability to increase revenue and increased depreciation expenses resulting from the recent construction of major new infrastructure and asset revaluation increases.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The increased ratio in 2024 was largely due to a reduction in operating grants associated with the timing of Financial Assistance Grants. Council’s reliance on rates revenue is expected to continue to rise into the future primarily based on moderate estimates of future levels of recurrent government funding and user fees and charges.

description: Rates compared to property values

variation: The ratio has increased in line with the valuation movements from the revaluations of infrastructure assets undertaken in the 2025 financial year this movement incorporates the new interpretation requirements for Accounting Standard AASB 13.

variation: The result for this measure has remained stable over the three year period.

variation: Minor increase in 2024 as a result of rates growth and increased income from landfill gate fees and interest on investments.

variation: Council continues to maintain an acceptable level of borrowings well within capacity to repay from usual revenue sources inclusive of rates & charges.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council continues to repay its borrowings at principal and interest repayments which are managed within resources.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Council's longer term liabilities inclusive of bank borrowings are well within Council's capacity to repay from usual sources of revenue. Council continues to repay principal reductions at rate of $500k per annum.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Asset and upgrade statistics have been bolstered in recent years due to increased Capital Works programs.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Financial Assistance Grants received in advance had a positive impact on this measure.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Decrease due to increased grants received including the Financial Assistance Grant and IMPACT Route.

Expenses per head of municipal population

Percentage of staff turnover
Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council's Environmental Health team conducted one inspection of the Maribyrnong Aquatic Centre. Additionally, water samples were taken from all pools, and the results met compliance standards

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The Maribyrnong Aquatic Centre had a slight drop in overall attendance compared to the previous financial year. The biggest declines occurred in July, August and September, largely due to unseasonal weather. Attendance by local schools also decreased, due to cost of living pressures. However, the construction of two waterslides and a splash park is expected to increase visitation numbers

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The financial cost of the Maribyrnong Aquatic Centre has increased significantly due to increases in fixed costs including utilities and cleaning products and services. Council has received funding from the Federal Government to assist with transitioning towards electrification of the Centre, which is expected to reduce the overall service cost

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Council continues to work diligently to ensure resident requests are responded to within a 24 hour period.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Both Council and the Lost Dogs Home continue to work together towards animals being returned to their owners which has seen an increase of reclaimed pets

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Extensive efforts are made to re-home all animals. Where owners cannot be established, assessments are completed to ensure the animal is suitable for rehoming with physical and behavioural checks completed prior to rehoming.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Council has achieved a slight reduction in costs through the implementation of several continuous improvement initiatives

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council continues to prosecute via the court system where serious offences have occurred that require a higher level of enforcement action.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: A combination of new staff and a new customer service database has increased the time taken to action food complaints. Staff upskilling has resolved this issue

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Of the total number of food premises requiring an assessment, 46% were completed

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Continued improvements in assessment processes and a focus on innovation and digital applications continues to drive down costs. Council has 1,127 food premises requiring registration or notification.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: 100% of all food premises found to have a major or critical outcome were reassessed to ensure satisfactory compliance. Procedures are in place to ensure non-compliances are followed up within the required timeframe

description: Food safety samples
variation: 209 food samples were obtained from registered food businesses and tested to determine if they were safe to consume. An additional 19 samples were obtained as part of surveillance activities highlighting Council's commitment to food safety

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council is committed to transparent decision making with only one Council resolution being made confidentially within the provisions of the Local Government Act 2020.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The result is supported by Council's continued dedication to consulting and engaging with the community. It also reflects a consistent rise compared to previous years

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance at Council meetings remains steady, with Council meetings held in a hybrid manner (in-person and virtual).

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: Council is committed to ensuring Councillor expenses are appropriately managed in accordance with the Local Government Act 2020 and the Councillor Support and Expenses Policy. A breakdown of Councillor costs per quarter can be found on Council’s website. Additionally, this amount is lower than the Consumer Price Index (CPI)

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: This steady outcome is supported by Council’s strong governance framework. It reflects a gradual increase, achieving the highest satisfaction level to date.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Council is shifting the ratio of print and digital collection items in response to borrowing data and customer request. A greater focus on digital acquisitions and  the introduction of a Library of Things is driving strong performance against this measure.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: 86% of the Libraries operating budget is spent on salary and related costs.  While the service continues to make savings and find new ways to innovate, salary increases continue to increase the cost of the library service

description: Loans per head of population
variation: A focus on reader development and sector-wide programs such as the Great Summer Read is supporting a strong number of loans per head of population

description: Library membership
variation: Council set a target to grow its membership base by 50% over two years. Improved membership reflects effective member acquisition campaigns, removal of barriers to joining the library and continued refinement of services and programming to better reflect community need.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Council hosted 416,166 visits in 2024/25, which is a 41% increase over the past two years. Council continues to reduce barriers to membership and refined collection and programs to better reflect community need.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Enrolments in the MCH service are stable with a slight reduction in births

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The MCH team has one staff member on maternity leave and two on long service leave. These absences are being covered by a combination of casual and permanent staff working additional hours

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation in the Maternal and Child Health (MCH) service has seen a slight increase, despite challenges such as staff sick leave resulting in uncovered shifts and the need to reschedule appointments. Attendance was also affected by the temporary relocation of an MCH centre due to capital works, requiring families to travel to an alternative site.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children declined during this period, partly due to some Aboriginal families relocating outside the municipality without yet engaging with services in their new area. As a result, they continue to be included in our data. In support of ongoing cultural connection and engagement, Council has partnered with VACCA and local Aboriginal Elders to deliver a Welcome Baby to Country ceremony.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Between January and July 2025, a higher number of birth notices were received later than usual, leading to an increased volume of Key Age and Stage visits taking place in July. The Maternal and Child Health (MCH) team continues to observe several families relocating to other municipalities, along with a rise in young infants requiring hospitalisation or re-admission after one month of age. Additionally, there has been a growing trend of families opting for home births or engaging private midwives. As these services typically provide care until the baby is six weeks old, many families are postponing their engagement with Council’s services until after this period

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Council continues to investigate opportunities for service level improvements including updating the Asset Plan and Road Renewal Program.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: To improve accuracy, Council assesses the entire sealed local road network against intervention standards, simulating an unconstrained funding scenario. This provides a clearer picture of the network’s actual condition. The cost of maintaining and renewing roads has risen sharply in recent years, reducing the volume of works achievable per dollar spent. While overall expenditure may be increasing, the extent of roads maintained to standard has declined. Council will continue to closely monitor investment levels, current road network hierarchy and standards to ensure alignment with the modelled projections.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The cost of sealed local road reconstruction continues to be consistent with inflation.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: There was a significant decrease in cost due to the type of projects listed in this program, as well as other contributions such as less complicated traffic management

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Customer satisfaction in relation to sealed local roads has improved, which is reflective of improved service delivery.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: Council continues to improve the time taken to decide planning applications, with extensions of time to provide further information generally reducing.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Industry shortages have resulted in  workforce shortages. The varied quality of planning applications received has also resulted in longer application assessment timeframes.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost of service has continued to increase when compared to previous years as the number of applications received has decreased by 11%.  The increased costs associated with construction discouraging development and the continued use of the Development Facilitation Unit (DFU) pathway within the State Government has impacted service costs. Applications lodged with the DFU require Council input and consideration without any credit to Council. Continuing industry and workforce shortages has also increased the costs of providing planning services.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Council's decisions continue to be sound, based on the planning merits of the case. In most cases contested decisions were able to be mediated, avoiding the need for a costly and lengthy VCAT Hearing.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Council's adherence to Key Performance Indicators (KPI) in contracts has resulted in service improvements and fewer missed collections in 2024/25

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: An increase in the number of bin collection services performed and an increase in bin collection rates has resulted in increased costs for the garbage bin collection service.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: An increase in the number of bin collection services performed and an increase in bin collection rates has resulted in increased costs to the recycling bin collection service.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: There has been a slight decrease in the volume of kerbside organics and recycling collected in 2024/25, compared to the previous year (5.6%). Waste volumes have also increased compared to the previous year (1.6%)

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The increase in expenses per property assessment reflects the Council’s continued investment in delivering high-quality services and infrastructure to the community, ensuring that resources are aligned with growing needs and expectations.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: No significant change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025 (3.13%).

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: No significant change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025 (0.7%).

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Unrestricted cash reduced due to increase in capital expenditure and Council’s continued investment in delivering high-quality services and infrastructure to the community.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The Council achieved a positive financial performance outcome, driven by a reduction in outstanding loan balances through ongoing repayments (numerator), coupled with an increase in rate revenue (denominator). The forecast increase in loans and borrowings is due to the planned $15 million new borrowings in the 2026/27 financial year is for the RecWest Shorten Reserve project.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: This positive outcome reflects the full-year impact of loan repayments in the current financial year, compared to the previous year where repayments were only made for part of the year. The forecast increase in repayments is due to the planned $15 million new borrowings in the 2026/27 financial year is for the RecWest Shorten Reserve project.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: This positive result reflects a reduction in non-current liabilities and an increase in the Council’s own-source revenue, highlighting a strengthened capacity to meet long-term financial obligations as they fall due. The forecast increase in non-current liabilities is due to the planned $15 million new borrowings in the 2026/27 financial year is for the RecWest Shorten Reserve project.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The decline in this ratio relative to the target & prior year is primarily attributable to an increase in depreciation expense resulting from asset revaluations, as well as a reduction in capital expenditure due to the deferral of some major projects.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The strong result exceeding 5% underscores the Council’s robust financial position and its effective fiscal management.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: No significant change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025 (-2.67%).

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: No significant change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025 (2.56%).

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in expenses per head of municipal population reflects the Council’s continued investment in delivering high-quality services and infrastructure to the community, ensuring that resources are aligned with growing needs and expectations.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in this ratio is primarily driven by the revaluation of infrastructure assets, the recognition of contributed assets, and the capitalisation of both the current and prior year's Work In Progress (WIP) projects.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: No significant change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025 (2.47%).

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: No significant change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025 (1.52%).

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: In 2024–25, the Council received a 50% advance Victorian Grants Commission (VGC) payment relating to the following financial year. In contrast, no advance was received in 2023–24 for the subsequent year; however, the majority (95%) of the 2023–24 allocation had already been received in advance during 2022–23.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: No change from 2023/2024 to 2024/2025.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: The decline in staff turnover indicates improved workforce stability, reflecting the Council’s commitment to employee engagement, and the positive influence of broader employment market conditions.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Health inspections of Council's aquatic facilities are conducted annually. In 2024/25, Council completed all required health inspections at aquatic facilities.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: This indicator measures the number of visits to aquatic facilities per head of municipal population. In 2024/25, there was a slight decrease in utilisation of aquatic facilities. This decrease resulted from a retiling project that Council undertook at Aquahub, which involved closure of pool access from November 2024 to April 2025. Council utilisation rates remain high due to successful community programming and engagement approaches.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: This measure relates to the overall cost of running Council aquatic facilities, less revenue received. This measure indicates positive and consistent financial performance.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: This measure relates to the average number of days between the receipt of an animal management request and the first action response.  The time taken to action animal management requests continues to remain low due to Council’s responsive approach.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: This measure relates to the percentage of collected registrable animals reclaimed under the Domestic Animals Act 1994. The number of animals reclaimed has decreased slightly but remains consistent with previous years. Council continues to focus on reuniting animals with their owners.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: This measure relates to the percentage of collected registrable animals under the Domestic Animals Act 1994 who are subsequently rehomed.  The 2023/24 result was adjusted to 52.63% due to a change in methodology by Local Government Victoria. The percentage of animals rehomed for 2024/25 is comparable to the adjusted 2023/24 figure.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: This measure highlights the direct cost of Council’s animal management service per registrable animal under the Domestic Animals Act 1994. The cost of animal management service is higher than previous year.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: This measure captures the percentage of successful animal management prosecutions under the Domestic Animals Act 1994. In 2024/25, The number of animal management prosecutions has decreased in comparison to previous years. Council was unsuccessful in two animal management prosecutions which is reflected in the overall result.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: This indicator measures the average number of days taken for Council to respond to food complaints (from receipt of an issue to the first response action). Data shown is for the 2024 calendar year to align with reporting to the Department of Health. The number of days taken to action food complaints is less than 2 days. Council's Environmental Health Officers continue to respond to customers as soon as they are received.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: This indicator measures the percentage of registered Class 1 and Class 2 food premises that receive an annual food safety assessment. Data shown is for the 2024 calendar year to align with reporting to the Department of Health.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: This measure captures the direct cost of providing food safety services per food premises. The cost of the food safety service is similar to the result for the previous year.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: This indicator measures the percentage of both critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications, which are subsequently followed up by Council. The data provided is for the 2024 calendar year and aligns with the Department of Health reporting requirements. In 2024, all premises that required a follow up inspection were undertaken.

description: Food safety samples
variation: This indicator measures the percentage of food samples obtained (per the required number of food samples). Data shown is for the 2024 calendar year to align with Department of Health (DoH) reporting requirements and is consistent with the previous year result.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: This indicator measures the percentage of Council resolutions at an Ordinary, or Special Council meeting, or at a meeting of a Special Committee of Council, consisting only of Councillors (and which are closed to the public under Section 66 of the Local Government Act 2020). The percentage of Council resolutions at meetings closed to the public has decreased due to the decreased number of tender evaluation reports during 2024/25 that required Council approval (>$500,000).

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Community satisfaction is measured in the annual Local Government Community Satisfaction Survey. Community satisfaction with "Community consultation and engagement" in 2024/25 remained consistent with results received in previous financial years.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Council determines policy and makes other decisions at Council Meetings that are typically held on a monthly basis. This indicator measures overall Councillor attendance at Ordinary and Special Council Meetings. In 2024/25, there was a 11.24% increase in attendance compared to the previous year. This resulted from a reduced number of councillors taking a leave of absence for meetings.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: This measure relates to the direct cost of delivering Council’s Governance service (per elected representative). In 2024/25, the cost of elected representation was consistent with results from previous years.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Community satisfaction is measured in the annual Local Government Community Satisfaction Survey. Community satisfaction with "council decisions" in 2024/25 remained consistent with results received in previous financial years.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: This measure refers to the percentage of the library collection which has been purchased over the last five years. The percentage of recently purchased library collection items now includes digital items.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: This measure captures the direct cost of the library service (per municipal population). The cost of the library service has increased when compared to the previous year.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: This measure captures the number of collection item (loans), per head of the municipal population. Loans per population are comparable to the previous year.

description: Library membership
variation: This measure looks at the percentage of resident municipal population who are registered library members. The increase in membership between 2023/24 to 2024/25 largely resulted from an increase in online registration and activities.  This was a new measure introduced to the Framework in 2023/24, so data before this point is not available.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: This measure shows the number of library visits per head of municipal population. Library visits per head of the population in 2024/25 are comparable to the previous financial year.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: The MCH service enrols newborn infants in the service (for home visits) following receipt of a birth notification from the hospital. This result is consistent with birth notices received by Council, with some variation due to the families of infants moving into and out of the municipality.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: This measure shows the cost of delivering Councils Maternal and Child Health service, per hour of delivered service. The delivery of additional support programs for families, including the Saturday clinic and Dad's Connect Sessions, have resulted in a slight increase in the cost of delivering the MCH service.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: This measure captures participation of children in key Maternal and Child Health age and stage appointments. Participation rates in 2024/25 are consistent with previous financial years.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: This measure captures the percentage of Aboriginal children enrolled who participate in the MCH service. Participation rates in 2024/25 are consistent with previous financial years.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: This measure shows the percentage of infants enrolled in the MCH service who participated in their four-week key age and stage visit.  Participation in this identified key age and stage visit has continued at the same rate as birth notifications received.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Road requests are defined as customer requests logged in Council’s corporate customer service application, Infor Pathway. In 2024/25, there has been an increase in sealed local road requests compared to the previous financial year. Requests relate to issues such as: line marking, pothole repairs, damaged roads and patching, and road sweeping.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Council defines the intervention level to be a road condition rating equivalent to "very poor". The 2024/25 result for sealed local roads maintained to condition standards remains consistent with previous financial years.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: This measure shows the total project costs associated with the reconstruction of a sealed local road. Despite the increasing cost of labour and materials, the cost of sealed local road reconstruction has decreased compared to the previous financial year.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: This measure shows the total project costs associated with the resealing of a sealed local road. Despite the increasing cost of labour and materials, the cost of sealed local road resealing in 2024/25 has decreased compared to the previous year.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Community satisfaction is measured in the annual Local Government Community Satisfaction Survey. Community satisfaction with "sealed local roads" in 2024/25 has remained consistent with results received in previous financial years.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: This measure shows the median number of days between the receipt of a planning application and a Council decision made regarding that application. In 2024/25, the time taken to make a decision on planning applications was lower than the previous financial year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: In accordance with the Planning and Environment Act 1987, a Council is permitted 60 statutory days to determine the outcome of a planning application.  In 2024/25, there has been an increase in the percentage of decisions made within this required timeframe, compared to the previous financial year.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: This measure shows the direct cost to Council to provide the statutory planning service, per planning application received. The cost of the statutory planning service in 2024/25 is comparable to the previous financial year.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: If an applicant disagrees with a decision of Council (in relation to a planning application) they can appeal that decision at the Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (VCAT). The percentage of Council decisions upheld by VCAT in 2024/25 is lower compared to the previous financial year but remains high.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: This indicator shows the ratio of bins missed (scheduled bin collections). This includes 120L, 80L, a second bin and fortnightly recycling kerbside bin collections. In 2024/25, there was a decrease in the number of missed bins compared to the previous financial year, demonstrating an improvement in the efficiency of this service.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: This measure highlights the direct cost to Council to provide the kerbside garbage bin collection service (per kerbside garbage bin). There was a 4.59% variance increase in 2024/25 which likely results from an increase to the consumer price index.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: This measure shows the direct cost to Council to provide the kerbside recyclables collection service per kerbside recyclables bin. The cost of kerbside recyclables collection service remain consistent with previous years.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: This measure refers to the percentage of garbage, recyclables and green organics collected from kerbside bins, that is diverted from landfill.  There has been a 3.02% variation from budget in the amount of waste diverted from landfill. This result may be due to the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme in the municipality reducing the volume of recycling collected. It has also been impacted by a reduction in the volume of Food Organics Garden Organics (FOGO) collected due to less rainfall during the year and a corresponding lower level of organic growth.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: This measure looks at the total expenses per property assessment. Over the reporting period, this indicator has increased marginally from 2023/24, in line with the growth in the municipality and increased expenditure as a result of price increases in insurances, contracts, utilities and enterprise agreement. The results remains in line with previous financial years and the range expected by Council. The favourable variance from budget to actuals for 2024/25 is due to a number of variances in terms of expenditure items, including employee costs, materials and services, depreciation and assets written off. Across all expense items in 2024/25, there was a favourable variance of $2.207m compared to adopted budget, providing a favourable result for expenditure efficiency within the financial year.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The increase is in line with the expected rate revenue increase associated with the rate cap and forecast growth within the municipality.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Prior year fluctuations of the liquidity ratio result is mainly attributable to recognising the new Accounting Standards AASB 15 and AASB 1058 which increased the value of Council's short-term liabilities. The ratio is expected to continue to fluctuate as projected new borrowings are drawn down, impacting Council's current liabilities.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The rise in this ratio compared to prior financial years primarily stems from an overall increase to cash balances, mainly relating to the 50% prepayment of 2025/26 Victorian Local Government Grants Commission (VLGGC) Financial Assistance Grant funding. The subsequent decrease in future years is linked to a different calculation being used within the budget document from the standard LGPRF ratio calculation.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: This indicator measures the ratio of total interest-bearing liabilities to rate revenue. It includes borrowings undertaken in 2015 and 2022 which are still being repaid. Future forecasts indicate that new borrowings will align with the Council's adopted Financial Plan.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The slight reduction since 2023/24 reflects the reduction in the principal loan amounts. Future forecasts reflect projected new borrowings in line with Council's adopted Financial Plan.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: In previous financial years, non-current liabilities were elevated primarily because of significant amounts of unearned income associated with the new Accounting Standards AASB 15 and AASB 1058. The increase in 2025/26 is attributed to projected new borrowings, while the subsequent decline reflects the reduction in long-term loan balances.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Although Council did not spend the expected 114% of depreciation on renewing and upgrading our assets in 2024/25, there is more than $9M works in progress that will continue into 2025/26.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The adjusted underlying results for 2023/24 and 2024/25 financial years have improved from the previous two financial years where Council had one-off impacts of the net loss in associates and joint ventures and higher assets write off as part of improvement initiatives which negatively impacted on this measure. This ratio is expected to remain steadily in future years in line with Council's financial direction.  The favourable variance from budget to actuals for 2024/25 is linked mainly to an increase in operating grants, predominantly due to the prepayment of the Victorian Grants Commission funding and lower depreciation expense than budgeted.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: In Victoria, council rate increases are limited by a cap set by the Minister for Local Government. The current and future trend is steady and in line with projected rate caps.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: This ratio has remained consistent with other prior periods with no material variation. Future ratio forecasts also indicate relatively similar ratios with no material movement.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The result demonstrates Council's ability to maintain a low-cost base per head of population. The 2024/25 increase is in line with CPI increases and growth within the municipality.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in infrastructure per head of municipal population is mainly related to a combination of new assets being added and an increase in large revaluation of Council's buildings and infrastructure assets to be in line with AASB 13 amendment on fair value measurement.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The result for population density per length of road has increased by 3.77%. The increase is largely due to the increase in population from 117,434 in 2023/24 to 119,354 in 2024/25.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The result has increased marginally from the prior year. Council will continue to explore various sources of revenue to ensure ongoing financial sustainability.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The main reason for the large variance in 2024/25 compared to the previous financial year arises from 100% of the 2023/24 Victorian Local Government Grants Commission (VLGGC) Financial Assistance Grant funding being received in 2022/23 and 50% of the VLGGC grant for 2025/26 being received in 2024/25.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: This measure looks at the relative socio-economic disadvantage of the municipality as derived from the ABS census of population and housing undertaken every five years.  The relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage in 2024/25 is consistent with results from previous financial years.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: The employee turnover rate has progressively decreased in recent years.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council had 3 operational aquatic facilities during this reporting period. The operating facilities all received a minimum of one aquatic inspection and pool water quality testing.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: There were 449,727 visits to aquatic facilities in 2024–25 in comparison to 387,922 from the previous year. This is an increase of 15.9 per cent and consistent year-on-year as population numbers and visitation rates both increased.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Increases in the cost of utilities and maintenance have had a negative impact on the cost per visit.  The service cost of aquatic facilities increased by 5.7 per cent from the previous period.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: The time taken to action animal management requests has remained consistent at less than two days over the last four years.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: We have worked with neighbouring councils and veterinary clinics to reduce the numbers of animals collected from outside our municipality. Every effort is made to reunite an animal with it's owner.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: We explore all avenues to reunite animals with their owner prior to offering them up for adoption to new owners.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Council consistently provides a cost effective service compared with similar councils. Officers conduct proactive patrols in public places, promote responsible pet ownership and registration, investigate dog attacks and conduct audits on domestic animal businesses.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council successfully prosecuted 4 dog attacks resulting in serious injuries to another dog and 2 dog attacks resulting in serious injuries to a person.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The average time to action food complaints is 2.97 - this is a slight increase from the 2023 reporting period.  There was an increase of 23 cases for investigation received from the previous year and of the total 364 cases, 14 of those had a response time greater than 10 days.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: There were 62 Class 1 premises and 2804 Class 2 premises requiring inspection, all were completed.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The number of food premises has increased, resulting in an 8 per cent decrease in cost of food service as compared to 2023–24.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: All 109 critical non-compliance outcome notifications received a follow up inspection. Four premises ceased trading.

description: Food safety samples
variation: A total of 764 statutory surveillance samples were taken exceeding the requirement of 687.  Of those samples, 72 were from Class 1 food businesses and 692 were from Class 2 and 3 food businesses combined.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council made 142 of its 198 decisions at meetings open to the public. Council made 56 confidential decisions in accordance with the requirements of Section 3(1) of the Local Government Act 2020. This result is a 7.6 per cent increase from the previous year. These decisions are often confidential to protect commercial or private personal information, such as decisions on grants, subsidiary trading results and contract procurement. Where possible Council resolves to make confidential decisions public at a time deemed appropriate.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: This year we achieved an increase of two percentage points, from 58 in 2024 to 60 in 2025. This score remains above the state-wide average and exceeds our target of 58.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance at meetings is consistently high with a  7.14 per cent increase from the previous year. Where Councillors have been unable to attend meetings apologies have been provided.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation:
This result represents a 12.9 per cent reduction in costs per councillor compared to the previous year. The Deputy Lord Mayor position was vacant for four months following the resignation of Lord Mayor Sally Capp on 30 June 2024. During this period, the Deputy Lord Mayor assumed the responsibilities of the Lord Mayor, leaving the Deputy position unfilled. This vacancy contributed to the overall reduction in costs.

However, costs for the City of Melbourne remain significantly higher than those of other councils. This is largely due to the higher allowances set by the Victorian Government for the Lord Mayor, Deputy Lord Mayor, and Councillors in the capital city, compared to those in other municipalities.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Satisfaction with Council decisions remains consistent with the previous year. This rate remains above average against the metropolitan and state-wide averages.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The overall size of the measured collection has increased due to the inclusion of eMagazine titles in this measure. There is however no material change in the percentage of items under 5 years from the previous reporting period.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: No material change in this measure.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Library loans exceed last years reported figure. This is due in part to the inclusion of loans of emagazines.

description: Library membership
variation: This is a 20 per cent increase in total number of registered members since the last reporting period. This is likely in part due to our new library branch "narrm ngarrgu" located in the north of the CBD. It is also of note that the previous reporting period was affected by lower library use due to Covid.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Visitation is tracking in line with the last reporting period. No material change.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Infant enrolments in the Maternal and Child Health service reached 101 per cent, within the expected range.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The cost of the MCH service per hour of service delivered is $89.91, which is within the expected range.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation rates remain stable and within the expected range.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation by Aboriginal children in MCH services increased by 9.68 per cent reflecting improved engagement and access.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Attendance remains strong, supported by outreach to culturally diverse families and continuity of care through a consistent Maternal and Child Health service.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The number of sealed local road requests received during the year has increased by 17.4 per cent compared to the previous year. Due to a large volume of the requests being generated by the community there is some inherent fluctuation depending on the amount of engagement from the community.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The percentage of sealed local roads that are below the intervention level not requiring renewal has decreased in 2024–25 compared to the previous year. This is still within the expected threshold.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Road reconstruction costs have increased by 1.5 percent compared to the last year. This is mainly due to a rise in the schedule of rates from 1 July 2024.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Road resealing cots have increased by 1 percent compared to the last year. This is mainly due to rise in schedule of rates applicable for the new year from 1 July 2024.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Community satisfaction with the condition of sealed local roads is 3 per cent higher than last year. This remains significantly higher than the state-wide average of 45 for this measure.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: A median of 88 days to decide planning applications was achieved for 2024–25. This a slight improvement from 89 days reported for 2023–24. This result is a reflection of improved processes and system upgrades throughout the financial year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: A total of 825 applications of 1091 or 75.62 per cent were decided within the required time frame. This exceeds the set target of 70 per cent for 2024–25. This represents a steady year-on-year improvement over the past 5 years.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost per application of the statutory planning service had shown a 20 per cent decrease from the previous year. Improved operational efficiency and an increase in number of planning applications are the main drivers. However, there were also salary savings due to staff movement. As a capital city council, we assess a substantial amount of planning applications that are referred by the Minister for Planning and Heritage Victoria. Often, these are large scale complex projects requiring considerable utilisation of resources. While these additional expenses incurred are reflected in the overall cost, the referred planning applications are excluded from this measure, resulting in a comparatively higher value.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal reviewed 16 appeals made against council's planning decisions. Council's decisions were upheld in 15 appeals, a 93.75 per cent outcome.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: While overall missed bin collections rose slightly to 2.53 per 10,000 bins in 2024–25 (an increase of 0.21 missed bins), we consistently perform better than the state average for this indicator.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Our kerbside garbage bin collection costs increased by 2.63 per cent this year. While our cost per bin appears higher than other councils, this is largely due to our unique service profile. The cost-per-bin measure doesn't account for differing bin sizes or collection frequencies. Crucially, we serve a higher proportion of apartment buildings, where shared bins receive multiple collections weekly for a lower total number of bins. This intensive service in high-density areas drives our per-bin cost up compared to councils with fewer apartments.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The cost of kerbside recyclables bin collection has reduced by over 18 per cent compared to last financial year. This may be attributed to the expansion of Container Deposit Scheme collection points across the municipality, which reduces our overall processing costs and provides a revenue credit for any eligible containers we collect. As well as a shift in purchasing behaviours of our residents that may be purchasing fewer glass and heavier materials, opting instead for lighter plastic containers. This change reduces the overall weight and processing costs of our recyclables.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Our 2024-25 results are comparable with last financial year, showing a small decrease of 2 per cent. This reduction may also be impacted by a recent influx of Container Deposit Scheme collection points across the municipality and purchasing behaviours of our residents, opting for lighter recycling bottles and containers that may influence overall diversion from landfill percentage. We are reviewing how to more accurately measure behaviour that leads to improved diversion from landfill in 2025–26 and anticipate an increase in diversion as we roll out new programs aimed at extracting organics from more households within multi-unit dwellings.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Expenses increased by 5.9 per cent in 2024–25, mainly in the areas of employee costs, materials and serviices, bad and doubtful debts, and other expenses which related to the write-off of non-capital project costs. Coupled with a slight drop in total assessments, the result was an increase in the expense per property assessment. The 2025–26 budget year will decrease due to expected reductions in materials and services and other expenses, but then increase in the forecast years as employee expenses and depreciation increase.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: A 3.9 per cent increase in general rates and municipal charges in 2024–25 coupled with the slight decrease in total assessments for the year led to a increase in average rate per property assessment. This result will increase over the budget and forecast years as general rates and municipal charges growth will average around 3.5 per cent where assessments are expected to increase by 1.0 per cent year on year.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The ratio improved on prior year due to higher level of cash holdings at 30 June, as current liabilities remained stable. The ratio is expected to improve in the 2025–26 budget year as cash balances improve, but will then dip in the foreast years as trade creditor balances increase.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
The public open space reserve increased during the year as we received contributions but no major acquisitions or spending occurred.  At the same time current liabilities remained fairly constant. This is likely to reduce during the budget and forecast years as we look to acquire property for public open space opportunities.
The current calculation ignores term deposits and pre-supposes that restricted reserves are backed by cash holdings, which is not the case for City of Melbourne. When taking into account term deposits, the City of Melbourne's unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities comes to a positive 42.6 per cent.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The loan balance remained at $110.0 million at 30 June, which was the same level as the previous year and well below the budget of $216.0 million. This was due to below budget capital expenditure related to the postponement of some projects and delays on other projects. Rates revenue will increase over the budget and forecasst years, while loans are expected to increase in the budget year as projects are completed, and then reduce in the forecast years as we pay down balances.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: $115.0 million in loans were drawn and repaid during the year which was well above prior year movement, with the rates base increasing 3.8 per cent over the prior year. There are no long term loan repayments expected in the budget year, but we are expecting to commence paying down the loan balance in the forecast years.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Own source revenue increased by 14.7 per cent during the year while non-current liabilities remained closely in line with prior year. Own source revenue will reduce in the budget year, increase in the first forecast year before dropping back in the 3rd year as a gain from disposal of property comes into play. Non-current liabilities will increase in the budget year due to expected borrowing, and will reduce in the forecast years as loans are repaid.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Asset renewal and upgrade expenditure increased marginally over the prior year, but was below budget by $24.0 million for the year as result of various delays experienced across projects, while depreciaition dropped from prior year. Asset renewal and upgrade expenditure is expected to increase in the budget and first forecast years, while reducing in the last two forecast years. Depreciation is expected to increase across all years.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: An expected surplus over prior year deficit on higher revenue was assisted by higher user fees, monetary contributions (developer contributions) and other income related to dividends received gave a result just below budget. This ratio is expected to reduce next year as total revenue falls back to normal levels before growing again in the following year with further property gains expected.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Total rate revenue increased year on year by 3.8 per cent which was mainly due to general rates charges. However, underlying revenue increased on prior year due to increases in user fees and gains on property disposals. This is expected to reduce in the budget year as total revenue reduces, before increasing in the forecast years as a further gain on disposal of property comes through.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Total rates revenue increased 3.8 per cent year on year and total valuations increased 2.3 per cent which had a negligible result on the ratio over prior year and target. This is expected to stay fairly level over the budget and forecast years.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Population increased by 6.8 per cent on prior year while expenses increased 6.0 per cent thereby giving a slight reduction in the ratio for 2024–25 over prior year. Expenses will reduce in the budget year before increasing over all forecast years.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: While populatuion increased 6.8 per cent over prior year, property valuations increased substantially in 2024–25 as a result of additional cost items included in current replacement costs for infrastructure assets as a result of changes to accounting standard AASB 13, and new unit rates adopted for the valuation of footpaths and roadways. The budget and forecast years will see steady growth on current levels.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The increase in ratio is a direct result of the increase in population of 6.8 per cent. The road length also increased by an additional metre in 2024–25.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in the ratio is a direct result of the increase in own source revenue which grew by 14.7 per cent during the year, which was greater than the increase in population of 6.8 per cent.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in recurrent grants of 15.4 per cent on prior year was the main reason for the increase in rate. 50.0 per cent of the 2025–26 financial assistance grant was received in June 2025.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Same result as prior year.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Resignations were on par with prior year, while total permanent staff increased by 16 FTE over the year.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: One inspection was completed in 2024/25.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Although visitations to aquatic facilities increased during 2024/25, population increased by a greater amount compared to visitations. This contributed to the decrease in the number of visitations per population compared to the 2023/24 result.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Increased attendance numbers combined with lower contract and maintenance costs has contributed to a decrease in the overall value. The decrease has been marginally offset by higher gas costs.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: The average number of days to action Animal Management requests has significantly decreased from 5.49 to 2.41 days for the same reporting period. This significantly improved result is due to being fully resourced with high performing staff executing excellent time management across all of their tasks.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: A slight decrease of 11% in the reclaim of animals for the same reporting period in the previous year. This can be attributed to the cost of living impacting animals being surrendered and impounded animals not being collected.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: An increase of 8.25% from 56.25 to 64.5% is a result of animals being less suited for re-homing based upon behaviour assessments of the animals, covering nature and temperament testing as an example.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The variation is a result of being fully resourced within the service and enterprise bargaining wage increases.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Six matters were heard and all six were successfully prosecuted during the financial year ending 30 June 2025.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The average number of days to action Food Complaints has increased from 1.45 days to 1.92 days. The response has increased marginally over the previous year due to an increase in workload and an increase in unexpected leave from staff.

description: Food safety assessments
variation:
There has been a notable increase in the proportion of Food Safety Assessments conducted over the past two years. The completion rate has risen from 51.89% and 50.89% in the previous years to 63.35% in 2024/25.
This represents an approximate 12% increase in inspections, reflecting a positive trend in our food safety compliance efforts.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Although the number of premises within the Municipality has continued to grow, the decrease in the cost of the service is primarily due to staffing vacancies within the Environmental Health Unit.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: No notifications were received during the financial year ending 30 June 2025.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Food samples are collected for a calendar year. 153 food samples were collected for the 2024 calendar year against 148 food samples as required for the 2024 calendar year, under Section 32A of the Food Act 1984.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: 65/240 decisions were made in closed session which equals 27% of total decisions made at Council Meetings from July 2024 - June 2025, a reduction of 2.17% on the previous result.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The 2024/25 result has marginally increased in comparison to that of 2023/24, and is significantly higher than the set target which indicates the community is increasingly satisfied with Council's community consultation and engagement. Based on results over the past years, the target will be reviewed for future years.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation:
The actuals figure is displaying incorrectly as there were 9 Councillors elected at the general election in 2020, which was applicable for Council Meetings held Jul-Oct 2024. This increased to 10 Councillors elected at the last general election held October 2024; applicable for Council Meetings held in Jan - Jun 2025.
Therefore, 44 Councillors attended Council Meetings for Jan - Jun 2025 out of a possible 50. 
This figure is inclusive of Councillors who did not attend a Council Meeting, but who had been granted a leave of absence by Council for that period.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: This includes costs for 10 councillors following the general election, inclusive of three new Councillors. There has been a small decrease on the previous results.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The 2024/25 result remains the same as results from the previous two years and indicates the community is generally satisfied with the decisions that Council makes.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The result for 2024/25 is similar to that of the previous financial year.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Although the lower ‘service cost’ reported was due to staff vacancies, once these vacancies are filled, the service cost will exceed 2023/24.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: There has been an increase in loans on the same time last year, however, proportionally that increase is lower than the increase in population growth. As a result, the loans per head of population has decreased in 2024/25.

description: Library membership
variation: There has been a relatively small increase in library memberships, primarily as a result of significant population growth across the City.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: There has been an increase in library visitors on the same time last year.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: All birth notifications received have been enrolled in the service with the result being comparable with that in previous years. In addition, some birth notifications were received late in 2023/24 but were not enrolled until the early part of the 2024/25 financial year. This explains the result being in excess of 100%.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The ‘service cost’ has increased by approximately 2.3%, compared to previous results.  Although salaries increased by 3% over the same period, this was reduced by the mix of lower paid graduate nurses.  Notwithstanding this, the overall service cost continues to exceed the funding received

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: The transition from a prioritised service model to a full service delivery model in Maternal and Child Health (MCH) services has led to a notable increase (14%) in participation. 9,612 children attended the MCH service at least once throughout the year and 14,404 children were enrolled in the MCH service.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: This is an increase of 20% from 2023/24 data, and can largely be attributed to the employment of an aboriginal outreach MCH nurse and her work in community outreach, better identification of families and data cleansing.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: The 2024/25 result is a slight increase from the previous financial years, and can be attributed to a return to a full service delivery model in Maternal and Child Health services during the year.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The results for this indicator vary considerably from year to year primarily due to the amount of development across the City. Although the 2024/25 result has decreased from the previous year, increased traffic across the sealed road network, including roads in proximity to land development and heavy vehicle transport corridors results in faster deterioration of the road pavements, continues to be the primary reason for requests to be submitted.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The improvement in this indicator compared to previous years is a great result owing to the greater commitment to the annual re-sealing program.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: No reconstruction work was undertaken in the previous two years and the variation from those years results from work on the Ferris Road-Alfred Road intersection undertaken in the first six months of the 2024/25 financial year. No reconstruction projects were undertaken in the last six months of the financial year.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The renewal projects in 2024/25 were mostly low traffic access street and place road projects, hence the renewal cost and traffic management were significantly cheaper.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Although there has been a slight decrease in community satisfaction, Council has exceeded targets set in our Road Asset Maintenance Contract and has spent considerable capital expenditure on roads to improve the road network.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: There has been a small reduction in the number of median days to determine an application in 2024/25. This can be attributed to growth in the  filling of vacant positions. Although this is an improvement, a large portion of applications required further information and referrals to other internal and external stakeholders before a decision can be made on the application, thereby impacting the overall result.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Reduced processing times over the past couple of years is a result of process improvements. The increase in the time taken to process applications in 2024/25 compared to previous years, is a result of more complex applications being received. Although the result in 2024/25 is an improvement, the amount of growth across the municipality requires Council to review the target for this indicator taking into account that growth.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: Staff costs are reflective of resourcing to keep pace with substantial growth occurring within the municipality, filling of vacant positions and staff training and development opportunities.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: There was a much smaller number of VCAT submissions in 2024/25 compared to previous years. All of the decisions were mediated to the point of consent orders.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: There were 6,026,358 garbage and recycling bins collected across City of Melton households in 2024/25. A total of 95 bins were missed throughout the year (51 garbage and 44 recycling bins), resulting in 0.16 bins missed per 10,000 collections, a significant decrease from previous years.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: In 2024/25, the garbage collection service cost $12.61M, equating to $154.26 per service. Of this cost, 42 per cent is attributed to collection, with 58 per cent attributed to disposal costs. This increase per service since 2023/24 is due to increased disposal costs.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: There are 78,089 households with 819 of these households subscribing to an additional recycling bin.  In 2024/25, the recycling bin collection service cost $4.34M, equating to $56.17 per service in 2024/25. This increase per service since 2023/24 is due to increased disposal costs.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Of the 66,490 tonnes collected through kerbside waste bins in 2024/25, 25,804 tonnes was diverted from landfill, a result of 38.81 per cent. This is a slight decrease on previous years which can be attributed to Council and State Government initiatives resulting in a higher uptake of other waste disposal options. These include the Melton Recycling Facility, free green waste disposal, hard waste collection services and the container deposit scheme with households choosing to return their cans and bottles instead of placing these in kerbside bins.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The 2024/25 movement in this indicator is largely due to unbudgeted maintenance costs that had originally been budgeted against capital expenditure. Council continues to monitor expenditure.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Results for this indicator have been consistent over the past few years. Revenue from rates has remained consistent with the increase in the number of rateable properties within the municipality.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Unfavourable movement in this ratio is largely due to outflows of capital projects delivery and land acquisitions. However the Council's liquidity remains strong in dollar terms and forecasted to remain so with substantial cash reserves in place over the medium term.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The prescribed calculation method excludes term deposits with an original maturity of greater than 90 days that are classified as Other Financial Assets. In 2025 there was a 77.67% improvement in the unrestricted cash ratio. The ratio is forecast to decrease by up an average of up to 74% by 2029. This is  driven by a significant capital works program scheduled over the next four year, totalling $1.2 billion.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council drew down on a loan for Melton Recreation Sports Club construction and  continued to repay borrowings as they mature and has had sufficient cash inflows to achieve this. Council intends to borrow to fund significant infrastructure investment in future years, new borrowings totalling $242.9 million by 2029.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council has consistently met debt obligations as they fell due, supported by adequate cash inflows throughout the period.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Council's Non Current Liabilities continue to remain stable and at manageable levels. The indicator is expected to increase to reflect $242.9 million futures borrowings over the next four years.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Asset renewal expenditure increased in 2025, driven by the delivery of a greater number of capital projects focused on renewing and upgrading Council assets. Actual expenditure remained below target due to the deferral of some works to 2026 and depreciation was $5.5 million above budget.  Looking ahead, Council anticipates significant asset growth over the medium to long term, which will result in higher depreciation charges.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The increase in this ratio during 2025 is primarily attributable to higher income received within the reporting period. The Commonwealth Government paid the full allocation for the 2025 financial assistance grants, along with an advance payment of 50% of the 2026 allocation.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The result for the measure is 2.72% below target and is expected to increase by a further 0.31% by 2029.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: This measure has remained stable over recent years and is a modest decline is expected in future.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The movement in this indicator is largely due to an increase in expenditure. Council continue to manage expenditure prudently despite the high level of growth experienced within the municipality.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Infrastructure constructed within the municipality has and will continue to increase at levels higher than population growth. The ratio is stable compared to prior years.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Results reflects a 17 % increase in population from 206,070 to 241,112 compared to a 4% increase in kilometres of road length. The population density is reflective of the growth in development within the municipality.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Indicator is impacted by fluctuations in populations data and land development within the municipality. There was a 17% increase in population. The result was impacted by significant development contribution income as a result of this development growth.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: This ration has increased as a result of the Commonwealth Government paying the full allocation for the 2025 financial assistance grants, along with an advance payment of 50% of the 2026 allocation.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
