variation: This measure has remained stable over recent years and is expected to remain at the same levels in future.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Staff turnover rate varies from year to year. The rate of 17.20% for 2024/25 remains below the Victorian industry average of 18%.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council's Environmental Health Officers conducted inspections on all council-owned aquatic facilities within the reporting period.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: There has been a modest increase in attendance across the centres, including two standalone seasonal outdoor pools, as well as an overall rise linked to the reopening of the Fawkner Leisure Centre.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The increased costs are primarily due to the reopening and commissioning expenses of Fawkner Leisure Centre, along with a general rise in utility and supply expenses, and the operation of two stand-alone seasonal pools.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: The number of animal management requests received has increased compared to the previous year, 2023/24. As a result, the average number of days between the time taken to action an animal management request has increased. The method of calculating the indicator in 2024-25 is slightly different to that used in 2022-23 and 2023-24, partly explaining the difference in performance. Council is proactively addressing the increased time taken to action requests through a focus on operational efficiencies and a comprehensive review of our practices to ensure improved outcomes moving forward.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: There has been a notable increase in the number of animals collected compared to the previous year due to changes in Local Government Victoria's reporting requirements, which now include all animals impounded by Authorised Officers and an organisation authorised to collect animals on Council's behalf. This includes surrendered, stray and lost animals collected by Authorised Officers and those handed into the pound by members of the public. There has been an increase in the number of surrendered animals due to factors such as financial hardship, and a significant increase in the number of stray, unowned or semi-owned cats brought into the pound. While there is a high chance that dogs are reclaimed, the prospect of cats being reclaimed is low, which impacts the overall reclaim rate for the Council.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: All efforts are made to reunite animals with their owners before offering them for adoption or transferring them to rescue groups. While this measure focuses on animals adopted directly from the facility and does not include animals transferred to rescue groups, it is important to note that the wat djerring Animal Facility has agreements with over 50 rescue groups to rehome animals. These partnerships provide animals with a better chance of finding a new home through reputable rescue organisations with a broad community reach, which is a positive outcome for animal welfare. Within this reporting year, 259 animals were transferred to rescue groups, representing 33% of unclaimed animals.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Council continues to refine the delivery model for animal management services. Service improvements and efficiencies have reduced overall service costs.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Of the three matters presented for the magistrates court, only one matter was not found in favour of the Council and struck out by the Courts.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The average number of days to respond to food-related complaints has improved from 2.18 last year to 1.92 this year. Council continues to prioritise reports from the public regarding food safety matters.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Action taken to address non-compliance has resulted in improved assessment completion rates from 71.7% in 2023/24 to 96.34% for this reporting period.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Council has undertaken a review (in alignment with the guidelines) which has noticeably increased the number of premises now included in the reporting, through the incorporation of Class  4 and temporary and mobile premises notifications.   There has also been some increase in the amount of fixed Class 1, 2 & 3 premises which is also influencing the results, as the overall cost of service is now more accurately distributed across a much higher number of premises.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: 100% of major/critical notifications were followed up and remain above the statewide Council average, reflecting a commitment to ensure business associated with high risk is inspected and monitored.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Council achieved the required target during the reporting period.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: A total of 260 decisions were made at Council meetings held in the last financial year, with 13 decisions being made in meetings closed to the public (primarily due to being commercial in nature). This is an increase over previous years' results; however, the result is well below the expected range for this indicator and continues to demonstrate transparent decision-making in line with the definition of confidential information in the Local Government Act.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The score for Merri-bek in 2025 is 68 which is consistent with our score in 2024 (69). This is the second year that Metropolis has completed the Customer Satisfaction Survey for Merri-bek. Metropolis' reporting typically shows a decline in outcomes across other Councils. As this was not found in Merri-bek, this is a very positive result.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance at meetings remains consistently high. When unable to attend, Councillors have provided apologies, and their absences have been acknowledged accordingly.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The increase can mainly be attributed to the Victorian Independent Remuneration Tribunal's determination to increase allowances.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The score for Merri-bek in 2025, at 69, is consistent with the result of 68 obtained in 2024. This finding suggests that satisfaction ratings are stable.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Investment remains consistent in collections purchased within the last five years. The slight decrease is due to the longevity of our digital materials, which increased considerably during the pandemic.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: A minor increase in the cost of library service per population is related to increased costs of goods and services but the increase of .89% is below the  Consumer Price Index (CPI)

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Merri-bek Libraries continued to expand our Library of Things this year, offering board games for loan supported by regular events at libraries. We also increased our investment in eaudiobooks available on the Libby platform, with simultaneous use licenses allowing more borrowers to access popular titles.

description: Library membership
variation: Merri-bek Libraries’ Makerspaces provide a range of equipment for library members to use for a wide range of creative activities. New regular programs like drop-in social sewing sessions and introduction to 3D printing have been very popular and contributed to a small growth in membership this year.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Visits to Merri-bek Libraries increased this year along with our population. We increased the number of adult programs on offer with additional Chatty Cafés, Conversation Clubs and the introduction of the regular LGBTIQA+ event Lee’s Café providing a variety of ways for the community to connect with each other.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Enrolments continue to be high and consistently greater than 100 per cent due to the number of babies for whom the Council do not receive a birth notice; yet, the baby is enrolled in the service following notification that they have relocated to Merri-bek. Enrolment numbers demonstrate ongoing demand, a high level of awareness and engagement with the Maternal and Child Health Service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Council investment in M&CH has increased as has enrolments and community need.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation in Merri-bek’s Maternal Child Health service remains consistent, reflecting the value the community places on early support for families, anticipatory guidance, assessment, and referrals.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Council has seen a decline in the overall participation rates of Aboriginal families choosing to participate in our service over the last financial year. The Maternal Child Health department is working collaboratively with local Aboriginal Community-controlled Organisation to increase participation through program trials such as ‘Welcome Baby to Country’.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation in the 4 week Key Age and Stage (KAS) consultation remains high at 88.45% reflecting strong community trust in the value of the Maternal Child Health service and importance families place on early support and connection.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Sealed road requests have decreased over the past two years due to maximising the council-allocated budget and a proactive maintenance program.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The percentage of sealed local roads below the intervention level has decreased slightly compared to previous years' results; however, the results remain within the expected range for ageing assets.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Road reconstruction costs in 2024-25 were slightly reduced due to more efficient project delivery, including well documented designs, going to tender earlier in the year and along with the larger than usual program it enabled competitive prices.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The increased cost reflects the current inflation as measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and the construction industry, as well as the higher level of patching being undertaken before resurfacing.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: The score for Merri-bek in 2025, at 68 maintains the better performance achieved in 2024 compared to prior years. This continued improved result is due to significant investment made in road reconstruction and resurfacing throughout Merri-bek.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The median days have decreased notably over the past few years, indicating improved performance, partly supported by a reduction in planning permit applications.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The urban planning service has now returned to a high level of performance following disruptions caused by the pandemic. The percentage of decisions made within the statutory timeframe has increased from 58.10% in 2021-22 to 76.73% being in excess of averages for inner and middle suburb metropolitan Councils.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: Despite reduced application numbers,  reduced relative value of applications lodged, and planning reform resulting in high value applications being lodged with the Minister for Planning, all resulting in reduced fee revenue, there has been an 22.68% reduction in the cost of the service due to associate adjustments in resourcing.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Results are reflective of good decision-making and the change in Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (VCAT) practices leading to an increased number of consent orders following mediated outcomes.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Missed service collections have remained stable, even with the addition of the expanded 4-bin service to our reporting framework for all waste streams for this reporting year. The majority of missed collections have occurred with the weekly general rubbish service. It’s also important to note that leniency is generally applied to this waste stream when the customer does not put the bin out on time, and this is reflected in the data, which is counted as a missed collection according to the guidance on this metric. .

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The increase in costs is mostly attributed to an increase of 2.7% in the waste levy and a 2.8% increase in tonnes of waste to landfill.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: For 2024/25 the cost is calculated as all recycling costs divided by number of glass and comingled recycling bins while the 2023/24 and prior year figures were calculated as all recycling costs divided by number of comingled recycling bins only.  While the variance has the appearance of being a significant reduction in cost per bin, it is actually largely unchanged.  It would represent a 6.3% cost increase year on year if the same methodology was applied.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: While the Council introduced weekly Food Organics and Garden Organics (FOGO)  collection in the previous reporting period and has included glass diversion in this report period, ongoing community education and engagement efforts to achieve the desired results from kerbside waste reform are still a work in progress. This includes a trial of fortnightly rubbish that is occurring in 2025-26 aimed at landfill diversion.  Council remains committed to refining our services and continuing community engagement to improve waste diversion outcomes.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Expenditure between 2023-24 and 2024-25 has increased by 9.3% or $21.4 million while the number of property assessments has only increased by 1.8%. The increase in expenditure is primarily related to a $9.5 million increase in loss on disposal of assets (this occurs when an asset is sold for less than the assets recorded value or an asset is demolished as a part of capital works upgrade works and still has a recorded value) and workforce changes such as enterprise award increases in alignment with the rate cap, workcover premiums, and an 0.5% increase to the superannuation guarantee.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: This result has decreased slightly due to the nature of properties being processed through supplementary valuations in 2024/25. These properties were slightly below the average capital improvement value within the municipality, resulting in lower average rates charges against the municipality average.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: While current assets increased by 1.1%, current liabilities also rose by 14.1%. Council's current liability increased primarily as a result of a loan repayment of $12.1 million which is due in 2025-26.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The indicator has increased in the current year from -64% to -21%, primarily due to a $13.7 million increase in cash and cash equivalents. This reflects a higher cash position held by Council at 30 June in line with projected cash flow requirements.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: This is a positive decrease as Council has reduced its debt relative to rate income. The improvement is due to the payment of principle repayments throughout the year and no new borrowings. Loan borrowings reducing by 6.51% while rate revenue increased by 5.32%. The improvement in the forecast period is primarily due to the repayment of the Local Government Funding Vehicle (LGFV) bond ($12.1 million) when it falls due in June 2026 and no new borrowings until 2028-29.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The ratio increased marginally from 1.11% to 1.44%, reflecting a $750k (36%) increase in loan repayments. This is as a result of movement in the Reserve Bank Australia (RBA) cash rates, resulting increases to our variable rate loan repayments. The ratio increases by almost 6% in 2025-26 due to the repayment of the Local Government Funding (LGVF) bond when it falls due in June 2026.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Non-current liabilities reduced by $12.1 million in 2024-25 as a result of the loan repayment moving to current liabilities. This resulted in a significant decrease in non-current liabilities relative to own source revenue.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The ratio decreased marginally, indicating Council continues to invest in renewing and upgrading assets at a rate well above annual depreciation. The small reduction reflects a minor shift in capital expenditure timing but still demonstrates a strong commitment to asset sustainability, remaining as low-risk as per the Victorian Auditor-General Officer (VAGO).

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The adjusted underlying surplus increased from 7.91% to 8.82%, reflecting an improvement in Council’s operating position. This is primarily due to the early payment of the 2025/26 financial assistance grant.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The ratio decreased slightly primarily due to the timing of receipt of the Commonwealth Financial Assistance grants for 2023-24, 2024-25 and 2025-26. It is anticipated that the timing of these payments will return to the usual pattern in future years, resulting in the increase shown in the forecast period.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: The result achieved remained consistent.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Expenses per head of municipal population increased from $1,264.88 to $1,343.62, primarily due to a $21.4 million (9.34%) rise in overall expenses from 2023/24 to 2024/25. The key contributors were a $3.9 million increase in employee costs, an $8.6 million rise in materials and services, and a $9.5 million net loss increase on disposal of property, plant, infrastructure, and equipment.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Infrastructure per head of municipal population decreased by 15.24%, primarily due to population growth and a significant decrease in the value of property plant and equipment, as a result of a revaluation.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The result of this indicator has remained consistent.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The result of this indicator has remained consistent.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population increased by 50.89%, driven by an approximate $9 million increase in recurrent grant funding between 2023/24 and 2024/25. The increase is primarily due to the Commonwealth Financial Assistance grant, with 100% of the 2024-25 allocation and a 50% advance payment of the 2025-26 allocation received in 2024-25. By contrast, the 2023-24 grant was received in advance in 2022-23, resulting in a lower result in 2023-24.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The result of this indicator has remained consistent.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Staff turnover experiences yearly variations influenced by a broad range of factors. Council continues to focus on reducing turnover through various means, including strengthening staff engagement and enhancing recruitment and retention strategies such as flexibility, leave options and employment policies.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: All aquatic facilities received an inspection by an authorised officer in 2024-2025 in line with the Public Health and Wellbeing Regulations. In addition to this, the aquatic facilities operator undertakes regular water quality sampling as per their water quality risk management plan.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Continued investment by operator to provide programs that are required and in demand by community has led to increase in usage.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: This years result is in line with the target and consistent with last years result. Council continues to action animal management requests within 24 hours.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Council will continue to encourage animal registration and microchipping to assist to have animals reclaimed by their owners.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Only animals deemed suitable for rehousing can be rehoused under the Domestic Animals Act. The reduction in this years result is due to the number of suitable animals received for re-housing.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: This increase is reflective of the direct costs to provide the animal management service.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: There were no animal management prosecutions in the previous financial year 2023/24, there was 1 Major Dog Attack prosecution in May 2025.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Response time to deal with food complaints has improved due to improved staffing levels.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Council continues to prioritise food safety assessment inspections, improved staffing numbers have assisted with this positive result.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: This is reflective of the direct costs to provide the Environmental Health  service. Much of the increase in costs is due to staffing levels getting back to full capacity.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Council has maintained its approach to major and non-compliance outcome notifications. As more food safety inspections were undertaken there were more non-compliances that required follow up.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Food samples obtained exceed the statutory target number pursuant to the Government Gazette.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: The number of council decisions made in confidential is directed by the subject matter of the reports. This will vary from year to year.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Council continues to explore better communication channels and effective engagement methods.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillors who formally submit an apology for a meeting are not deemed absent for the purposes of this measure.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The reduced cost of elected representation was due to a reduced number of Council Meetings during the election period.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Council continues to work on its engagement strategies and transparent decision-making processes.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Council is committed to the renewal of its library collection. In 2024-2025 the major supplier of Council's library materials sold to another company and this changeover slowed the purchase of new materials.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Council provides a wide range of library services, including five branch libraries, one library agency and a rural outreach library service delivering services to three remote, sparsely populated small townships. The breadth of this service impacts on the cost effectiveness, combined with the Mildura branch opening seven days per week and servicing interstate customers.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Loans includes e-books and e-audiobooks and physical materials.

description: Library membership
variation: Library Membership is continuously decreasing and increasing due to new members joining the Libraries as well as old memberships being cancelled due to inactivity after 3 years.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: In person visits to Library Service sites have increased from the previous year. This can related to increased use of Library services by the community such as computers and Wi-Fi and Library programs.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: This figure has remained steady and is over 100% due to out of state clients entering the Victorian service. This occurs frequently given Mildura’s close borders with New South Wales and South Australia E.g. some infants that are born or live outside of Victoria (who we do not receive birth notices for) have been enrolled into our service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: There is a slight reduction in cost of the service due to  FTE available in service,  generally steady and consistent.

description: Participation in the MCH service
Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: The slight decrease may be attributed to the employment of a Maternal and Child Health Nurse by the Mildura District Aboriginal Service, which may have influenced service utilisation at Council’s Maternal and Child Health service.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: The slight decrease may be due to clients accessing Maternal and Child Health services through the Mildura District Aboriginal Service. Ongoing monitoring and analysis will continue to ensure service accessibility and engagement.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The increase in requests is due to the inclusion of kerb and channel requests in 2024/25 which weren't included in previous reporting periods.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The cost of sealed local road reconstruction varies depending on the extent of reconstruction required and the type of road being rebuilt. i.e. residential streets require less effort to rebuild than a collector road.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Cost of materials have increased and the majority of works this resealing season occurred in urban areas which is less efficient as compared to long straight reseals in the rural areas.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The Statutory Planning team has recruited staff and streamlined processes which has contributed to a decrease in the amount of time taken to decide planning permit applications.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The Statutory Planning team has recruited staff and streamlined processes which has contributed to applications being decided within Statutory timeframes.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Council had nine permit applications determined at VCAT in 2024-2025 with one set aside. In the previous year Council had  five permit applications determined at VCAT and all five were upheld (not set aside).

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: This increase is due to bins being collected by external contractors (rather than council) and it takes time for the drivers to learn the collection routes. The addition of the glass bin service has also added to the increase.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The increase in landfilling costs are due to an increase in the landfill levy fee for waste and cover material and also an increase in collection costs.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: An increase in costs is due to an increase in recycling processing costs including the gate fee and transport costs after re-tendering the services and the cost of an additional, fourth kerbside bin service for glass.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: There has been a decrease in diversion percentage due to the contamination rates in the kerbside recycling bin increasing. This has lowered the tonnes being diverted from landfill.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Expenses for financial year 2024-25 are consistent with budget with little to no variance.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Rate increases continue to be managed within the State Government's rate cap.  Whilst there has been increased number of properties and development in Mildura, Council's costs continue to rise and be impacted by external events.  Council continues to seek a balance between the demand for services and infrastructure, and the communities capacity to pay.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: This result is influenced by carried forward capital works and increased large-scale projects spanning multiple financial years.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: There has been an increase in unrestricted cash held due to the timing of the forward payment of the Financial Assistance Grants.  The majority of council cash is held in long term deposits which are no included in cash calculations for this variance measure.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council has no new borrowings forecast and is concentrating on paying off existing loans.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council has continued to make normal repayment of existing loans with loan balances continuing to decrease, with rates increasing each year this will cause a variance year to year.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Council has continued to make normal repayments of existing loans with no new borrowings forecast.  Future provision calculations have also reduced due to decrease in CPI.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Mildura Rural City Council aims to have a ratio of around 100% which means we are adequately maintaining our current asset base. This will vary over time and can be influenced by factors such as external funding.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: For 2023-24 the Victorian Grants Commission reversed all previous forward payments of financial assistance grants with no forward payment made in June 2024 making the payment instead in July 2024. They have now forward paid 2025-26 grants in June 2025 which has created an artificial surplus result which varies to the prior year.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The timing of financial assistance grants from the Victorian Grants Commission varies this measure from year to year as it changes the underlying revenue amount.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: The trend has increased slightly due to the higher increase in some property values. Mildura Rural City Council will tend to have higher rates compared to property values due to the inherent factors of our municipality such as population size, population density, population dispersion and remoteness from a major population centre.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: This trend has been increasing due to the sustained high inflation rate and increase in the cost of living currently being experienced.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Council continues to invest in the community with further development in community assets e.g. early learning centres.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Mildura Rural City Council's population of 57,626 compared to its extensive road network of 5,090 kilometres across 22,080 square kilometres (largest council in land area in Victoria), results in a low population density per length of road.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Like many Councils, Mildura Rural City Council is severely dependent on revenue derived from our rating revenue.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The Victorian Grants Commission forward paid financial assistance grants for 2025-26 making payment in June 2025.  The grant is recognised in the year received which creates large variance.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Mildura Rural City Council is the fifth most disadvantaged local government area according to the 2021 SEIFA index. Only four of the seventy-nine Council's in Victoria are more disadvantaged than Mildura Rural City Council.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Our staff turnover rate has returned to previously reported levels, after a few years (in particular 2022-23) affected by Council transitioning out of Aged and Disability Services.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: There were 6 health inspections during 2024/25 financial year across 5 aquatic facilities. These consisted of annual water quality testing for each aquatic facility, as well as food handling and Worksafe.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Mitchell Shire has a municipal population of 56,384 people who made 250,337 visits to aquatic facilities during 2024/25 financial year. This represents a 64% increase year on year in participation across the leisure service. Council completed several significant capital works to enhance the experience at our facilities, including a new boiler at Kilmore Leisure Centre, significant pool shell rectification works at Seymour War Memorial Outdoor Pool, a group fitness room upgrade at Seymour Sports and Aquatic Centre and a new Virtual Group fitness offering at both Kilmore and Seymour indoor facilities which has increased the number of Group fitness classes from 40 per week to over 160 per week

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The direct cost of aquatic facilities is reducing in line with the increase in attendance/utilisation of our facilities.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Whilst the number of customer requests received Council have increased, we are now capturing all Animal Management requests types. This data now provides a true indication of all Animal Management requests received by our Local Laws department.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Council promotes responsible pet ownership encouraging all pet owners to register and microchip their pets. This aids in returning found animals to their owners.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Council continues to rehome as many dogs and cats as possible and developing strong and ongoing relationships with rehoming organisations is important.  Council's local cat rehoming facility was closed for most of the 2024/25 FY. Council made efforts to rehome through other facilites.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: While the costs to deliver the animal management service has stayed relatively steady, with the growing population within our municipality, this reflects an overall increase to the cost per population for this service.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: Council had 1 animal management prosecution for the 2024/25 financial year compared to 9 animal management prosecutions in the 2023/24 financial year. The reduction compared to previous years as a result of fewer serious dogs attacks.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Our Environmental Health Department action all food complaints within a maximum of two working days of receipt of a complaint.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: Our Environmental Health Team consistently achieves a result of 100% for all reporting periods.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The 15% increase in environmental health costs is primarily due to an upgrade in software during the year, and enhancement of data collection programs. Additionally, CPI and superannuation rates, and expenditure in relation to the education and engagement of the onsite waste water management plan have driven direct costs to increase.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: Our Environmental Health Team consistently achieves a result of 100% for all reporting periods.

description: Food safety samples
variation: The 2024 food sampling program consisted of 4 rounds. Each round focused on a different high-risk procedure, product or premises.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council makes very few decisions at meetings closed to the community. The small number that remain confidential related to private commercial information and Council business information (CEO recruitment matters).

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: The Customer Experience Strategy recently endorsed by Council and Community Engagement Policy have been updated to focus on increasing engagement with community in the future.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance has been high and is a reflection of the level of engagement of the predominantly new Councillor group. Council meetings are conducted in a hybrid fashion, maximising participation opportunities.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost for elected representation is predominantly made up of councillor allowances which are set by and applied in line with the Victorian State Government.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Council makes decisions in the best interests of the community, of which not all will satisfy community expectations, however as elected representatives it is their role to make these decisions.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation:
Mitchell Shire Libraries continue to commit to a modern and relevant library collection which reflects our communities' expectations to access current and high-quality material and resources. With the available budget, robust outsourcing of shelf-ready items and obtaining the most competitive pricing for the purchasing of resources, we have been successful in sustaining the standard of the collection at an acceptable level.
The 2024/25 financial year result reflects the entire library collection, including print material (books, magazines, serials, maps, plans, manuscripts) and physical audio visual or digital materials (CDs, DVDs) and toys and games on hand that can be reserved or borrowed.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The cost of library services per head of population reflects the ongoing commitment to deliver library services.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Our libraries had 147,782 loans during the reporting period (physical Items 128,624 and eResources 19,158) from a population of 56,384

description: Library membership
variation: Mitchell Shire Council Library Services maintains 10,042 registered library users from a population of 56,384. A currently registered member has a recorded transaction during the past three years.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Mitchell Shire Council Customer and Library Service Centres recorded a total of 10,042 library visits during the 2024/25 financial year from a population of 56,384. The Mitchell Shire libraries are a co-located with our Customer Service Centres and it is likely that this combination of services has inflated our result.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Mitchell Shire Council MCH service maintains high levels of engagement however, it should be acknowledged that participation in the MCH Service is not compulsory, and some families may seek physical and developmental assessments from other services, including GPs and Aboriginal Health Services.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: Council provides a lean and efficient service while meeting the nurse/child ratios of 1:130 as agreed to in the Enterprise Bargaining Agreement.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: This percentage only reflects those children due a KAS consultation within the reporting timeframes. For this number to be accurate, a full 12 months numbers must be recorded. Participation in the MCH service is not compulsory and some families choose to attend other services for their health and developmental assessments, or they may also choose not to have their children assessed as they have no concerns around their children's health and development.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation:
This percentage only reflects those children that are due a KAS consultation within the reporting timeframe. For this number to be accurate, a full 12 months must be recorded. There are 18 months between 2yr and 3.5yr KAS assessment, and some Aboriginal children will not need to engage with the MCH Service at this time. Some children are in Out of Home Care or have moved out of the region, however their client file remains with Mitchell until they engage with another Local Government MCH Service.
Mitchell MCH continues to achieve high participation rates with our community.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation in the MCH service remains steady for the 2024/25 FY.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: 4.17% decrease in requests from 2023/24, from a peak during the 2023/24 financial year the number of roads requests increased considerably due to the widespread and significant weather events in 2024, with a 173% increase recorded in January 2024 alone.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Sealed road condition audits are conducted every 3-4 years and are due again in 2025/26 financial year. Council regularly reviews the condition data, prioritises and rectifies the roads that are below the required standard with the budgeted funds available.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: 10.48% increase on cost due to include a full road reconstruction and CPI increase. Our Roads Maintenance team collect customer request data through a search of the corporate recordkeeping system, using keyword search terms and the date range for the financial year.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: 30.48% increase in cost due to CPI increase and trialling a new product to correct road shape without having to do a full reconstruction. Costs to re-seal sealed roads during 2024/25 financial year includes resurfacing (spray seal or asphalt) and minor pavement patching.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: The reduction in satisfaction level could be contributed to the significant increases in maintenance and construction costs resulting in a reduced work scope. Further to this, the floods in October 2022 and January 2024 had a significant impact on the condition of our roads. While we have made considerable progress in repairs, these events have required additional resources, affecting other planned road projects.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: Council has experienced one of the biggest growth increases in new planning permit applications. This trend has continued throughout the year with only December 2024 and January 2025 giving some reprieve.  Resourcing numbers remained during this period meaning officers could not deal/cope with the growth in application. The organisation has quickly moved to address this problem by approving additional staff resources for the planning team. Recruiting for the new positions was not finalized until June 2025 when new staff members joined the team. The backlog created is reflecting the decline in number of applications processed within statutory days. The benefits of additional resourcing should begin to be seen in the 2025/26 financial year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames

The organisation has quickly moved to address this problem by approving additional staff resources for the planning team. Recruiting for the new positions was not finalised until June 2025 when new staff members joined the team. The backlog created is reflecting the decline in number of applications processed within statutory days.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: in 2022/23 7 of 12, in 2023/24 4 of 8 in 2024/25 8 of 15 were upheld / varied / affirmed / not appealed or remitted. As in previous years, any change in the results of such a small number of decisions has a significant impact on our % result

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The four bin system commenced in March, and the full impact of the change of service structure is not yet truly reflected in the annual diversion rate.

description: Expenses per property assessment

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Council's current year result of $1,946.90 is reflective of the mix of income that makes up rates and charges income and the spread based on valuation changes.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Current assets will sufficiently cover Council operations and current liabilities. The result is significantly impacted in 2024/2025 due to an decrease in non-current investments which is not included within the ratio.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
Council's Unrestricted Cash does not take into account $85M held in investments. Also, based on definitions per LGPRF, we have included trust funds and deposits and unspent grants within unrestricted cash, however this is also included within current liabilities therefore influencing the result. Adjusting for both of these, Councils unrestricted cash ratios would have been a positive result of 84.86%.

The above also explains why Council is forecasting a negative unrestricted cash for the 2025-2026 Budget year and following three financial years. Within Council's adopted annual budget document, a different calculation method is used for the unrestricted cash ratio.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council's current year's result of 26.29% is within Council's expected range for this measure as per the Council's Loan Borrowing Policy, which is 0 – 60%. 2024/25 actuals are lower than target as Council did not draw down on borrowings as planned due to reduced borrowing requirements.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The 5.57% is within Council's expected range for this measure as per the Council's Loan Borrowing Policy, which is 0 – 10%. 2024/25 actuals are lower than target due to slightly increased rates and charges revenue and reduced principal and interest repayments.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Council's current year's result of 38.4% is within Council's expected range for this measure of 0 – 70%, and is consistent with prior year results. Councils indebtness ratio of 38.42% has increased inline with an increase to both employee and landfill restoration provisions.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation:
Satisfaction with Council’s decision-making in the interest of the community rose marginally this year, up one per cent to 7.2 out of 10, maintaining a ‘good’ rating and matching the 2025 Metropolitan Melbourne average. 

Except for the unusually low result in 2022 – a year of particularly poor satisfaction with local government post-pandemic-satisfaction with Council’s decision-making has remained stable around the long-term average of 7.2 out of 10 (‘good’) since 2016.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The library team delivered a range of initiatives this reporting period to enhance customer access and satisfaction with library collections. description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Cost of the service is tracking to target and reflects the value and resources required to deliver effective library service to the Monash community. description: Loans per head of population

Library service now has access to a new tool to accurately measure active membership for the last three years, including event attendees.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The number of visits to our libraries increased by 11% from previous year. This measure reflects not only borrowers of our collections, but also participants in programs that did not require bookings, students studying and casual visitors etc. The total library visits were affected by two temporary closures at the Wheelers Hill and Mount Waverley libraries, due to shelving improvement works.

description: Participation in the MCH service

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Maternal and Child Health staff continue to provide a culturally sensitive service. Participation increased from 75.47% in 2023/24 to 81.65% in 2024/25, successfully meeting the target set. Council's Maternal and Child Health Service continues to strive to be inclusive and welcoming, addressing the needs of First Nations and culturally-diverse families.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation in the Maternal and Child Health service remains on-par with the 2023/24 result of 96.10%. This attendance reflects families who initially engage with the service upon birth at Monash, but subsequently relocate to other municipalities shortly thereafter.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The number of local road requests has remained consistent compared to the previous reporting period.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Programming of road resurfacing has increasingly been better planned at Monash. Council's continuous commitment to invest in the renewal of its road network has kept the number of roads classified as "very poor condition" to a low number.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: Council places a strong emphasis on working with applicants and objectors to achieve good outcomes in planning determinations.
