Whilst this has increased the median days, it has reduced the number of appeals to VCAT.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Council is committed to working with applicants and objectors to achieve good outcomes in planning determinations, hence additional time may be taken in negotiating land use and development issues. Council continues to review processes in view of continuous improvement and efficient decision making.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The continued decrease in the number of applications received, combined with the uptake of new and existing State Government approval pathways, has had an impact to the cost of service.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The number of matters progressed to VCAT was relatively low, largely attributed to negotiation during the assessment process to achieve good outcomes for all parties. The increase in decisions upheld reflects effective decision making and engagement with parties during the compulsory conference process, where consent can be reached between parties if possible.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: There has been a significant reduction in the number of missed bins following Council actions to uphold the collection contract conditions. The contractor is now performing well, achieving almost 50% below the allowable missed bin target.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: There has been a cost increase from the 2023/24 period which can be attributed to the increased EPA levy. However, Council is still performing well, through maintaining a high level of diversion from landfill and reduced contamination, resulting in costs landing below target.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The cost of recycling has remained fairly stable from the 2023/24 financial year.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: There continues to be a strong level of diversion from landfill, despite the removal of several Tonnes of recyclable materials through the Container Deposit Scheme (CDS). Had these items remained in our waste streams, our diversion rate would be much greater.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Total expenses increased by 2.2% compared to the previous year. This outcome was primarily influenced by several factors. Employee costs rose as a result of new budget initiatives and an increase in fixed-term staff associated with the Suburban Rail Loop Authority (SRLA) and other programs; these costs will be reimbursed by the respective organisations. Additionally, the statutory Superannuation Guarantee Levy increased from 11% to 11.5%. Material and services expenses also grew, largely due to the transfer of expenditure from the capital works program that could not be capitalised as assets. Depreciation further contributed to the increase, mainly reflecting the full-year impact of asset capitalisation from the 2023/24 capital works program.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The rate cap of 2.75% declared by the Minister for Local Government was applied to the 24/25 rate charges.  This resulted in an increased ratio of 2.6% which includes 1.1% growth from new property assessments.  Future trends are indicating similar level increases with a projected growth in rates and fluctuations in CPI levels.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The higher liquidity ratio compared to the prior year is driven mainly by the value of assets held for sale. Council’s budgeted liquidity ratio included a higher level of assets held for sale which are now expected to occur in the future.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The variation in this ratio is due to a $22M increase in cash held in short-term deposits (with maturity dates of 90 days or less) compared with the 2023/24 level. This change is primarily the result of proceeds from asset sales, mainly associated with land acquisitions proposed as part of the Suburban Rail Loop Authority (SRLA) Project. These proceeds are committed to fund the proposed Glen Waverley Library project. Although these funds contribute to a favourable variance for the unrestricted cash position, they are committed and quarantined within a discretionary reserve. If discretionary reserves are taken into account, Council’s unrestricted cash position remains consistent over the next four years.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council achieved a debt free status as at 30 June 2015. Future year budgets may require borrowings for various capital works projects. However, this will be considered and decided upon by Council at the appropriate time.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council achieved a debt free status as at 30 June 2015. Future year budgets may require borrowings for various capital works projects. However, this will be considered and decided upon by Council at the appropriate time.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The growth in own source revenue is driven by sale of Council land, higher statutory fees and fines as well as increased user fees at our leisure and aquatics centres.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The reduction in this ratio compared to the previous year is attributable to increased depreciation expense resulting from additional assets being recorded, as well as a slightly lower Capital Works Program than the prior year. Council's expenditure on renewal and upgrades continues to exceed asset depreciation. Notwithstanding these variances, Council consistently maintains expenditure levels significantly above state averages, thereby supporting ongoing investment in asset renewal and upgrades.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
The improvement in the adjusted underlying surplus result is mainly attributed to the timing of Council asset sales. The sale of assets significantly affected the variance, along with the Council receiving 50% of 2025/26 Financial Assistance Grants in advance; as these grants have no associated obligations, grant income was recognised in 2024/25. Increased statutory fees and user fees also contributed to the favourable income result. These factors were partially offset by higher employee cost provisions, increased insurance premiums, Workcover expenses, depreciation, and the reclassification of certain capital expenditures as operating expenditures due to not meeting the capitalisation threshold. 
Although the council projects an underlying deficit of ($1.16m) or (0.49%) for 2025/26, this remains within established risk parameters. The council maintains sufficient liquidity to fulfill its financial commitments, and its operational position currently reflects a moderate surplus. Projections suggest that the underlying position will become positive from 2027/28 onwards. Revenue generated through asset sales is classified as an exceptional occurrence and may result in significant fluctuations in the underlying position during applicable periods.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: While Council rate revenue increased by 2.75% which includes growth in supplementary rates, adjusted underlying revenue increased by 22.3% mainly due to land sale income has caused the ratio to decrease.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: The variance reflects Council’s capped rate increase of 2.75%, alongside a 10% average increase in property values across Monash, as part of the 2025 revaluation.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The ratio rose 0.5% from 2023/24, with total expenses up 3.4%. Council implemented an EBA increase and hired temporary staff, partially funded externally. The Superannuation Guarantee Levy increased to 11.5%, and depreciation grew due to asset capitalisation from the 2023/24 program. Future costs are expected to rise with CPI.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The infrastructure asset class increased compared to 2023/24 primarily as a result of revaluation adjustments. This outcome was influenced by amendments to accounting standard AASB 13, as well as the completion of major projects within the 2024/25 capital works program.  Furthermore, the municipality recorded a population growth rate of 2.7%.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The increment is due to an increase in population and the road length used to calculate this KPI has decreased as a result of improved data quality.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The growth in own source revenue primarily resulted from the sale of Council land, a significant one-off event that substantially affected Council revenue. Furthermore, statutory fees and fines increased, alongside higher user fees at leisure and aquatics centres as operations returned to greater capacity.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The higher result for 2024/25 is mainly due to Commonwealth Financial Assistance grant funding received in 2024/25, which increased significantly compared to the previous year; a 150% increase over the 2023/24 allocation. This includes the full amount of the 2024/25 grant funding, including an advance payment of 50% of the 2025/26 allocation. Roads to Recovery funding increased in 2024/25 under a new funding agreement covering the period 2024 to 2028, which provides an increased annual allocation to Council. The municipality recorded a population growth rate of 2.7%.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: A score of 9 suggests City of Monash has low levels of disadvantage.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Council experienced 114 terminations of permanent employees within the 2024/25 financial year. This was a reduction of 21 terminations against the previous financial year. Of the 114 terminations, 108 were voluntary, including eight retirements, which is an increase of eight when compared to the previous year. Of the six non-voluntary terminations, this is made up of four dismissals and two redundancies.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Inspections were conducted in accordance with statutory requirements and Council's program.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: There was a combined 988,539 aquatic visits to Ascot Vale Leisure Centre (AVLC), Keilor East Leisure Centre (KELC), and Queens Park Swimming Pool during 2024-25. Visitation at AVLC was 8% higher and KELC was 11% higher compared to 2023-24.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: While additional patronage has increased income, this has been offset by higher utility costs which has caused an overall increase in the cost of delivering the service.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: There has been a slight increase in the time taken to action requests. As Council maintains an excellent response time that is in the lower end of the range, this is reflected as a large shift proportionally. Efforts continue to be made to enhance processes to further improve response times.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: There was a very slight decrease in the percentage of pets reclaimed, but overall a similar result to Council’s historical trend.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: There has been a significant uplift in the number of rehomed animals, largely due to the exclusion of unregistrable animals from the indicator’s calculation methodology.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The 4% increase is primarily driven by rising costs associated with the animal pound, which makes up the majority of the service expenditure. This adjustment reflects general inflationary impacts over the period.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: No animal management prosecutions were undertaken during  2024-25.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Council maintained a strong response rate to food safety complaints.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: All scheduled food safety assessments were successfully completed during the reporting period.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: Council maintained a service that is more cost effective than the municipal average as a result of enhancements to improve the efficiency of the food safety service.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: All major and non-compliant follow-up inspections were successfully completed.

description: Food safety samples
variation: The legislated target for food sampling was fully achieved.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: While Council aims to have matters heard publicly wherever possible, the number of confidential decisions has increased due to several CEO employment and Remuneration Committee matters, as well as additional VCAT Planning and Property matters, and the appointment of Committee members. These were all confidential as defined under the Local Government Act 2020.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Council actively engaged with the community on a number of important initiatives, including deliberative engagement going beyond the minimum requirement for the development of new strategic plans. Community satisfaction has increased, exceeding the set target.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance at Council meetings remained high. Council's meeting frequency changed from fortnightly to monthly in the 2024-25 year.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The slight increase is attributed to the mandatory requirement under the Local Government Act 2020 to provide annual professional development training.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Community satisfaction with Council decisions has increased markedly which is attributed to improved engagement and governance practices and improved transparency through open communication.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: Continued increasing costs of collection items has seen a drop in the number of physical items that are able to be purchased over the last 5 years. The current overall collection size increased slightly in preparation for the reopening of the redeveloped and enlarged Ascot Vale Library in July 2025.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Cost of library service remains consistent with previous years.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Despite limited operations of the Ascot Vale Library during temporary relocation for redevelopment works, loans have increased across the year, especially via digital platforms. In 2024-25, digital magazine loans were approved for inclusion in the calculation, contributing to the increase.

description: Library membership
variation: There was an increase in registered members accessing digital services during the past three-year reporting period. Results reflect a change in reporting for the digital platform Overdrive, which resolved an error accessing user data, which was unavailable in 2023-24.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: The library service continued to be well used by residents. The smaller, temporary Ascot Vale Library pop-up attracted less use but increased visitation at Avondale Heights Library (12% increase), and Flemington Library (up 12.5%) maintained a stable visitation rate. Visits to the Niddrie and Sam Merrifield branches remained stable.	The Ascot Vale Library pop-up was temporary to allow for the main site to be redeveloped, which will provide a larger area and more resources when reopened.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Infant enrolments in the maternal and child health service remained steady. All infants are enrolled from birth notifications received. The result was greater than 100% due to a number of infants enrolled in the service following notification by domiciliary services and families moving into the municipality.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: There were slightly less hours of service delivery due to staff leave that was unplanned and could not be replaced with relief staff. Building and maintenance costs were slightly higher than in previous years. Onboarding of new team members and increases in the Enterprise Agreement have contributed to higher costs.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Participation in the maternal and child health service remained consistent, with a marginal decrease from 2024 participation. Families that missed appointments were proactively contacted to reschedule appointments. Staff vacancies were able to be more readily filled.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Participation in maternal and child health services among Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander children was strong. The service fosters an inclusive environment where families feel safe identifying as Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander. Culturally appropriate support options are promoted with families, such as playgroups.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Participation in the four-week Age and Stage visit remained strong. Effort is made to maintain consistency in nursing staff from the initial home visit through to the four-week appointment which fosters strong relationships and trust, and this contributes to continuity of service use. The number of babies eligible for the four-week Age and Stage visit is influenced by several factors, such as infants experiencing extended hospital stays and occurrences of stillbirths.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Improvements to data collection and service delivery processes identified new data that had not previously been included in this calculation. As a result, the rate of sealed local road requests now shows an increase, however Council continues to operate an efficient service representing effective action on local road improvements.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: All local roads were maintained to condition standards, ensuring that none fell below the renewal intervention level.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The increase can be attributed to two major road reconstruction projects being undertaken during 2024-25. The road reconstruction works included underground storm water drainage, kerb and channel, road pavement and driveway crossovers.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Roads were prioritised for resealing in accordance with Council's Road Management Plan and Asset Management Plan. In order to improve the quality of road services over a longer time, new treatment options were trialled.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Council's focus on undertaking quality road repairs over the recent years has influenced improvement in community satisfaction.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The efficiency gains that have been made through system improvements, and the time taken to decide planning applications was maintained.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Decisions were made on 786 applications (both Standard and VicSmart applications). Over 97% of VicSmart applications and over 80% of Standard applications were decided within the assessment timeframes of 10 and 60 days respectively, maintaining Council's strong performance and continuing the trend of improved service.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The decrease can be attributed to the temporary reduction in staff numbers.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Of the 25 matters reviewed by VCAT, 15 were resolved through party consent. This high rate of resolution highlights the ongoing success and effectiveness of the mediation program.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: The rate of missed bin collections decreased year-on-year. Council consistently worked with the contractor to identify the causes of missed collections and implement proactive measures to further improve collection services, such as driver training.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost of the landfill collection service decreased. This can be attributed to a reduction in the amount of waste sent to landfill, despite an increase in the number of households requiring bin collections. The low wholesale price of diesel fuel also positively impacted the cost of the service.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: A reduction in recycling waste disposal costs as well as a decrease in the amount of recycling waste contributed to the decreased cost of the service. The community's continued interested in the Container Deposit Scheme has positively impacted the reduction in recycling waste.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: The rate of waste diverted from landfill decreased slightly year-on-year. This was achieved through less waste being sent to landfill, and more food and green organic waste. The reduction in recycling waste is attributed to growing community participation in the Container Deposit Scheme.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The variance is due to minor increases in the cost of materials to deliver on infrastructure renewal.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The variation is due to an increase of $4.90 million in the general rate revenue compared to a minor increase in the number of property assessments.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The increase is due to reduction in current liabilities for trade and other payables.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The variance is due to a reduction in current liabilities amount for trade and other payables.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The variation is due to borrowings of $12.8 million proposed as part of the 2024-25 budget not being used. The projected forecasts indicate Council's intentions to borrow funds for capital work projects.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The variation is due to borrowings of $12.8 million proposed as part of the 2024-25 budget not being used. The projected forecasts indicate Council's intentions to borrow funds for capital work projects.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The variation is predominately due to an increase in user fees and other income. The projected forecasts indicate Council's intentions to borrow funds for capital work projects.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The variation is due to the actual spend for renewal and upgrade $8.1 million higher than the 2023-24 amount.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The variance is predominately due to an increase in user fees ($2.8 million) and operating grants ($5.0 million). The majority of the operating grants increase was due to receiving $2.56 million of the 2025/26 allocation in advance.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The variance is due to an increase in the underlying revenue amount for user fees, operating grants and other income compared to 2023-24.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: The minor variance is due to the percentage increase in property valuations less than the percentage increase in rate revenue.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The minor variance is due to the increase in population being greater than the increase in costs to deliver services.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: The variance is due to the decrease in valuation in the infrastructure asset class for recreational, leisure and community facilities.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: The increase in population has resulted in a higher population density per length of road.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: The variation is  predominantly due to an increase in own-source revenue for user fees and other income.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The variance is due to the timing of the receipt for the Special Purpose Financial Grants. In June 2025, Council received $2.5 million (50%) of the 2025-26 grant prepaid which meant the total received for 2024-25 was significantly higher than 2023-24.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: This measure is from the Census and so is unchanged since the 2021 survey. The result indicates that, at a municipal level, Moonee Valley is relatively advantaged compared to other council areas. However, there are areas within Moonee Valley that have greater disadvantage, including lower household incomes, less / lower qualifications, and more workers in unskilled occupations. Council makes efforts to lessen the impact of disadvantage on these communities in its service delivery.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: The 2024-25 turnover rate of 14.11% is a slight increase on 2023-24. The result represents a stable and healthy level of staff movement that falls within our average historical range.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: The increase in utilisation of local aquatic facilities was due to an increase in the average temperature during the summer months and the pools being open on more days and for longer hours.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Last year’s figure was impacted by staffing and resourcing constraints, which led to extended response times across the reporting period. This year, those constraints were not present for the majority of the period, allowing for a significantly improved response time. The improvement also reflects ongoing enhancements to internal processes and procedures that have supported more efficient service delivery.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: The decrease in reclaim rates this year is broadly consistent with the trends observed in the previous period. A large proportion of impounded animals continue to be cats, including a high number of feral cats, which are far less likely to be reclaimed. This is due in part to low microchipping and registration rates in cats, which remains a key challenge for councils across the sector.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: While this year’s rehoming figure is lower than the previous year, it is still higher than earlier reporting periods and reflects consistent work by Council to promote rehoming outcomes. Council continues to support animal rehoming through partnerships and targeted adoption campaigns.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
Animal management prosecutions
variation: Two dog attack prosecutions initiated in 24/25 with the result not as yet determined through the court system.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: The increase in the average number of days to action food complaints, from 2.84 to 4.94, reflects the need to prioritise complaints based on potential public health risk. During the reporting period, more urgent or high-risk matters were prioritised for immediate attention, which resulted in lower-risk complaints being actioned closer to the end of the SLA timeframe. Importantly, the average response time remains within the 5 working day SLA, and complaint management continues to meet required service standards.

description: Food safety assessments
Cost of food safety service
variation: The previous year incorporated all the Class 4 notified businesses but after reading the reporting guidelines it seems we were to only include ones that were added (notified) through the reporting period and not existing ones. (they don’t expire).

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: The decrease in the percentage of critical non-compliance outcome follow ups from 80% to 63.64% is primarily due to the timing of non-compliance identification. The majority of major non-compliances were identified towards the latter part of the reporting period, which meant that follow-up actions and notifications could not be completed within the same reporting timeframe. These were followed-up in the next reporting period.

description: Food safety samples
Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: The majority of decisions made at meetings closed to the public have been tender evaluation and contract related.  These tend to be periodical in nature based on contract renewal periods.  There have been fewer this financial year as a result.

description: Participation in the MCH service
Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: An increase in the participation rates of our Aboriginal families is a result of additional support provided through our Enhanced and Universal MCH service.

Sealed local road requests
variation: Council’s sealed road network has performed better than previous years for various reason, although climatic conditions, specifically the much lower the average rainfall, is the predominate factor.

variation: This year's result is comparable to the previous results, excepting last year's result was an Outlier and above expectations. This year's figure is in line with industry expectations.

variation: VCAT : % varies due to an improvement of application which were decided in Council's favour. Due to low numbers a small difference does have a major impact on the result.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The significant increase in 2025/26 is due to Council paying back the principal of $4.5 million of an interest only loan drawn down in 2015/16.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Non-current liabilities have decreased from last financial year due to a significant portion of interest-bearing liabilities moving to current liabilities. Council has an interest only loan that will mature early in the 2025/26 financial year.

Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: Improvement mainly due to Council receiving 50% of the 2025/26 Grants Commission allocation in the 2025/26 financial year.

The 2024/25 allocation was not paid forward. Therefore, significantly reducing recurrent grant income in the 2023/24 financial year.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
Percentage of staff turnover
variation: The reduction in turnover is primarily due to Council’s ongoing work in establishing itself as a destination employer. Attraction and retention efforts, coupled with a powerful employee value proposition (make working with us work for you) has resulted in the continual evolution of professional and leadership development platforms, progressional opportunities, reward and recognition incentives and flexible work arrangements. The result is more of our people electing to stay with Council and pave a rewarding career.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Authorised officers conducted sixteen inspections to ensure a clean, healthy and safe environment across Council's three aquatic facilities.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Utilisation of our aquatic centres has remained consistent with previous years post COVID, signalling strong and consistent attendance numbers at our centres.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: The higher cost-per-visit ratio is mainly due to increased expenses associated with essential upgrades and renewal work at the Pelican Park Aquatic Centre. Additionally, we faced higher operating costs for pop-up gym facilities that were opened in Hastings while Pelican Park was closed. We also experienced increased management fees to cover Belgravia for any loss of earnings during the closure, as well as higher utility costs at Yawa Aquatic Centre.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Council continues to prioritise animal management issues that pose a high risk to protect the safety of our community and to act promptly on animal management requests. Our time taken to action requests was consistent with 2024. The increase from 2023 can be attributed to a 73% rise in requests.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Of the 839 animals collected, 388 were reclaimed by their owners, representing an increase of over 100% compared to the previous year. When pets cannot be reunited with their owners, Council makes every effort to rehome them.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: The increase in the number of animals rehomed from 2022 is primarily due to a change in the calculation, which now excludes reclaimed animals from the number of animals collected. With more pets unclaimed this year, we had more opportunities to rehome pets via our Pets Available for Adoption webpage, PetRescue and social media. Of the unclaimed pets collected, we were able to rehome over half.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: The increase against last year is primarily due to the addition of superannuation, increased contractor costs to cover staff who went on annual leave and an increase in salaries and wages as we filled permanent positions. With our new system, superannuation can now be split out from payroll costs and allocated to a service where previously it was lumped together in a general ledger.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: The Mornington Peninsula Shire Council is dedicated to safeguarding community safety and the health and wellbeing of animals on the Peninsula by pursuing prosecution only when it is justified and necessary. There was one instance where we chose to withdraw legal action against a defendant due to exceptional circumstances presented by the defendant's barrister in court.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: Council is committed to prioritising food complaints and taking action as quickly as possible. This year’s result is consistent with previous years.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: To ensure the safety of residents and visitors to the Peninsula, Council aims to inspect and approve every food premise before they start trading. This year’s result is up from previous years as only a very small number of newly established premises that began operating in late 2024 were not assessed by the end of the year.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: The increase over last year is primarily due to the addition of superannuation in the cost of the service and increased contractor costs to cover annual leave and long service leave. With our new system, superannuation can now be split out from other payroll costs and allocated to a service where previously it was lumped together in a General Ledger. The overall decrease in the cost of the food safety service from 2022 is due to the realignment of internal costs, which now reflect the resources allocated to our food safety service.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: To ensure the safety of our community, we continue to prioritise food safety compliance programs across the Peninsula. Our follow-up on critical and major notifications result remains high and is consistent with previous years.

description: Food safety samples
variation: Council is dedicated to ensuring food safety across the Peninsula. In our ongoing efforts we obtained 304 food samples for analysis, over the 263 food samples required. This proactive approach underscores our commitment to maintaining high standards and protecting public health.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Council aims to support transparency and openness in the community by minimising closed-door decision-making. During the period, nineteen decisions were made in meetings closed to the public. Ten of these decisions related to personal information, seven to private commercial information, one to Council business information, and one to legally privileged information.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Satisfaction with consultation and engagement remains steady at 66, compared to 67 recorded in June 2024. The upward trend in our results over the past two years marks a significant improvement from our unusually low score of 50 in 2023. We have raised next year’s target to reflect the new trend.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance at council meetings remains high, as Councillors are dedicated to representing their wards and constituents. However, attendance has declined since 2022 due to our transition back to in-person meetings and an increase in unplanned meetings.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The total cost of elected representation decreased from last year, mainly due to lower consultant fees. The increase in costs since 2022 is primarily due to higher Councillor allowances, which are established by the Victorian Independent Remuneration Tribunal.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Satisfaction with council decisions remains steady at 62, compared to 64 recorded in June 2024. The upward trend in our results over the past two years marks a significant improvement from our unusually low score of 46 in 2023.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The decrease against last year is primarily due to a 10 per cent reduction in the number of items purchased in the last 5 years, as we sought to constrain costs across the organisation. We also had a minor decrease in the number of items in our overall collection as we weeded out titles that were not used or had reached their end of life.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The increase over last year is primarily due to the addition of superannuation and increased casual costs to cover staff who went on annual leave and long service leave. With our new system, superannuation can now be split out from payroll costs and allocated to a service where previously it was lumped together in a general ledger.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: This year's results are primarily due to a 10 per cent increase in the number of items loaned, which can be linked to a slight rise in library membership relative to the overall growth of our municipal population.

description: Library membership
variation: This year's result reflects a slight increase in library membership compared to the overall growth of our municipal population.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: This year's result is due to a 10 per cent decrease in library visits compared to last year, after accounting for the growth in our municipal population.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Although we continue to experience high engagement results with our service within the community, which underscores the importance of maternal and child health services for families with children aged 0 to 6 years, we have seen a decrease in the number of infants enrolled. This decline is attributed to receiving fewer birth notifications than in previous years.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The increase against last year is primarily due to the addition of superannuation and an increase in normal salary and wages to cover staff who went on annual leave and long service leave. With our new system, superannuation can now be split out from payroll costs and allocated to a service where previously it was lumped together in a general ledger. The decrease from 2023 is mainly due to a reduction in casual Maternal Child Health salaries and wages to cover permanent staff on leave and less venue and equipment hire.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: We are pleased to see participation increasing, surpassing previous years. This rise is largely due to more children attending the Maternal Child Health (MCH) service at least once during the year, even though fewer children were enrolled in the year compared to previous years, and the introduction of Saturday appointments for working parents. This increase in attendance can also be attributed to the Shire’s MCH service proactively monitoring attendance for Key Age and Stage appointments and reaching out to clients to book overdue appointments.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: This year’s rise in participation is primarily due to a twelve per cent increase in enrolments against last year, although we did see higher attendance rates as well. This increase in attendance can be attributed to the Shire’s MCH service proactively monitoring attendance for Key Age and Stage appointments and reaching out to clients to book overdue appointments. Compared to 2023, we have experienced a six per cent rise in enrolments with fewer attendances, which explains the decrease from 2023.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Community participation in the Shire’s Maternal Child Health 4-week Key Age and Stage visits remains high and is comparable to results in previous years. We continue to monitor client attendance and actively contact clients who are overdue on their Key Age and Stages appointments. Additionally, for ongoing visits, we now ensure continuity of care by having the same nurse who visits the client’s home also see them at the centre, as far as reasonably possible.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: This year we received a total of 1,948 requests, which represents a 44% decrease from the 3,475 requests we received in 2023. However, this year's requests represent a 27% increase compared to last year. The rise in requests corresponds with the insights gathered during our community engagement for the 'Integrated Transport Strategy', and the results from our 2025 Community Satisfaction Survey. The community expressed concerns around road quality and maintenance response times. We hope to address these concerns with a 72 per cent increase in funding for our 2025-26 road renewal program.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Of the 1,380 kilometres of sealed local roads across the Shire we maintained 1,365 kilometres within condition standards, beating our target for the year.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Road rehabilitation works started in January and were finished by April 2025. There were no road rehabilitation works in the previous year as resources were redirected to our Road Resealing Program for 2023-24.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: This year’s cost is consistent with that of the previous year. Road resealing works started in January and were finished by April 2025. There were no works undertaken in 2022 as resources were focused on the Shire's Road Rehabilitation program (sealed local road reconstruction).

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads has remained steady at 53, compared to a score of 56 recorded in June 2024. The upward trend in our results over the past two years marks a significant improvement from our unusually low score of 38 in 2023. While this progress is encouraging, it also highlights the potential for further improvements, which we hope to see as a result of our 72 per cent increase in funding for our 2025-26 road renewal program.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: While we received 8 per cent fewer applications than last year, additional labour resources and process improvements have contributed to shorter processing times of applications.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Although we received 8 per cent fewer applications, we have made steady progress by increasing the number of planning applications decided within the required timeframes, bringing us closer to our target.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The increase against last year is primarily due to the addition of superannuation and increased agency labour costs to cover staff who went on annual leave and long service leave. With our new system, superannuation can now be split out from payroll costs and allocated to a service where previously it was lumped together in a general ledger.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The decrease from 2023 is mainly due to a change in 2024 in how the measure is calculated - it now includes Consent Orders, which is a decision or direction by VCAT that gives effect to an agreement that has been reached between parties.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Over the past year we completed more than 7.9 million bin lifts, but we unfortunately missed 4,304 bins as part of our collection service. This increase from last year can be partly attributed to having fewer bins in our system, due to the transition to a newer database, which meant that there were fewer bins collected. We will continue to work with our provider to ensure that all bins scheduled for collection are picked up.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: Although the result remains consistent with previous years, the direct cost of the service has decreased from last year. However, bin maintenance is now included in the contract bin lift rates, which experienced a 3.8% increase, resulting in a similar kerbside garbage bin collection cost.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The increase in the cost of the service since 2024 is due to an adjustment in the old contract and the commencement of a new contract in May 2024, which introduced new rates with bin maintenance now included in the contract bin lift rates. Additionally, there has been a 3.6% increase in the number of kerbside recyclable bins collected.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: We experienced a small decrease compared to 2024. A 5.4 per cent reduction in recyclables received, compared to 2024, may be due to the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme that was introduced on 1 November 2023.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Council always seeks to deliver the highest quality services in the most efficient way and has contained expenditure per property assessment through its goal of ongoing savings. The increase in 2025 is largely attributable to CPI, indexation of the Shire's contracts and depreciation.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Council's rates, our primary source of income that delivers essential services to our community, is limited by the rate cap under the Fair Go Rates System, which was set at 2.75% for 2024-25. Supplementary income is 0.9% of the total rates revenue in 2025, which is the growth of our rates income in 2025.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Our current assets have increased by $10 million from 2024 due to cash and cash equivalents, with delayed expenditure for capital works. There has been a decrease in working capital due to Current Liabilities increasing $6 million, due to trade and other payables increasing along with unearned income.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: To maximise the return of funds to Council, we allocated cash to term deposits. The term deposits are classified as Other Financial Assets, with a total of $100.6 million recorded as of June 30, 2025. It's important to note that if these term deposits were considered Cash and Cash Equivalents, the Unrestricted Cash Ratio would have been 99%, resulting in a positive outcome.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: No new loans were drawn during the year. With $2.7 million in loan repayments and a rate revenue increase, we have managed to reduce our loans and borrowings compared to our rates.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: This year’s result is reflective of our reduced loans and borrowings and an increase in rate revenue. Borrowings originally planned for 2025 have been deferred to 2026, resulting in a future increase from 2026.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Council's long-term liabilities remain stable, indicating an appropriate level of debt. We experienced a slight increase in non-current liabilities, which was offset by a 2% rise in own source revenue. This increase in revenue is attributed to a higher rate income, increased usage of waste collection services and an increase in infringement income, despite a 9% decrease in the waste service charge.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: Council continues to invest in community infrastructure and focus on our renewal gap, ensuring that we are spending enough to maintain or replace assets to the desired standard. Asset renewal and upgrade are driven by the condition assessment of assets and varies year on year.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: While the decline from 2024 is due to an 8 per cent increase in expenditure that was only partially offset by a 3.3 per cent increase in income, the outcome does reflect the long-term challenges of Local Government. With rate capping, cost shifting from the State Government and the rising costs of materials and service delivery, we face a challenging economic environment. These issues are expected to have a significant impact on us in the coming years.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Rate revenue continues to be Council's main source of income for funding community services and capital works projects. Own source revenue is inflated in 2025, due to receiving the full year allocation of financial assistance in 2025 along with 50% of the 2026 allocation.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Property values increased this year on the Mornington Peninsula, however were below the rate cap.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Council always aims to provide high-quality efficient services while controlling expenditure through ongoing cost savings. The 2025 increase is primarily due to the Consumer Price Index (CPI), contract indexation, depreciation and increased staff costs.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Although our municipal population has seen a slight increase, our investment in infrastructure has been primarily affected by the delivery of our Capital Works Program ($52 million) and the revaluation of infrastructure assets.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: A small rise in the municipal population has resulted in a slight increase against last year.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Our growth in own-source revenue aligns with our budget expectations and has been driven by the rate cap and supplementaries throughout the year. Our own-source revenue is higher than usual due to Council receiving the full allocation of financial assistance in 2025, in addition to 50% of the allocation for 2026.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Recurrent grants saw a significant increase in 2025 due to the receipt of the full year allocation of financial assistance grant in 2025, along with 50% of the allocation for 2026.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Mornington Peninsula's Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage Index from the 2021 Census was in the eighth decile. The first decile indicates the most disadvantaged and the tenth decile indicates the least disadvantaged.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Our turnover rate is slightly higher than last year but remains steady and consistent with trends observed over the past three years. This figure encompasses all types of turnover, including the conclusion of temporary contracts, involuntary terminations, and redundancies. Notably, this year we saw an increase in retirements as well as staff relocations, both within the state and to other regional areas of Victoria.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: All pools were inspected and water quality tested during the financial year.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation: Strong attendances (26% increase compared to 2023/2024) were achieved for the 2024/2025 pool season, which was the highest since 2018/2019. It also had the largest attendance by schools in over a decade. There were minimal closures due to cold weather with consistent days of warm weather, which helped keep attendances high.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Due to constant warm temperatures across the pool season there was minimal closures due to cold weather. This helped achieve our highest attendance rate since the 2018/2019 pool season (26% increase compared to 2023/2024). This has helped offset the cost of pools.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: All Animal Management requests are actioned within 1-2 business days.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: There has been no real change in number of animals reclaimed, compared to 2023/2024. However, happily, there has been a 44% decrease in surrender of dogs by the public, as well as a 21% decrease in surrender of cats by the public, when compared to 2023/2024.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Adoption/rehomed rates decreased by 13%, compared to 2023/2024, while collection numbers dropped by 12%.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: Costs have increased by 19%, compared to 2023/2024, with recruitment to fill vacancies in Local Laws now completed.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: No animal management prosecutions were undertaken for 2024/2025.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation: All food complaint requests are actioned within 1-2 business days.

description: Food safety assessments
variation: All high-risk setting food premises were inspected in 2024. The overall inspection program was impacted by reoriented priorities, a demand in other Environmental Health functions (including an increase in unsewered development in the municipality) and complaints requiring intricate investigations. In addition reduced staffing which has now been recruited for.

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: While there has been an increase of 4% in the number of registered premises, there has been an 8% increase in the cost of providing the service compared to 2023/2024.

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation: No critical or major non-compliance outcome notifications about a food premises were received during 2024.

description: Food safety samples
variation: 100% of the required food samples were obtained and tested in 2024 (47 Class 2 + 5 Class 1).

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Four resolutions were passed at closed Council meetings.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Council has worked hard to increase community consultation and engagement using a number of strategies including: media, briefings and streaming of Council Meetings on YouTube. Result has improved compared to 2023/2024 and exceeded our target by 1.92%, as well as exceeding the state-wide average by 6%.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Seven Councillors were absent on eight separate occasions.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: Service costs increased by 3% compared to 2023/2024, while the number of councillors increased by 14% (1) after the VEC review of electoral ward boundaries.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: A great result exceeding the state-wide average of 49 and reflecting how Council has endeavoured to raise public awareness of its decisions, meetings, and activities through media releases, live streaming of Council Meetings to the internet, and actively encouraging community involvement in projects via our online community engagement platform.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: 40% decrease in library collection items purchased compared to 2023/2024.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Cost of library services increased by less than 1% compared to 2023/2024.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Loans increased by 14% compared to 2023/2024.

description: Library membership
variation: Registered library users increased by 5% compared to 2023/2024.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Visits increased by 30% compared to 2023/2024.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: All births were enrolled in the Maternal and Child Health service.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: There has been a 10% decrease in Maternal and Child Health nurse hours worked and a 6% decrease in service costs, compared to 2023/2024.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Attendance increased by 2.4% while enrolments decreased by 2.7%, compared to 2023/2024.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Low numbers of Aboriginal children enrolled (16.5 in 2021/2022, 19 in 2022/2023, 17 in 2023/2024, 21 in 2024/2025), can skew the percentage change between years.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Includes more children born in June 2024 who became eligible for a 4-week key age and stage visit during 2024/2025 than June 2025, which caused the result to exceed 100%.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The number of local road customer requests received in 2024/2025 decreased compared to 2023/2024. This supports the continued consistent customer satisfaction survey result received.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: The majority of sealed local roads are considered to be in good condition. Result is stable compared to 2023/2024 and is within 0.18% of target set.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Road reconstruction unit rates vary depending on a number of factors, including project value, specifications, road length, width, and depth, material types, design criteria, and other related works such as minor drainage, shoulders, cut and fill, and resource availability. As a result, a rural council cannot avoid price fluctuations in road reconstruction projects. Small projects are extremely expensive, which could be attributed to the project's scope and increased demand for civil contractors. Costs decreased by 40% compared to 2023/2024.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Unit rates for road resealing are determined by a number of factors, including seal designs, road lengths, widths, aggregate sizes, material types, road functionalities, associated preparation work, available resources, and so on. As a result, a rural council cannot avoid price changes for road resurfacing projects.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: A consistent result exceeding the state-wide average of 45. The 2024/2025 survey showed continued stability in customer satisfaction, which is also reflected in less customer requests received. Council is working hard to provide good services to residents.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: Due to the changeover of our system, an accurate calculation could not be provided this year.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Due to the changeover of our system, an accurate calculation could not be provided this year.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: Cost of services and planning applications received both increased by 4% in 2024/2025, compared to 2023/2024.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: There were seven VCAT decisions made during 2024/2025, with four being upheld by VCAT. Numbers of appeals made to VCAT were five in 2023/2024, five in 2022/2023 and three in 2021/2022.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Kerbside missed bin collection requests decreased by 16% compared to 2023/2024 with the commencement of new kerbside collection contractor from 01 October 2024.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: From 1 Oct 2024 new kerbside collection contract commenced with a term of 7 years with new rates. 2024/2025 result includes 3 months of previous contract rates as well as 9 months of the new contract rates. There has been an 11% increase in the costs of collecting and processing general waste, compared to 2023/2024.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: From 1 Oct 2024 new kerbside collection contract commenced with a term of 7 years with new rates. 2024/2025 result includes 3 months of previous contract rates as well as 9 months of the new contract rates. There has been no change in the costs of collecting and processing recyclables, compared to 2023/2024.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: 2.66% increased general waste compared to mixed recycling collected from households, when compared to 2023/2024. Diversion of glass from mixed recycling stream due to Victoria's Container Deposit Scheme (CDS) and separation at the transfer station. Result is below target by 4.11 points.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
There has been a 8% increase in expenses in 2024/2025, compared to 2023/2024. This is partly made up of the recognition of net loss on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment (PIPE) $1.8M greater than in 2023/2024 year as capital works are completed. Many key strategic decisions were made during 2024/2025 including a change in organisational structure to respond to the growing demands of the community, a commitment to continue delivering aged care services and the ongoing introduction of new obligations from the Federal Government as part of doing so (including delays in our ability to commence providing some services while being required to make upfront investments in them), and our success in securing a contract to provide Municipal Building Services to a neighbouring Shire and the increased cost (and income) associated with that.
Forecast results expect an average increase of 2% on the 2024/2025 result, with property assessment numbers expected to remain stable. 
2024/2025 result is within 6.53% of target set.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation:
Increase is in line with the Fair go Rate System (FGRS). 
Forecast results are conservatively averaging 2.1% rate caps, with property assessment numbers expected to remain stable.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Current assets increased by 30% with the opening of several new term deposits held due to the receipt of 50% brought forward payment of the 2025/2026 Federal Assistance Grant and other grant funds received. Current liabilities increased by 49% at 30 June 2025 with the receipt of grant funding for the Castlemaine Art Museum redevelopment project and the resulting recognition of the unearned grant income liability. The ratio decreases in forecast years to reflect the reduction in term deposits held. Result is within 6.37% of target.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
The definition of cash excludes term deposits invested for periods of more than three months. If term deposits held for more than three months but less than 12 months were included as cash, the unrestricted cash ratio’s would be 106.99% in 2024/2025, 17.05% in 2025/2026, 30.02% in 2026/2027, 26.18% in 2027/2028 and 16.69% in 2028/2029.
Carried forward capital works are unknown and not considered in future years.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: No new loans occurred during the year, while principal repayments were made across the year that reduced the level of borrowings. One new loan ($1.4M) is currently budgeted for in 2025/2026, while one existing borrowing will be paid out ($1.2M). The ratio decreases in the following forecast years to reflect the reduction in loan borrowings as loans are paid off.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Two loans were paid off during 2021/2022 year, including one that required a $1M balloon repayment, and two loans were paid off in 2022/2023 year. No new loans were drawn down in 2024/2025. One new loan ($1.4M) is currently budgeted for in 2025/2026 with one loan to be paid off ($1.2M) during 2025/2026. The ratio decreases in following forecast years to reflect the reduction in loan borrowings as loans are paid off.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Own source revenue increased by 2.63% in 2024/2025. The continuation with declining own source revenue growth is expected in forecast years due to rate capping, while non current liabilities are expected to increase in 2025/2026 with new borrowings of $1.4M, before reducing in following years with borrowings being paid off.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation:
Asset upgrade works decreased by 54% and asset renewal works decreased by 9%, while depreciation expense increased by 7%, compared to 2023/2024. Council's focus is on investing in accordance with our adopted asset management plans (where they exist) and therefore the extent to which this ratio reflects our performance is questioned. Forecast years include the grant funded Frederick Street / Mechanics Lane Redevelopment in years 2025/2026 and 2026/2027.
Result is within 8.82% of target.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
Overall, income was 9% greater than budget, while expenses were 5% greater than budget.
In terms of income, 50% of the 2025/2026 Federal Assistance Grant (FAG) was paid early in 2024/2025 ($3.5M).
In terms of expenses, net loss on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment (PIPE) were greater than budget by $1.2M consisting of recognition of written down value (WDV) on asset capitalisations and increasing operational service costs, while depreciation was greater than budget by $809K due to higher than budgeted EOFY revaluation adjustments reported for 2023/2024. Costs are forecast to rise faster than revenue in subsequent years.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Total rate revenue (including waste charges) increased by 4.9%. The adjusted underlying revenue increased by 24.4% in 2024/2025, due to 50% of the 2025/2026 Federal Assistance Grant (FAG) being paid early in 2024/2025 ($3.5M). This increase in grants is not expected to continue in forecast years. Result is within 12.0% of target.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Capital improved values (CIV) have increased at a greater rate than rates revenue, and is expected to continue in forecast years.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: Net loss on disposal of property, infrastructure, plant and equipment (PIPE) were greater than budget by $1.2M consisting of recognition of written down value (WDV) on asset capitalisations and increasing operational service costs, while depreciation was greater than budget by $809K due to higher than budgeted EOFY revaluation adjustments reported for 2023/2024. Many key strategic decisions were made during 2024/2025 including a change in organisational structure to respond to the growing demands of the community, a commitment to continue delivering aged care services and the ongoing introduction of new obligations from the Federal Government as part of doing so (including delays in our ability to commence providing some services while being required to make upfront investments in them), and our success in securing a contract to provide Municipal Building Services to a neighbouring Shire and the increased cost (and income) associated with that. Population growth remains low.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: $23.0 million (net) in asset revaluations/indexations were undertaken on buildings, roads, bridges, drainage, kerb and channel, footpath, land and site improvement asset classes in 2024/2025. In addition, capital works to maintain the Shire's assets and infrastructure continue in line with the long term Financial Plan, with the assistance of grant funding, while population growth remains low.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Population growth remains low (1%) with minimal change in length of local roads.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Own source revenue has remained stable compared to 2023/2024. Population growth continues to remain low.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: An increase to recurrent operating grant funding occurred in 2024/2025 with 50% ($3.5M) of the estimated 2025/2026 allocation of Federal Assistance Grant (FAG) funding brought forward into 2024/2025, while population growth remains low.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Socio-economic disadvantage index remains stable compared to prior years.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Resignations/terminations decreased by 19.87% in 2024/2025 compared to 2023/2024. An error was identified in calculating staff turnover in 2021/2022 whereby casuals and fixed term resignations/terminations were included. The 2021/2022 result should have been 22.83%.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Due to reduced availability of EHO resourcing, focus was diverted to completing statutory workload (food premises inspections). Under the Public Health & Wellbeing Act 2008, Council may inspect aquatic facilities for compliance with the Public Health & Wellbeing Regulations 2019, whereas under the Food Act 1984, Council has a statutory obligation to inspect registered businesses. Council is committed to ensuring that all aquatic facilities are inspected in the next reporting period. The facility was inspected for regulatory compliance of chemical treatment and safe service delivery.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation:
Nillumbik residents diverted 15,000 tonnes of recycling and green waste from landfill through the kerbside bin service in 2024-25. 
 
The diversion rate is slightly lower than last year, primarily due to a decrease in recycling and green waste tonnes, but consistent landfill tonnes. Recycling tonnes have continued to trend downward since 2020-21, however, another contributing factor is the introduction of Victoria’s container deposit scheme in November 2023.

Nillumbik waste audits show a third of landfill tonnes on average is food waste needlessly being put in the red bin. Using the green bin or home composting instead for food scraps is the simplest change that would have the greatest impact on minimising waste sent to landfill.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The minor decrease in this measure is driven by an overall decrease in expenditure for the 2024/2025 financial year relative to a minor increase in assessments. This has largely resulted from the gradual reduction in the provision for landfill rehabilitation and stability in the level of capital works and work-in-progress expensed. Other impacts include increased across spend in agency staff and contractors, offset by position vacancies. There have also been increases across amenity mowing, software maintenance, legal fees and insurance premiums. Leisure facility contract expenditure increased, but is counteracted by corresponding increase in contract income. These increases were not budgeted for and account for the variance between target versus actuals.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Of the 171 animals impounded from 1 July 2024 and 30 June 2025, 78 animals (45.61%) were successfully reclaimed by their owners. 31 out of the 171 animals impounded were surrendered by their owners(18.13%). As surrendered animals will not be reclaimed, this impacts the reclaim rate. We utilise the Home Together program though the Lost Dogs Home to prevent pet surrenders by connecting people in crisis to support. Officers will always make every effort to return lost pets to their owners to avoid impounding.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Of the 93 animals that were either surrendered or not reclaimed, 44 were successfully rehomed, representing a rehoming rate of 47.31%. Unfortunately, a number of surrendered animals are unable to be rehomed due to various factors, which impacts the overall rehoming rate. Excluding these cases, the rehoming outcomes remain strong. All available avenues are explored to ensure animals have the best chance of finding new homes. Additionally, the foster carer network plays a vital role in rehabilitating pets with health or behavioural concerns, further supporting positive rehoming outcomes.
