description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation: Despite ongoing efforts to reduce the number of decisions made in meetings closed to the public, the volume of such decisions has slightly increased over the past year. Examples of these efforts include development and implementation of a procurement specific template which encourages contract awards (where possible) to be considered in an open meeting of Council with the confidential information being included in the attachment to the report. Council officers also recently reviewed the process for designating information confidential, including decisions made at meetings closed to members of the public. Greater scrutiny is now applied to determine which information must be considered confidential. All confidentiality designations now require a reason explaining why the relevant legislation applies and consideration is given to when each matter can be made public.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation:
Satisfaction with community engagement has risen significantly, reaching its highest level on record and meeting LGV targets. This improvement reflects Council’s increased investment in community engagement planning and implementation over the past two years. Contributing factors include more rigorous reporting of engagement findings, regular community communications, and an unprecedented number of place-based engagement activities.

In 2025, our survey methodology changed from computer-assisted telephone interviewing (CATI) to face-to-face interviews, and our rating scale changed. These changes likely had a positive effect on the scores.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: All Councillors have the option to attend Council Meetings either in person or virtually. This contributes to a higher attendance rate at Council Meetings. There were two apologies received by Councillors during the 2024/25 financial year. Three Councillors were on approved leaves of absence for one meeting each during the 2024/25 financial year, these instances were marked as 'in attendance at meetings.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost per councillor has increased marginally this year, reflecting a combination of factors. Councillor allowances were adjusted in line with sector standards, recognising the responsibilities of the role. The new councillor cohort has actively engaged in training and development opportunities, supporting effective governance and informed decision-making. Additionally, inflationary pressures have contributed to higher costs for goods and services, resulting in a modest overall increase in expenditure.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation:
Community satisfaction with Council’s decision-making has increased by 19% this year, reflecting stronger public confidence in how Council engages with the community. However, satisfaction remains 4% below the sector average, indicating areas for continued improvement. This uplift is linked to several key changes. A new cohort of councillors has brought fresh perspectives and renewed energy, with a strong focus on transparency and responsiveness. Council also updated several governance policies, including the Councillor Gifts and Hospitality Policy, reinforcing ethical standards and public trust.
In 2025, our survey methodology changed from computer-assisted telephone interviewing (CATI) to face-to-face interviews, and our rating scale changed. These changes likely had a positive effect on the scores.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: We continue to invest strongly in our library resources with a shift from 55 per cent of our collection aged under five years in 2023/24, to 58 per cent in 2024/25. This trend is underpinned by ongoing purchasing of new titles, and a reduction in our physical collection aged over five years, which should result in continued improvement in this indicator toward ALIA national standards.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: The variation between 2023-24 and 2024/25 results is due to capital purchases unintentionally being omitted from the 2023-24 calculation. The actual cost to Council of the library service per population in 2023/24 was $51.16 which means there was a 5.5% increase in cost in 2024/25.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: Loans of our physical and digital collections continue to rise, up 3% from 2023/24. This figure shows a continued community engagement from our membership base utilising our lendable collections.

description: Library membership
variation: 9,594 new members joined the library service in 2024/25. Whilst this number is slightly down on 2023/24 (~1%), there was an increase in active library membership, indicating a larger number of our membership base were actively using our collections and technology services.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: There were 568,210 library visits in 2024/25, an increase of 50,734 visits or 9.8% compared to visitation in 2023/24. This steady rise in visitation is related to a significant increase in in-person library programs and events, and increased usage of in-person library services.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Enrolments in MCH remain consistent, and overall participation and engagement has slightly increased.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The total cost of this service is similar to the 2023/24 financial year. The decrease in the cost per hour to Council can be attributed to an increase in the total staff hours worked in the period.

description: Participation in the MCH service

Families who have missed a visit are followed up with a reminder and offered outreach visits in childcare centres if they are finding it hard to get to the maternal and child health centre.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children

variation: There has been a slight increase in the participation in the 4 week Key Ages and Stages in June 2025. There were fewer birth notices received in 2024-25 with more participating in visits compared to previous years.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: There has been a decrease in the number of sealed local road requests. Of the requests received many of them related to kerb damage caused by tree roots, pavement deformation resulting from aging underground assets of service authorities, and the condition of old bluestone laneways

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: As per the Road Management Plan, Council conducts visual condition inspections every three years for all roads within the municipality. The annual target was almost met, with the number of road sections below the intervention level expected to improve through ongoing work programs addressing segments of roads in poor condition. Council continues to invest in road condition improvements with a $9m allocation made in the new Council Plan.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Council experiences significant variance in the costs to undertake works based on the type of works and scope each year. The significant reduction in the average cost of road reconstruction for 2024-25 is attributed to the absence of bluestone works projects during this period compared to the previous year.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The rise in the average cost of sealed local road resurfacing is primarily due to increased contracting costs and cost of the supply of associated materials such as asphalt.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads was above target. This year, the community satisfaction survey was updated to allow residents to provide feedback on both major arterial roads and highways (managed by VicRoads) and sealed local roads (managed by Council). Including both types of roads in the survey helped provide context and encouraged respondents to consider them separately. This change likely contributed to the positive results, with satisfaction for the maintenance and repair of sealed local roads in Port Phillip rated at 72, close to the metropolitan average of 73. In 2025, our survey methodology changed from computer-assisted telephone interviewing (CATI) to face-to-face interviews which may have also had a positive effect on the scores.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: There has been a focus on the active management of planning applications over the last financial year to improve the efficiency of decision making and the overall customer experience. There has been a decrease in the median days it takes to decide a planning application.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Council has increased the number of decisions made within timeframe. This is attributed to the active management of planning applications with a focus on the efficient administration of application processes.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost of the administering the planning service has substantially reduced due to greater efficiency in the administration of application processes and by enabling staff to act as advocate at the Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The number of planning decisions upheld at VCAT in 2024/25 continues to exceed Council's target of 70 per cent and is above the average of most other municipalities. The percentage has increased since than last financial year which affirms that Council is making sound planning decisions.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Missed kerbside bin collections have decreased significantly compared to the previous financial year, particularly those resulting from contractor service delivery failures. This improvement reflects Council’s work with our kerbside collection contractor. Targeted in-field audits and the identification of repeat service issues have led to corrective actions, contributing to a positive trend. Figure represents community reported missed collection but as part of process Council validates contractor at fault reports as part of a continuous improvement program.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost of the kerbside garbage collection service has increased in 2024/25 due to two factors; higher disposal costs resulting from landfill levy increases, and an increase in collection contract rates.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The cost of recycling kerbside collection services has increased slightly; however, this increase has been kept below the Consumer Price Index (CPI) by offsetting rises in the collection contract with savings in disposal costs. these savings were achieved through collaboration with other councils to deliver significant efficiencies in the new recycling processing contract, which commenced in July 2024.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: There has been continued improvement in landfill diversion in 2024/25 as residents settle into the second year of the Food and Organics (FOGO) service. Increased familiarity with the service has been supported by targeted education initiatives, including flip-top bin audits across both recycling and FOGO streams. These audits provided direct feedback to individual properties, helping reinforce correct usage and improved participation rates. The set target of 40% was almost met. Council is currently reviewing its waste strategy, which aims to further promote diversion from landfill.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
Expenses per property assessment for 2024/25 show a favourable comparison to 2023/24, primarily due to the one-off disposal of land last year for development of community housing. Excluding this item, the ongoing cost base increased marginally by 1%, which is below the 2% inflation rate, reflecting effective cost containment and $1.5m of efficiency savings.  
Forecast expenditure is expected to rise, driven by cost shifting from State Government, including substantial increases in land tax, WorkCover premiums, congestion levy and landfill levy.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The 2024/25 ratio increased in line with the rates cap legislation.  This ratio is expected to increase subject to the forecast rates cap to be set by the Victorian Government.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation:
Council continues to meet its short-term financial obligations. The ratio has declined compared to the previous year, primarily due to reduced cash reserves following a record $103.9 million investment in community infrastructure during 2024/25, which included strategic land acquisitions for open space.
In line with Council’s endorsed long-term financial plan, substantial investment in community infrastructure will continue, necessitating further drawdowns on cash reserves.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Council’s total cash reserves are invested in accordance with our Investment and Treasury Policy, aiming to optimise investment returns while prioritising capital preservation, liquidity and corporate social responsibility. This indicator reflects a snapshot of short-term cash and investments relative to current liability as at 30 June 2025, and may fluctuate depending on the allocation between short-term and long-term investments.  Our prudent financial management ensures all current liabilities are settled promptly.  Over 62 percent of supplier invoices are paid within 14 days of receipt, in alignment with the Small Business Charter.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council have no current borrowings and no future borrowing plans.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council have no current borrowings and no future borrowing plans.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Consistent with prior year. This result is impacted by lease liabilities recognised in accordance with Australian Accounting Standards.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation:
Council spent $103.9 million on community assets during the 2024/25 financial year. $40 million were renewal and upgrade expenditure which represented 169% on depreciation. 
This ratio increased by 51% on 2023/24 due to record spend on works in 2024/25.
It is lower than the budgeted ratio of 196%, mainly due to deferred project spend in parks and open space. 
That said, the 2023/24 result indicates appropriate asset management as Council’s investment on renewing and upgrading our assets exceed depreciation.  This will increase in future years as we deliver our significant portfolio program.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
The improvement in the operating surplus for 2024/25 was primarily driven by increased user and statutory parking fees, stronger developer contributions, and the early receipt of $1.8 million in annual general-purpose funding from the Victoria Grants Commission.  
Our long-term financial plan projects continued positive surpluses in future financial years. Both surplus and depreciation remain vital funding sources to support the renewal, upgrade, and expansion of community infrastructure to meeting growing needs.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: Consistent with prior year we have revenue streams from multiple sources that Council can rely on to fund our services, with no over-reliance on rates. This will remain consistent in future years.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: This ratio is influenced by a number of factors including the annual valuation of properties in the municipality, growth in properties during the year and the rates cap. This result is consistent with prior years and long team forecast targets.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: This has reduced from prior year due to a reduction in expenditure mainly as a result of asset disposal costs in the prior year due to significant transfers for community housing.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: This has increased due to the completion of projects in 2024/25 resulting in record spend during the year. In addition the impact of accounting standard changes for the recognition of fair value costs has resulted in a $150 million valuation increase to Infrastructure asset values.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Council's estimated resident population increased by 5.5% in 2024/25, and the length of local roads remained consistent. This has resulted in an increase in the population density per length of road.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: This result has remained consistent with the prior year as the increase in revenue is matched by the increase in population.

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: There was a substantial increase in this indicator, primarily due to the early receipt of $1.8 million in annual general-purpose funding from the Victoria Grants Commission during 2024/25.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: The SEIFA relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage score is measured every five years. The score summarises a range of information about the economic and social conditions of people and households within the City. It indicates that there was an increase in disadvantage when compared to other local government areas in the most recent results. There are several pockets of disadvantage and vulnerable members of our community are experiencing mental health issues, substance abuse problems, childhood developmental difficulties, insecure housing and employment. Building a safer and active community with strong social connections is key to supporting the community we serve.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: There has been an increase in overall turnover for 2024-25. This is primarily attributed to the strategic exit of the majority of our Aged Care services in Quarter 2. Additionally, a slight rise in resignations during Quarter 4 reflects broader market trends, as the candidate landscape continues to stabilise following pandemic-related fluctuations. In addition, some frontline services remain affected by ongoing national labour challenges. Internally, we have continued to evolve our practices to strengthen our position as an employer of choice. Our focus remains on enhancing the employee experience for current team members, in alignment with our attraction and retention strategies.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: While Council owned facilities were not inspected within the reporting period, sampling was conducted in early 2025 to ensure that the seasonal pools that were in operation met chemical and microbiological parameters under the Public Health & Wellbeing Regulations 2019. Under the Public Health & Wellbeing Act 2008, Council may inspect aquatic facilities for compliance with the Public Health & Wellbeing Regulations 2019,and inspections are conducted within the pools registration period. Council is committed to ensuring that all aquatic facilities are inspected in the next reporting period.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation:
Excluding non-recurring items, the result for 2024–25 is $4,116, compared with $4,039 in 2023–24 on a consistent basis. The increase of approximately 2% in recurring expenses per property assessment reflects the inflationary pressures experienced during 2024–25.

The majority of Queenscliffe’s expenditure is non-discretionary, being necessary to deliver the core services expected of a local council. Given the Borough’s very small ratepayer base, this results in a higher expenditure per property assessment relative to other councils.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Rate revenue represents a high proportion of Queenscliffe’s revenue and spread across a very small ratepayer base.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation:
At the end of the 2024–25 financial year, current assets decreased as Council invested in term deposits with maturities greater than 12 months to secure favourable interest rates in the prevailing environment.

As at 30 June 2025, total cash, cash equivalents and other financial assets were $12.6 million, compared with $11.6 million at 30 June 2024.

It’s important to note that the impact of capital works are not included in forward forecasts, which makes the projected results appear higher than they will be in practice. Council’s long-term financial plan will be finalised in October 2025.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
In 2024–25, Council held $11.6 million in term deposits with original maturities exceeding 90 days, classified as restricted cash for LGPRF reporting purposes (compared with $9.8 million in 2023–24). This strategy was adopted to secure favourable interest rates in the prevailing economic climate.

Council anticipates converting a portion of these term deposits into operational cash to fund key projects, while continuing to maintain a strong operational cash balance over the next three years.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Council has a debt-free balance sheet.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council has a debt-free balance sheet.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The reduction in non-current liabilities in 2024–25 is attributable to a decrease in the provision for employee leave (non-current portion) as at year end.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation:
Some major capital works projects planned for 2024–25 were delayed due to changes in project scope and the time required to secure approvals. Because of this, spending on renewing and upgrading assets was slightly lower than expected, with $1.248 million invested compared to $1.282 million in depreciation.

To help fund future asset renewal, Council set aside a further $374,000 in the Asset Replacement Reserve during 2024–25.

It’s important to note that upgrade works are not included in forward forecasts, which makes the projected results appear lower than they will be in practice. Council’s long-term financial plan will be finalised in October 2025.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation:
The timing of Financial Assistance Grant (FAG) payments can make year-to-year results appear higher or lower than they really are. If these grants were counted in the year they were meant for, Council’s results would have been 2.0% in 2021–22, –4.3% in 2022–23, –2.4% in 2023–24, and 2.3% in 2024–25.

Looking only at ongoing income and expenses (including depreciation), and matching grant funding to the correct year, Council’s underlying result for 2024–25 would have been 3.1%, compared with 2.1% in 2023–24.

Budget and forward forecast results have been calculated by allocating FAG payments to the year to which they relate. On this basis, the average underlying result over the past four years is consistent with the projected average for the next four years.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Council’s score in the Community Satisfaction Survey declined by one point compared to the previous year, despite increased community consultation. Between late January and the end of February 2025, Council conducted an extensive engagement campaign to help shape the 2025–2029 Council Plan. This included 23 in-person events across 17 townships, resulting in 946 completed surveys and 1,241 suggestions for the Council Plan. Council conducts quarterly surveys, which may explain why the engagement score does not fully reflect the breadth of recent consultation activities.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: Councillor attendance was 89 per cent across five Council meetings in the previous term of Council. Councillor attendance at open and additional meetings since elected in November 2024 was high, with 96 per cent of attendance.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost of elected representation is higher this year as Council invested in a significant Councillor Transition and Induction Program to support the newly elected representatives in November 2024. The cost is also higher due to the initial set up and on-costs of Councillor equipment such as mobile phones and the Chief Executive Officer employment recruitment process conducted in 2025.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: Council's score of 45 for community decisions is one point lower than last year. It is comparable to the score for similarly sized large rural councils (46).

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: The small variation is due to the increase of costs per item.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: Capital expenses for 2024/25 were higher than previous year.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The increase in library membership can be contributed to the increase in loans and the expectation current patrons are borrowing more books.

description: Library membership
variation: New measure for 2023/24. The expected range is 20 to 40 per cent and the result was above Council's annual budget target of 35 per cent.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: New measure for 2023/24. The expected range is 2 to 6 library visits, and the result was within range.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation: Council continues to achieve 100 per cent or above for infant enrolments in the Maternal Child Health service. Highlighting a highly effective engagement with our children and families within the Shire.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: This result remains within the expected range. The slight increase compared to the 2023/24 financial year is due to unexpected staff shortages. Council is committed to providing high-quality Maternal and Child Health services.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: This result remains within the expected range. There was a slight increase compared to the 2023/24 financial year but the result is still above Council's annual budget target of 70 per cent.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Council continues to maintain strong engagement with First Nations families with young children. A 6.77 per cent increase in participation is attributed to an ongoing focus on cultural safety, awareness, and inclusive practices. Through its partnership with the Best Start initiative, Council is working to create culturally safe environments that acknowledge and respect Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultures. In addition, Council collaborates with the Aboriginal Network and various support agencies to strengthen referral pathways and further support engagement with First Nations families.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: Council recorded an attendance rate of 103 per cent for infants at the 4-week Key Ages and Stages Visit program, compared to 90 per cent in 2023/24. This indicator is above Council's annual budget target of 95 per cent.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The slight increase in overall requests is due to increased weather events which impacted local roads.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Council's funding levels for the renewal of sealed local roads has allowed Council to be consistently above the target standard for local sealed roads.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: The increase in sealed road construction is due to deep-lift pavement stabilisation in urban environments. Other projects in previous years have only required a shallow-lift pavement reconstruction.

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: The increase in the square metre rate of resealing is mainly associated with a change in the type of sealing treatment selected on some of Council's heavier trafficked roads, along with a general price rise in bitumen and quarry product.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Community satisfaction with sealed roads remains relatively low, despite asset condition data indicating that sealed roads in the Shire are generally in a good state of repair and within nominated service levels. The poor condition of some arterial roads in the Shire may also be contributing to lower community satisfaction. This is supported by a tailored question in the Community Satisfaction Survey that asked respondents to specify which sealed local roads they were most concerned about. The answers indicate that many of the sealed roads that are of greatest concern to residents are administered by the Department of Transport and Planning and not Council.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: As a result of allocating additional resources to address the backlog of legacy applications, coupled with a renewed focus on meeting statutory timelines, Council has achieved a significant reduction in the average time taken to determine planning applications. This improvement reflects enhanced internal efficiencies and a stronger commitment to service delivery.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: Council has achieved a significant improvement in the number of applications determined within required timeframes. Allocating additional resources reflects the improvement of internal efficiencies and a stronger commitment to service delivery.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost of delivery statutory services has decreased by nine per cent compared to the previous year, which is attributed to the allocation of additional resources to reduce planning application backlogs

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: Four of the four Planning Permits that were appealed to Victorian Civil Administrative Tribunal (VCAT) had decisions that were upheld (supported).

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: There have been fewer missed kerbside collection bins this year due to improved contractor performance.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost per property for delivering kerbside garbage collection services has increased due to higher waste disposal costs and the service being tendered with new rates applying.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: Recycling costs have reduced this year after the commencement of a new service contract.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Waste diversion from kerbside collection services was slightly lower than previous years. Due to the introduction of the Container Deposit Scheme reducing recycling tonnages collected and dry weather conditions have led to reduced kerbside green waste tonnages.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: The increase compared to target is primarily due to one-off unbudgeted expenses due to natural disasters and the disposal of infrastructure assets. Future forecasts are still valid.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Variation not greater or less than 10 per cent.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Result is higher than target due to increased investments in the year.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: The amount of cash held in cash and cash equivalents in 2022/23 was high as a result of receiving 100 per cent of the 2023/24 financial assistance grant prior to the end of the year. Cash held in cash and cash equivalents in 2023/24 and 2024/25 returned to historically normal levels.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: The increase in this measure compared to the previous year relates to new loan proceeds of $3.02M.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: The increase in 2023/24 related to a full year of repayments on loans for the Korumburra Community Hub and Korumburra streetscape, as the loan proceeds were received part way through 2023/23. This is consistent with 2024/25 levels.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Variation not greater or less than 10 per cent.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The increase in this measure compared to 2023/24 relates to higher renewal and upgrade capital works completed in 2024/25. The variation compared to the target is due to both higher depreciation and various capital works budgeted in 2024/25 that were not completed and expected to be completed in 2025/26.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The improvement in underlying result in 2024/25 relates to both a significant increase in revenue due to the timing of receipt of the Grants Commission Financial Assistance Grant funding, as well as a decrease in expenses due to the previous year including significant natural disaster costs relating to the February 2024 storm.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The decrease in this indicator is due to an increase in adjusted underlying revenue due to the timing of receipt of the Grants Commission Financial Assistance Grant funding.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: Variation not greater or less than 10 per cent.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation: The decrease in expenses per head in 2024/25 is primarily due to a slight decrease in expenses related to natural disasters compared to 2023/24.

description: Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: Asset unit rates have increased in value because of material and construction cost increases. Indexation and revaluations were performed across infrastructure asset classes as a result.

description: Population density per length of road
variation: Population growth was low for 2024/25.

description: Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
variation: Variation not greater than or less that 10 per cent

description: Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: The increase in this indicator is due to an increase in adjusted underlying revenue due to receiving 100 per cent of the 2024/25 and 50 per cent of the 2025/26 Financial Assistance Grants allocation, while receiving no Financial Assistance Grants in 2023/24 due to receiving 100 per cent of the allocation in 2022/23.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
variation: Variation not greater or less than 10 per cent.

description: Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Increased turnover is the result of 16 terminations due to genuine redundancy, dismissal, deeds of release and death.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: All Council operated aquatic facilities that were open during the 2024–2025 period were inspected at least once by an authorised Environmental Health Officer. One facility underwent a second health inspection in response to a complaint.

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: Increases faced in daily operating costs and renewal works associated with ageing outdoor facilities.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation: Staffing shortages throughout 2024-2025 resulted in delays, this has now been resolved and requests in 2025-2026 will be responded to in a timely manner.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: The increase in animals being reclaimed can be attributed to community members registering and microchipping their animals, ensuring they are able to be returned home when collected by Rangers.

description: Animals rehomed
variation: Council has increased its social media presence; using Facebook and Council’s website as a tool to rehome animals

description: Cost of animal management service per population

Live information available via Council's website on completed and scheduled works. In summary, these have contributed to the reduced number of requests received.

variation: Significant increase in material costs coupled with more accurate capture of fleet costs has resulted in price escalation.

variation: Increase in cost is primarily due to fees being waived for the Business Façade Improvement grant applications and applications lodged by community / non-for-profit groups, such as for sports facility upgrades.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: This improvement reflects the ongoing efforts of our waste services team and contractors to enhance service reliability and responsiveness across our region

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: This is in proportion with the increase to Council of the disposal to landfil fees, transport costs and a small increase in the total amount of waste transported from the Shire to landfil.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: This is due to Council installing a new bunker system at Hamilton transfer station enabling recycle waste streams to be be better seperated before being sent for processing, creating a saving to Council from reduced reliance on service providers. In addition and overall reduction in glass bottles and aluminium cans presented in the kerbside bins due to the introduction of the CDS scheme.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
Expenses per property assessment
variation: Council expenditure was above budget, most notably for depreciation which was $2.751m above budget and this increased the measure. The depreciation increase was due to increases in the asset values compared to when the budget was set.

description: Average rate per property assessment
Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: Council has strong liquidity with $1.55 of cash and other current assets for every $1 owed to creditors and other current liabilities.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: Unrestricted cash increased due to a prepayment of an untied grant late in June 2025 of over $5m. The grant related to funding for 2025/26 however is required to be recognised upon receipt of the funding.

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: Loan funds were drawn down during the year for the payment of capital projects.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Future years loan repayments are expected to increase in line with additional borrowings to help fund intergenerational projects.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: Loan funds were drawn down during the year for the payment of capital projects which has increased non-current liabilities.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: The capital works delivered was a total of $23.180m for the 2024-25 year compared to $18.143m in the prior year with 87% of the 2024-25 capital works being for renewal and upgrade. Works undertaken increased compared to the prior year and exceeded the 2024/25 budget however certain projects were in progress from 2023/24 that carried forward for completion in 2024/25.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The surplus result was an improvement compared to the prior year with an increase in operational grants in 2024/25 due to the early payment of the financial assistance grants.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The rates proportion of revenue reduced in comparison to the previous financial year due to the increase in total revenue caused by higher levels of grants received. Total rates revenue was consistent with the prior year with a small increase.

description: Rates compared to property values
Expenses per head of municipal population
Infrastructure per head of municipal population
variation: During the 2024/25 financial year a number of asset revaluations were undertaken resulting in the value of Council infrastructure increasing by $77 million.

description: Population density per length of road
Own-source revenue per head of municipal population
Recurrent grants per head of municipal population
variation: Recurrent grants increased compared to the previous year due to the early payment of financial assistance grant funding received in June 2025. In addition the 2023/24 finanical year recurrent grants was low due to the finanical assistance grant being paid early and therefore recognised in the previous financial year.

description: Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage
Percentage of staff turnover
variation: Staff turnover has decreased due to retention of permanent staff, through a renewed focus on organisational values.

description: Health inspections of aquatic facilities
variation: Council ensures aquatic facilities operate in accordance with the Public Health and Wellbeing Act 2008. Six inspections were undertaken across Council's three aquatic facilities this year (Harold Holt Swim Centre, Prahran Aquatic Centre and Prahran Square water feature).

description: Utilisation of aquatic facilities
variation:
This result is an improvement compared to 2023-24 which was impacted by a 3.5-month closure of Prahran Aquatic Centre for remedial works. There has been a return to strong member attendance at Prahran Aquatic Centre. 
Harold Holt Swim Centre attendance has been impacted by a decline in Learn to Swim enrolments. However, there has been an increase in hire bookings  largely through the Chinese Swim Association, which has lessened the overall attendance impact at Harold Holt Swim Centre.

description: Cost of aquatic facilities
variation: This result has increased and can be attributed to higher costs of pool maintenance supplies and reactive maintenance works.

description: Time taken to action animal management requests
variation:
Note: There was a change in IT processes in 2022–23 resulting in administration requests being included in the total request number. If calculated correctly, the figure for 2022–23 should be reported as 5.48 rather than 1.00.
There has been an increase in the time taken to action animal management requests this year, which can be attributed to staff vacancies.

description: Animals reclaimed
variation: Enhancements in registration promotions continued to support the reclaiming of animals this year.

description: Animals rehomed
variation:
There was a slight reduction in the number of unclaimed collected animals rehomed this year (2024-25: 18; 2023-24: 22). The number of unclaimed collected animals was generally consistent with last year (2024-25: 39; 2023-24: 38).
Animals suitable for rehoming have been rehomed, with remaining animals awaiting further behavioural training or vet care.

description: Cost of animal management service per population
variation: This result has slight increased this year in line with CPI and inflation.

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: All three animal management prosecutions heard during 2024-25 were successful.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation:
There has been a slight improvement in response times this year which will be further supported by planned technology upgrades enabling real-time data entry with the introduction of One Council.
Note: Food Safety measures are reported for the 2024 Calendar year in line with Local Government Performance Reporting Framework guidelines.

description: Food safety assessments
variation:
All food premises requiring an inspection were successfully inspected this year. Increased monitoring and reporting are ensuring that officers consistently achieve their KPIs.
Note: Food Safety measures are reported for the 2024 Calendar year in line with Local Government Performance Reporting Framework guidelines

description: Cost of food safety service
variation: This result has slightly increased and can be attributed to an increase in the number of food premises this year (1,407) compared to last year (1,344)

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications
variation:
All 239 annual inspections that resulted in major or critical non-compliance were reinspected for compliance. Officers also ensured actions to remove any immediate risks to public health were implemented at the time of the inspection.
Note: Food Safety measures are reported for the 2024 Calendar year in line with Local Government Performance Reporting Framework guidelines

description: Food safety samples
variation:
Council was required to obtain and submit 239 food samples for analysis in 2024, including 33 samples from Class 1 food premises and 206 samples from class 2, class 3A or class 3 food premises. Council exceeded this requirement, collecting a total of 280 food samples (33 samples from Class 1 food premises and 247 samples from Class 2 and Class 3 food premises).
Note: Food Safety measures are reported for the 2024 Calendar year in line with Local Government Performance Reporting Framework guidelines.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation:
Council strives to make decisions in open council wherever possible as part of its commitment to public transparency.
The percentage of confidential decisions is subject to large year on year variance due to the low numbers involved. The 2024/2025 result is in line with historical averages and within Council’s target range.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Target has been met as, according to LGPRF Guidelines, Council is within 2.5% variance below the target. This result is slightly unfavourable compared to 2024 and the metro result (both 56), and significantly favourable compared to the State-wide result (50). Forward targets within the FY26 budget anticipate consistent and minor improvements in result based on increased visibility and targeting of engagement work to reach a broader audience. Uplifts in Council’s reach, delivery and relevance of engagement activities, along with an increase in the range of available channels and methods of feedback, will contribute to the continuous improvement of our engagement performance over coming years.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation: As per the Local Government Better Practice Guide, Councillors are recorded as having attended a Council meeting if they are on an approved leave of absence. Given all leave requests have been granted this year, a result of 100% is to be expected

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The cost of elected representation was lower during this year due to holding of a general election, which resulted in a period of approximately three weeks which Councillor allowances were not payable

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: This result is unfavourable compared to 2024 (55), significantly unfavourable compared to the metro result (56) and significantly favourable compared to the State-wide result (49).

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: This result has improved and reflects a return to consistent supply chains following COVID-19 impacts during 2020 – 2022. The collection remains current following an active program of collection maintenance.

description: Cost of library service per population
variation: This result has slight increased this year in line with CPI and inflation.

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The re-opening of Toorak/South Yarra Library in September 2024 and the addition of new eCollection items has led to an increase in loans this year (2024-25: 658,255; 2023-24: 651,585). The slight decrease in the overall result can be attributed to an increase in population (2024-25: 114,038; 2023-24: 111,335).

description: Library membership
variation: This indicator considers current registered members who have recorded a transaction during the past three years. This year’s result therefore refers to 1 July 2022 to 30 June 2025, and is favourable compared to last year’s result (1 July 2021 to 30 June 2024) as last year’s result still included periods of service closure due to pandemic restrictions.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: This result has decreased and can be attributed to the closure of Toorak/South Yarra Library until September 2024, and the closure of Study@Greville in April 2025. Library visits were steady following the re-opening of Toorak/South Yarra Library. Malvern Library welcomed more that 45% of all library visitors this year.

description: Infant enrolments in the MCH service
variation:
All families of children born during this time have completed a home visit. This result is consistent with previous performance and demonstrates the value placed in our Maternal and Child Health service.
Note: Results over 100% occur where babies were born late in the previous reporting year, but enrolment occurred early in the new reporting year; or where the birth notice was received in a different Council, but enrolment occurred within Stonnington.

description: Cost of the MCH service
variation: The cost of Maternal and Child Health service has slightly increased and can be attributed to higher number of hours worked by MCH nurses.

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation:
This result is consistent with 2023-24. There were 3,220 children enrolled in the MCH service, of which 2,557 attended at least once in the year.
Note: Any 0.5 increment of enrolment or attendance occurs when a child moves in or out of Council part way through the year.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation:
Participation rate reflects high attendance by Aboriginal children.
There were 18.5 Aboriginal children enrolled in the MCH service, of which 15 attended at least once in the year. Participation by Aboriginal children fluctuates due to the small number of registered Aboriginal children in Stonnington. 
Note: Any 0.5 increment of enrolment or attendance occurs when a child moves in or out of Council part way through the year.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: This result is consistent with previous years and demonstrates strong participation. Strong participation is an indication of how our community values our Maternal and Child Health service.

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: The result has increased due to the new rules for taking customer requests from all sources and inputting them into the Customer Request system, as well as a number of requests for pit lids being picked up as road-related requests.

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation: Result is consistent with 2023-24 and is within range of the target (99.78%)

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: This result has slightly increased and can be attributed to multiple factors including CPI and inflation, and increased material and labour costs

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: In FY2023–24, there was a higher cost of sealed local road resealing due to a focus on resurfacing local roads, which involves more complex and resource-intensive works. In FY2024–25, a greater proportion of works focused on laneways, which are less costly due to reduced traffic management and fewer service authority requirements. As a result, the cost of road resealing decreased in FY2024–25

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: This result is significantly unfavourable compared to 2024 (64), unfavourable compared to the metro result (59) and significantly favourable compared to the State-wide result (45).

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: There has been a slight improvement in application processing time compared to 2023-24. This result is compliant with the State Government’s 60-day statutory requirement.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: This result is consistent with 2023-24. There were 1,003 applications decided within required timeframes this year compared to 1,102 applications in 2023-24. It should be noted that not all applications can be decided within the 60-day Statutory timeframe as complex applications require more time to complete negotiations and assessments.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The cost of the statutory planning service has slightly increased this year and can be attributed to a reduction in number of applications received compared to last year (2024-25 - 1,273; 2023-24 – 1,384).

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The number of VCAT appeals this year (59) was generally consistent with 2023-24 (63), reflecting stability in decision-making outcomes. The number of Council decisions supported by VCAT also remained stable this year (52) compared to 2023-24 (48). These results may vary in future years as Council continues to make decisions in the best interests of the community.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: There was an increase in kerbside collection bins missed this year. This is attributed to protected industrial action undertaken during December 2024 and is not reflective of broader service issues.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost of the kerbside garbage bin collection service has increased in line with increases in CPI, fuel indexation, landfill gate disposal costs and other associated landfill expenses.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The overall cost of kerbside recyclables bin collection and processing has slightly decreased. This can be attributed to fewer tonnes of recyclable material processed for 2024-25 compared to 2023-24.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill is consistent with 2023-24, however, is below the set target of 45.00%. This target was set in line with Council’s Towards a Circular Economy: Our Future Waste Strategy 2022–2025 and assumed the roll out of a universal Food Organic Garden Organic (FOGO) service during 2024-25, which was delayed to 2025-26. It is anticipated that an increase in diversion in line with the target will be observed post implementation. Note that there was a 4.1% reduction in recycling tonnes processed this year compared to 2023-24, accounting for the slightly reduced overall diversion result.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: Council is delivering quality services in accordance with its long-term plan. The marginal increase in this indicator compared to 2023-24 is primarily driven by increased depreciation and amortisation costs from the capital and Digital Transformation programs, partially offset by reduction in SaaS expenditure. This has resulted in a 1.38% increase in annual operating expenditure, from $219.52 million in 2023-24 to $222.55 million in 2024-25.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: Council continues to have one of the lowest average residential rates and charges across metropolitan Melbourne. Council’s actual rate increase was capped at 2.75 per cent for 2024-25 together with an increased number of property assessments.

description: Current assets compared to current liabilities
variation: The Council can comfortably meet all short-term financial commitments. The increased working capital ratio in 2024-25 compared to prior year is due to $41.00 million of borrowings that were classified as current in 2023-24 were re-financed in 2024-25 and are now shown as non-current liabilities. Working capital is expected to range between 124-157% over the next four financial years.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation:
Unrestricted cash is defined as cash (excluding term deposits greater than 90 days) less Council’s: public open space reserve, trust funds and deposit, conditional grants unspent, and funds for capital carryovers. While the Council maintains adequate cash levels to support day-to-day operations and deliver community infrastructure, Council does not have sufficient cash levels to fully cover restricted obligations at the same time. 
Note that the prior year comparatives did not exclude the Open Space Reserve.  To restate this, the prior year numbers for unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities are:
FY22   -158.40%
FY23   -123.01%
FY24     -69.00%

description: Loans and borrowings compared to rates
variation: During the financial year, Council borrowed $1.27 million of new funds, and paid back $3.64 million - a net reduction of $2.36 million. It is consistent with Council's long term financial plan and pay-back strategy.

description: Loans and borrowings repayments compared to rates
variation: Council made principal loan repayments of $3.64 million in 2024-25. New loans and associated repayments are forecast in the next 4 years to assist in funding major community infrastructure. This is consistent with Council's long term financial plan.

description: Non-current liabilities compared to own source revenue
variation: The increase in the 2024-25 actual is due to $41.00 million of loans in current liabilities, which Council re-borrowed during the year. The trend reflects Council's medium-term borrowings strategy to fund the capital works program over the next four years. Additional loan borrowings of $7.0 million for 2025-26, $8.0 million for 2026-27,  $4.5 million for 2027-28 are forecast to assist in funding community infrastructure.

description: Asset renewal and upgrade compared to depreciation
variation: This metric is above the expected range, and illustrates Council’s focus on ensuring a sustainable level of funding is provided for the renewal of community infrastructure. The metric is below target assumptions due to higher depreciation driven by revaluations and capitalisation at the end of the previous year. Council has a robust capital program that ensures appropriate levels of renewals and upgrades are maintained. Council has allocated sufficient funding to the capital works program to meet its renewal and upgrade requirements in future years.

description: Adjusted underlying surplus (or deficit)
variation: The adjusted underlying result has been impacted in recent years by increased Software-as-a-Service costs that cannot be capitalised.  In 2024-25 this resulted in $7.10 million of Digital Transformation costs expensed. In addition, inflationary impacts and depreciation have also added to higher expenditure, refer to ‘expenses per property assessment’ for further details.

description: Rates compared to adjusted underlying revenue
variation: The 2024-25 result is relatively consistent with the 2023-24 result. Rate revenue will continue to be a key source of funding for the delivery of high quality services and infrastructure to the community.

description: Rates compared to property values
variation: This result illustrates the relatively low rating burden for the Stonnington community, and Council continues to have one of the lowest average residential rates and charges across metropolitan Melbourne.

description: Expenses per head of municipal population
variation:
Council continues to manage a relatively low level of expenditure per head of population. This is through the efficient and effective delivery of high-quality services to the community within a responsible budget. The slight decrease in this indicator compared with 2023-24 was a result of an increase in expenditure (refer to further details in ‘Expenses per property assessment’ metric) offset by an increase in population.

2022: 114,340 (2021 ERP) - revised post submission to 106,190

description: Critical and major non-compliance outcome notifications

Following up non-compliances is a high priority for officers, to ensure that the risk to public health is adequately controlled.

description: Cost of the MCH service

description: Participation in the MCH service
variation: Strategies to increase engagement rates for key age groups (1.5 years to 3.5 years) have been successful in 2024-25.  Overall engagement is steady.  Engagement figures can be impacted by movement of families in and out of the Shire, where families elect not to engage with MCH services as their children grow older.

description: Participation in the MCH service by Aboriginal children
variation: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander family participation increased. Families are offered to engage with Surf Coast Shire MCH service, the local Aboriginal Community Controlled Organisation, or both.

description: Participation in 4-week Key Age and Stage visit
variation: All families are offered Key Age and Stage (KAS) consultations from birth.  Engagement in the 4 week KAS consultation can be affected by extended hospital stays after birth, or family choice to delay engagement whilst a private midwife is still providing postnatal care (usually until approximately 6 weeks of age).

description: Sealed local road requests
variation: Compared to the previous period, Surf Coast Shire Council has seen a decrease in the number of sealed road requests. (SEE COMMENT)

description: Sealed local roads maintained to condition standards
variation:
Surf Coast Shire Council engages an external specialised road auditor every three years to undertake a whole network assessment, utilising a condition assessment procedure that is based on industry guidelines to ensure a repeatable process so that previous data is able to be compared. The last audit was undertaken in 2022-23 with the next assessment due to occur in 2025-26. 

The condition data from the asset database, along with updates based on completed capital works, are inputs into the strategic asset modelling undertaken annually on Council’s asset base. The outputs from the annual modelling are the framework of the renewal program that is developed and delivered each year as part of the wider capital works program.

description: Cost of sealed local road reconstruction
variation: Surf Coast Shire Council has reported a 12.53% reduction in the cost of sealed local road reconstruction in 2024-25 compared to the prior year. Council rehabilitated semi-rural roads during 2024-25, which is more cost effective than rehabilitation of major urban roads in 2023-24

description: Cost of sealed local road resealing
variation: Surf Coast Shire has seen a significant reduction of 83% in the cost of local road resealing in 2024-25. This is due to the 2024-25 resealing program focusing entirely on large-scale rural roads using spray seal. In contrast, the 2023-24 reseal program involved asphalting urban roads, which has a much higher cost per square metre.

description: Satisfaction with sealed local roads
variation: Satisfaction with sealed local roads has slightly decreased from 57 to 52 in the latest Community Satisfaction Survey. While this represents a small decline, the result remains above the state average of 45 and the average for large rural councils of 39. This indicates that, despite the drop, our community continues to rate the condition of local sealed roads more positively than many comparable areas.

description: Time taken to decide planning applications
variation: The 21% reduction in average decision times from 80 to 63 days reflects the combined impact of targeted backlog clearance, process streamlining, and a recent shift in application types. Current market conditions, including increased construction costs, have led to a majority of simpler, less ambitious proposals that better comply with the planning scheme, enabling faster assessments. However, some applications still require extended timeframes, and this overall trend may shift as market conditions evolve.

description: Planning applications decided within required time frames
variation: The percentage of planning applications determined within required timeframes increased from 68.7% to 74.8%, bringing us closer to our 75% target. This improvement reflects the combined effects of backlog reduction, streamlined processing procedures, and a shift toward simpler application types—factors that have supported more efficient assessment and decision-making and faster turnaround times overall.

description: Cost of statutory planning service
variation: The increase in net service costs reflects general rises in operating expenses alongside a decrease in the number of applications received. Although fees per application have also increased, the overall reduction in application volume has resulted in decreased fee revenue, contributing to the higher net cost to Council.

description: Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT
variation: The percentage of Council planning decisions upheld at VCAT decreased slightly from 95% to 91.7%. However, this corresponds to just one appeal not upheld each year—11 appeals were determined this year compared to 20 last year. Despite fewer appeals overall, the consistent number of decisions not upheld highlights the strong and steady performance of Council’s planning decisions at VCAT.

description: Kerbside collection bins missed
variation: Council’s contractor has rolled out improved technology in their fleet of collection vehicles which supports better results by providing drivers with more accurate detailed information about routes and collection points. Missed bins is a KPI that is proactively monitored monthly during contract management meetings.

description: Cost of kerbside garbage bin collection service
variation: The cost of kerbside waste collections remains steady with a slight increase of 3.32% compared to the previous financial year. Surf coast Shire Council remains committed to delivering reliable waste collection services for our community.

description: Cost of kerbside recyclables collection service
variation: The cost of recycling collections has remained steady, with an increase of 1.55% compared to the previous financial year. This cost increase reflects rate adjustments within Council’s collection contracts due to CPI and fuel prices. Council remains committed to delivering reliable and sustainable recycling services for our community.

description: Kerbside collection waste diverted from landfill
variation: Recyclables collected via the kerbside waste system have reduced due to the introduction of the Victorian Container Deposit Scheme. Reduced Food Organics and Garden Organics are due to drier climate for 2024-25.

description: Expenses per property assessment
variation: During the 2024 financial year Surf Coast Shire transferred assets totalling $24.25 million to Great Ocean Road Coast and Parks Authority under the Great Ocean Road and Environs Protection Act 2020. As this transfer is recognised as an expense it increased the expenses per property assessment compared to the target budget. In 2027 the Surf Coast Aquatic and Health Centre will be operational increasing depreciation and operating expenses. The spike in the 2028 year is in relation to higher amortisation expense in relation to the Anglesea Landfill airspace.

description: Average rate per property assessment
variation: The forecast increases in the average rate per property assessment are in relation to the budgeted rate cap and supplementary rates in relation to new buildings.

The 2026 ratio does not have the effects of unearned revenue as the prior years do. The decrease in the 2027 ratio is due to capital works expenditure being funded from reserves. The decrease in the 2029 ratio is in relation  to the transfer of provisions for the landfill rehabilitation in preparation for rehabilitation works in 2030.

description: Unrestricted cash compared to current liabilities
variation: At 30 June 2025 Councils creditors are $8 million higher than 2024 due to amounts owed but not yet paid for the construction of the Surf Coast Aquatic and Health Centre . This ratio excludes other financial assets - term deposits with maturities from 3 months to 12 months, if these were included the ratio would be mostly positive. Council manages its cash flow to ensure sufficient cash to pay liabilities when due.

description: Cost of animal management service per population

description: Animal management prosecutions
variation: During the 2024/25 financial year, 15 successful animal management prosecutions were finalised. These prosecutions addressed serious dog attacks, unregistered breeders, and animal cruelty matters.

description: Time taken to action food complaints
variation:
In 2024, Council received 14 food safety related complaints, four fewer than in 2023.  
To reduce risks to the community, Council prioritises prompt responses to food safety concerns. This commitment is reflected in the 2024 results, with all food safety related complaints being responded to within one day.

description: Council decisions made at meetings closed to the public
variation:
Council made a total of 224 resolutions during the 2024–25 financial year, with 19 decisions (8.48%) made in meetings closed to the public. Decisions relating to leave requests from Councillors are considered in closed sessions due to the sensitive nature of these matters. Of the 19 closed session decisions, 9 specifically related to Councillor leave.
All meetings open to the public are broadcast live via Council’s website and archived online for future access. Council remains committed to transparency and accountability and actively seeks to minimise the number of decisions made in closed sessions.

description: Satisfaction with community consultation and engagement
variation: Councils' participation in the 2025 Local Government Community Satisfaction Survey resulted in a community satisfaction rating that was four points above the large rural average and two points higher than the state-wide average. This reflects positively on Council’s performance in community consultation and engagement processes.

description: Councillor attendance at council meetings
variation:
Councillors demonstrated a strong commitment to serving the community, reflected in a 100% attendance rate across 22 ordinary Council meetings and one unscheduled meeting during the 2024/25 financial year.
The continued use of a hybrid meeting model combining in-person and virtual attendance supports a high level of participation.

description: Cost of elected representation
variation: The 2024/25 result is slightly lower than the previous year. This decrease is attributed to the new prescribed training requirements, which have led to most Councillor training being delivered in group settings, reducing the cost of individual sessions.

description: Satisfaction with council decisions
variation: The satisfaction score for Council decision making as measured by the 2025 Local Government Community Satisfaction Survey resulted in a two-point increase compared to last year. Council notes that the large rural average and state-wide average resulted in a decline of two points. Wellington's results are 9 points higher than the large rural average and 6 points higher than the state-wide average.

description: Recently purchased library collection
variation: This year, we’ve seen a significant uplift in our collection standards, driven by more effective collection management. This progress is enabling us to build a contemporary collection that remains current and relevant to the needs of our community.

description: Cost of library service per population

description: Loans per head of population
variation: The number of library loans has remained steady compared to last year, supported by the continued expansion of traditional lending to include e-resources, technology, and online services throughout the 2024/25 financial year.

description: Library membership
variation: Library memberships increased in the 2024/25 financial year, reflecting the continued popularity and relevance of our services to the community.

description: Library visits per head of population
variation: Library visitation continues to be very strong indicating that library spaces and services are being well utilised. Although the overall 2024/25  visitation is lower than previous years this can attributed to Stratford Library being closed for renovations for over 2 months.
